- The step angle depends on the number of rotor teeth and the number of phases:

$$
\beta = \frac{360°}{N_r \cdot m}
$$

- VR steppers can achieve small step angles (down to 5°–15°) by using multi-tooth structures.

**Comparison with PM Stepper (1 mark):**

| Feature | VR Stepper | PM Stepper |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| Rotor | Soft iron, no magnet | Permanently magnetised |
| Holding torque at zero current | **Zero** (rotor is free) | **Non-zero** (detent torque) |
| Step size | Typically larger (5°–15°) | Typically 7.5°–90° |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Applications | Light duty, low holding torque | Where holding torque is critical |

---

**(b) Hydraulic Cylinder Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m, $d = 45$ mm $= 0.045$ m, $Q = 25$ L/min $= 25 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 4.167 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, $P = 100$ bar $= 100 \times 10^5$ Pa $= 1 \times 10^7$ Pa.

**Step 1: Cap-end area (extension stroke):**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Rod-end area (retraction stroke):**

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08^2 - 0.045^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0064 - 0.002025)}{4} = 3.435 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**(i) Extension force and velocity (2 marks):**

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_1 = 1 \times 10^7 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}265 \text{ N} \approx 50.27 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0829 \text{ m/s} \approx 82.9 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**(ii) Retraction force and velocity (2 marks):**

$$
F_{retr} = P \cdot A_2 = 1 \times 10^7 \times 3.435 \times 10^{-3} = 34{,}350 \text{ N} \approx 34.35 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
v_{retr} = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{3.435 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1213 \text{ m/s} \approx 121.3 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**(iii) Hydraulic power input and mechanical power output during extension (2 marks):**

$$
P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = 1 \times 10^7 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-4} = 4166.7 \text{ W} \approx 4.17 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
P_{mech,ext} = F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext} = 50{,}265 \times 0.0829 = 4166.6 \text{ W} \approx 4.17 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Overall efficiency (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{mech}}{P_{hyd}} = \frac{4166.6}{4166.7} \approx 0.99998 \approx 100\% \text{ (ideal case, no losses)}
$$

In practice, with friction, leakage, and pressure drops, $\eta \approx 85$–$95\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Hydraulic Problems):**
> - **Forgetting the area ratio** for retraction: students often use $A_1$ for both directions. The correct retraction area is the **annular** area $A_2 = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$.
> - **Unit conversions** are the #1 source of errors: 1 bar $= 10^5$ Pa, 1 L/min $= 10^{-3}/60$ m³/s. Always state these conversions explicitly.
> - **In ideal (lossless) problems**, efficiency may come out to exactly 100% — do not panic; this is mathematically correct when no losses are specified.
> - **For load-bearing problems**, subtract friction force $F_f$ from the theoretical force: $F_{net} = P \cdot A - F_f$.

---

### 5.3 Additional Practice Question (Bonus)

> [!NOTE]
> The following question may appear as **Part B, 14-mark** with modifications. Practice it as a self-test.

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Practice]**

A robot joint is driven by a stepper motor with 100 rotor teeth and 2 phases. The motor is required to rotate the joint by 90° in 1 second with uniform motion.

Calculate:
(a) Step angle
(b) Number of pulses required for 90° rotation
(c) Pulse frequency
(d) Holding torque if the phase current is 2 A and $\frac{dL}{d\theta} = 0.05$ H/rad
(e) Torque-speed characteristic interpretation — what is the maximum safe stepping frequency if the pull-out torque drops to zero at 1500 Hz?

*(Answers: (a) 1.8°/step; (b) 50 pulses; (c) 50 Hz; (d) 0.1 N·m; (e) 1500 Hz.)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Read This Before the Exam.**

**Drive Systems in Robotics — Master Concepts:**

- [x] A drive system converts stored energy into mechanical motion at the robot joints.
- [x] Three principal classes: **Electrical (DC/AC Servo, Stepper), Hydraulic, Pneumatic**.

**DC Servo Motor — Key Points:**

- [x] Back-EMF: $E_b = K_e \omega$
- [x] Torque: $T = K_t I_a$ (with $K_t = K_e$ in SI units)
- [x] Transfer function: $\theta(s)/V_a(s) = K_m / [s(\tau_m s + 1)]$ (neglecting $L_a$)
- [x] Linear torque-speed curve; high torque at low speed makes it ideal for robotic joints.
- [x] Requires encoder/tachogenerator for closed-loop control — open-loop position is unstable.

**AC Servo Motor — Key Points:**

- [x] Two-phase induction type with reference and control windings, 90° apart in space.
- [x] High rotor resistance → linear torque-speed curve → no pull-out instability.
- [x] Modern industrial robots use **BLDC** or **PMSM** under "AC Servo" umbrella.
- [x] Controlled by varying amplitude and phase of control voltage.
- [x] Torque: $T \approx K \cdot V_R \cdot V_C \cdot \sin(\theta_m)$

**Stepper Motor — Key Points:**

- [x] Step angle: $\beta = 360° / (N_r \cdot m)$
- [x] Open-loop control — no encoder needed for simple positioning.
- [x] Three types: **VR, PM, Hybrid** — Hybrid has the best performance.
- [x] Three torque-speed regions: **Pull-in, Pull-out, Slew**.
- [x] **Resonance and missed steps** are common failures at high speeds.
- [x] Microstepping (e.g., 1/256) enables sub-degree resolution.

**Pneumatic Actuators — Key Points:**

- [x] Compressed air (6–8 bar) → linear or rotary motion.
- [x] Force: $F_p = P \cdot A \cdot \eta_m$
- [x] Velocity: $v = Q / A$
- [x] **Compressibility** of air → "springy" response; needs closed-loop for precision.
- [x] **Clean, safe, low-cost** — ideal for food, pharma, explosive environments.
- [x] Maximum pressure of 10 bar limits force density.

**Hydraulic Actuators — Key Points:**

- [x] **Incompressible** oil → rigid, precise response.
- [x] Force: $F_h = P \cdot A$; Retraction force uses annular area.
- [x] Power: $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$
- [x] **10–20× higher force density** than pneumatics.
- [x] Requires reservoir, pump, filters, relief valve → high cost and complexity.
- [x] **Leaks are a major concern** in hydraulic robots.

**Selection Heuristics for KTU Problems:**

- [x] **Light load + clean room + simple motion** → Pneumatic
- [x] **Heavy load + high precision + rigid** → Hydraulic
- [x] **Medium load + continuous smooth motion + variable speed** → AC Servo
- [x] **Light/medium load + discrete steps + open-loop** → Stepper
- [x] **Educational / low-cost** → DC Servo

**Most Frequently Asked Equations in KTU ESE:**

- [x] $E_b = K_e \omega$ and $T = K_t I_a$
- [x] $\beta = 360°/(N_r \cdot m)$
- [x] $F = P \cdot A$ (both pneumatic and hydraulic)
- [x] $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$
- [x] $v = Q/A$
- [x] Pascal's law: $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$

**Examiner's Final Tip:**

- [x] Always draw a **neat block diagram or schematic** — it carries 1–2 marks free.
- [x] Always **state units** with every numerical answer.
- [x] For derivations, **show every step** — partial credit depends on it.
- [x] In comparison questions, use a **table** for clarity.
- [x] Justify drive selection using **payload, precision, environment, cost** — all four whenever possible.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — DRIVE SYSTEMS & ACTUATORS**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force and Torque Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Force and Torque Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Force and Torque Sensors (F/T Sensors)** are transducer devices mounted on a robotic manipulator — typically at the **wrist**, **end-effector**, or within **joint assemblies** — that convert mechanical quantities (force $\vec{F}$ and torque $\vec{\tau}$) acting on the robot into measurable electrical signals. These sensors provide the **haptic feedback** required for compliant motion control, assembly, deburring, polishing, and human-robot collaborative (cobot) tasks.

Mathematically, a 6-axis F/T sensor measures the **wrench vector** $W \in \mathbb{R}^6$:

$$
W = \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \\ \tau_x \\ \tau_y \\ \tau_z \end{bmatrix}
$$

Where the first three components are **Cartesian forces** along the principal axes and the last three are **moment components** about the same axes.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Hand:**
> Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to pick up a raw egg from a table. Without seeing, you rely on the **sensation of touch** in your fingertips. The pressure receptors in your skin detect *how much* force you are applying, *which direction* the egg is rolling, and *how twisty* the shell feels. If you press too hard, you crush it; too soft, and you drop it.
>
> A **Force/Torque sensor is the "skin" of a robot**. It gives the otherwise "blind" metallic arm the sense of touch, allowing it to:
> - Feel contact with an object
> - Detect the weight of a payload
> - Sense resistance during insertion (e.g., a peg in a hole)
> - Avoid crushing delicate parts

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard physical quantities govern F/T sensor behavior:

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gauge Factor | $GF$ | dimensionless | $\approx 2.0$ (constantan) |
| Young's Modulus (Steel) | $E$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ | $\approx 200 \times 10^9$ |
| Young's Modulus (Aluminum) | $E$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ | $\approx 69 \times 10^9$ |
| Poisson's Ratio (Steel) | $\nu$ | dimensionless | $\approx 0.30$ |
| Piezoelectric Constant (Quartz) | $d_{33}$ | $\text{C/N}$ | $\approx 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ |
| Gravitational Acceleration | $g$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | $9.81$ |

## 1.4 Types of Force/Torque Sensors — Classification Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification:** The two principal sensor categories are:
>
> 1. **Internal Sensors (Proprioceptive):** Joint torque sensors embedded *within* the actuator — measure joint forces directly.
> 2. **External Sensors (Exteroceptive):** Wrist-mounted sensors between the *last joint* and the *end-effector* — measure the *Cartesian wrench* at the tool point.

Based on **operating principle**, the technology stack is:

- **Strain Gauge Based** (most common in industrial robots)
- **Piezoelectric Based** (high dynamic range, no DC response)
- **Capacitive Based** (high sensitivity, MEMS-compatible)
- **Optical Based** (immune to EMI, used in MRI environments)
- **Force Sensing Resistors (FSR)** (low-cost tactile skin)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Strain Gauge (Deep Dive)

A **strain gauge** is the fundamental sensing element inside most F/T sensors. It operates on the **piezoresistive effect** — the change in electrical resistance of a conductor when subjected to mechanical strain.

### Operational Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1: Mechanical Input:** An external force $F$ is applied to the sensor body (often an elastic element called a *flexure*).
- **Step 2: Strain Generation:** The flexure deforms infinitesimally, producing a strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ along its sensitive axis.
- **Step 3: Resistance Change:** The thin metallic foil grid of the strain gauge stretches or compresses, altering its length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$. This produces a fractional change in resistance:

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} + \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A}
$$

- **Step 4: Electrical Output:** This tiny resistance change is converted to a measurable voltage using a **Wheatstone bridge** circuit.
- **Step 5: Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering, and ADC conversion produce a digital force/torque reading.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Gauge Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **The Gauge Factor (GF)** is the *sensitivity constant* of a strain gauge. It bundles the **geometric effect** (shape deformation) and the **piezoresistive effect** (material property) into a single dimensionless multiplier.

$$
GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}
$$

- For metallic gauges (e.g., constantan), the piezoresistive term is negligible, so $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 1.6$ to $2.1$.
- For **semiconductor gauges** (silicon), the piezoresistive term dominates, giving $GF \approx 100$ to $200$ — but with greater temperature sensitivity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD** — The following table is the *complete* formula inventory required for any F/T sensor problem in the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Mechanical strain (longitudinal) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma = F / A$ | Engineering stress | $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$ |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (linear elastic region) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 4 | $F = k \cdot x$ | Spring force (linear deflection) | $\text{N}$ |
| 5 | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Piezoresistive relation | dimensionless |
| 6 | $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge factor definition | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_o = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$ | Wheatstone bridge output voltage | $\text{V}$ |
| 8 | $V_o \approx V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$ (for single active gauge) | Bridge output for $1/4$ bridge | $\text{V}$ |
| 9 | $\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | Torque from force and moment arm | $\text{N·m}$ |
| 10 | $\tau = J \cdot \alpha$ | Rotational Newton's 2nd law | $\text{N·m}$ |
| 11 | $P = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}$ | Mechanical power (force $\times$ velocity) | $\text{W}$ |
| 12 | $P = \vec{\tau} \cdot \vec{\omega}$ | Mechanical power (torque $\times$ angular vel.) | $\text{W}$ |
| 13 | $C = \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial F}$ | Mechanical compliance | $\text{m/N}$ |
| 14 | $K = \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial x}$ | Mechanical stiffness | $\text{N/m}$ |
| 15 | $C = 1/K$ | Compliance–stiffness duality | — |
| 16 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{n \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{(1+n)^2} \cdot 4$ (for $n$ active gauges) | Generalized bridge equation | $\text{V}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These equations form the backbone of:
> - **Robotic assembly** (peg-in-hole insertion with force feedback)
> - **Surgical robots** (Da Vinci system uses F/T feedback for suturing)
> - **Collaborative robots** (Cobots like UR5 stop on contact)
> - **Force-controlled polishing/grinding** of turbine blades
> - **Haptic teleoperation** in space and undersea manipulators

## 2.4 Piezoelectric vs. Piezoresistive — Critical Comparison

| Property | Piezoresistive (Strain Gauge) | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Resistance change under strain | Charge generation under strain |
| **DC Response** | ✅ Yes (static force measurable) | ❌ No (only dynamic) |
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate ($GF \approx 2$) | Very High |
| **Frequency Range** | DC to $\sim 1$ kHz | $0.1$ Hz to $\sim 100$ kHz |
| **Temperature Drift** | Moderate | Low |
| **Typical Use** | Industrial robots, load cells | Vibration analysis, dynamic impacts |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** A common exam pitfall is assuming piezoelectric sensors can measure *static* weight. **They cannot** — the generated charge leaks away, so the signal decays to zero. Use piezoresistive gauges for steady-state force measurement.

## 2.5 Force/Torque Sensor Mounting Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Sensor Placement:**

1. **Wrist Sensor:** Between the last robot joint and the end-effector (tool). Measures the *Cartesian wrench* exerted on the tool. Common in industrial arms (e.g., ATI Mini45, JR3).
2. **Joint Torque Sensor:** Inside each actuator. Enables *whole-body compliance* — used in **DLR lightweight robots** and **KUKA LWR**.
3. **Base Sensor:** Mounted on the robot pedestal. Detects collision forces from the *environment* on the *whole structure*.
4. **Tactile Skin:** Distributed sensor arrays on the *surface* of the robot. Used in prosthetics and cobots.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resistance Change in a Strain Gauge

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the relationship $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ from first principles.

**Starting Point — Resistance of a Uniform Conductor:**

$$
R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}
$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = resistivity of the conductor material $[\Omega \cdot \text{m}]$
- $L$ = length of the gauge $[\text{m}]$
- $A$ = cross-sectional area $[\text{m}^2]$

**Step 1 — Take the Total Differential:**

$$
\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{dL}{L} - \dfrac{dA}{A}
$$

> **Logic:** The relative change in resistance equals the sum of relative changes — the resistivity term *adds*, the length term *adds*, but the area term *subtracts* (because $A$ is in the denominator).

**Step 2 — Express Geometric Terms via Strain:**

The longitudinal strain is defined as:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{dL}{L}
$$

The transverse strain, governed by **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$:

$$
\varepsilon_t = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon = -\dfrac{dA}{2A}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\dfrac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back:**

$$
\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \cdot \varepsilon
$$

$$
\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon \cdot (1 + 2\nu)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Gauge Factor Definition:**

By definition, the Gauge Factor is:

$$
GF \equiv \dfrac{dR/R}{\varepsilon}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dfrac{dR}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon
$$

**Result — The Master Piezoresistive Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The fractional change in resistance is *linearly proportional* to strain, with $GF$ as the proportionality constant. This linear relationship is the foundation of every strain-gauge-based F/T sensor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the output voltage of a quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit.

**Circuit Topology — Wheatstone Bridge:**

A standard bridge has four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arranged in a diamond, with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ applied across opposite nodes and output $V_o$ measured across the other two.

**Step 1 — Voltage Divider on Left Arm (Top to Bottom):**

$$
V_B = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage Divider on Right Arm (Top to Bottom):**

$$
V_D = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}
$$

**Step 3 — Output Voltage Definition (Bridge Diagonal):**

$$
V_o = V_B - V_D
$$

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Balanced Bridge Condition:**

When all resistors are at their nominal value $R$ (no strain), $V_o = 0$. This is the *balanced* state.

**Step 5 — Apply Small Perturbation (Strain Active in $R_1$ Only):**

Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$, while $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$. Substitute:

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R}{(R + \Delta R) + R} - \dfrac{R}{R + R} \right)
$$

$$
V_o = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R}{2R + \Delta R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Approximation for $\Delta R \ll R$:**

$$
\dfrac{R}{2R + \Delta R} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \Delta R / 2R} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \left( 1 - \dfrac{\Delta R}{2R} \right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_o \approx V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right)
$$

$$
V_o \approx -V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the Strain Gauge Relation $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:**

$$
V_o \approx -\dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}
$$

**Final Result — Quarter-Bridge Output:**

$$
\boxed{V_o \approx \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}}
$$

(We drop the negative sign by convention since magnitude is of interest.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Force to Voltage Transfer Function (Full F/T Sensor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Objective:** Derive the end-to-end transfer function: $F \;\rightarrow\; \varepsilon \;\rightarrow\; \Delta R \;\rightarrow\; V_o$.

**Step 1 — Force to Strain (Mechanics):**

For a cantilever flexure of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $h$, under tip force $F$:

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{E \cdot b \cdot h^2}
$$

This comes from beam bending theory: $\sigma_{max} = M \cdot c / I$, where $M = F \cdot L$, $c = h/2$, $I = bh^3/12$, and $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$.

**Step 2 — Strain to Resistance Change:**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon_{max} = GF \cdot \dfrac{6FL}{E b h^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Resistance Change to Voltage:**

$$
V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon_{max}}{4} = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF}{4} \cdot \dfrac{6FL}{E b h^2}
$$

**Final Combined Transfer Function:**

$$
\boxed{V_o = \dfrac{3 \cdot V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot F \cdot L}{2 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot h^2}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The output voltage is **linearly proportional to the applied force** $F$, scaled by:
> - Excitation voltage $V_{ex}$
> - Gauge Factor $GF$
> - Geometric lever arm $L$
> - And *inversely* proportional to the **structural stiffness** $E b h^2 / L$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A strain gauge with $GF = 2.05$ is bonded to a steel cantilever ($E = 200$ GPa, $L = 0.1$ m, $b = 0.02$ m, $h = 0.002$ m). The bridge excitation is $V_{ex} = 5$ V. A force of $F = 10$ N is applied at the tip. Calculate:

(i) The maximum strain $\varepsilon_{max}$
(ii) The fractional resistance change $\Delta R / R$
(iii) The bridge output voltage $V_o$

**Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{6 \cdot 10 \cdot 0.1}{200 \times 10^9 \cdot 0.02 \cdot (0.002)^2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{6}{200 \times 10^9 \cdot 0.02 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{6}{1.6 \times 10^4} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4}
$$

> **Result:** $\varepsilon_{max} = 375 \, \mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain)

**(ii) Fractional Resistance Change:**

$$
\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon_{max} = 2.05 \cdot 3.75 \times 10^{-4} = 7.6875 \times 10^{-4}
$$

> **Result:** $\Delta R / R \approx 0.000769$ (or **769 ppm**)

**(iii) Bridge Output Voltage:**

$$
V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon_{max}}{4} = \dfrac{5 \cdot 2.05 \cdot 3.75 \times 10^{-4}}{4}
$$

$$
V_o = \dfrac{3.844 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 9.61 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}
$$

> **Result:** $V_o \approx 0.961$ mV

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The output is *tiny* (sub-millivolt range). This is why **amplifiers with high gain** (typically $1000\times$ or more) and **low-noise design** are essential in practical F/T sensor electronics.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Sensor Type | Strain Range | Frequency Range | Output Signal | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Strain Gauge (Metal)** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ | DC – $1$ kHz | $\Delta R / R$ (bridge) | Static/dynamic force, industrial robots |
| **Semiconductor Gauge** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$ | DC – $10$ kHz | $\Delta R / R$ (large) | High-sensitivity MEMS, prosthetic sensors |
| **Piezoelectric** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ | $0.1$ Hz – $100$ kHz | Charge $Q$ | Impact, vibration, dynamic-only |
| **Capacitive** | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-3}$ | DC – $10$ kHz | $\Delta C$ | Tactile skin, low-power applications |
| **Optical (FBG)** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ | DC – $50$ kHz | Wavelength shift $\Delta \lambda$ | EMI-immune, MRI, aerospace |
| **FSR (Polymer)** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ | DC – $10$ Hz | Resistance change | Low-cost tactile sensing, grippers |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Strain Gauge F/T Sensor Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Force F] --> B[Elastic Flexure]
    B --> C[Mechanical Strain Epsilon]
    C --> D[Strain Gauge Foil]
    D --> E[Delta R change]
    E --> F[Wheatstone Bridge]
    F --> G[Differential Voltage mV]
    G --> H[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    H --> I[Low Pass Filter]
    I --> J[Analog to Digital Converter]
    J --> K[Microcontroller DSP]
    K --> L[Force Torque Output Vector]
    L --> M[Robot Controller]
    M --> N[Compliance Control Loop]
```

> **Signal Flow Interpretation:** Force $\rightarrow$ Strain $\rightarrow$ Resistance Change $\rightarrow$ Bridge Output (mV) $\rightarrow$ Amplification $\rightarrow$ Filtering $\rightarrow$ ADC $\rightarrow$ Digital Processing $\rightarrow$ Robot Control Action.

## 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Topology (Quarter, Half, Full Variants)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Quarter["QUARTER BRIDGE - 1 Active Gauge"]
        Q1[Excitation Vex Top] --> Q2[R1 Active Gauge]
        Q2 --> Q3[Node B]
        Q3 --> Q4[R2 Fixed]
        Q4 --> Q5[Ground]
        Q6[Excitation Vex Top] --> Q7[R3 Fixed]
        Q7 --> Q8[Node D]
        Q8 --> Q9[R4 Fixed]
        Q9 --> Q10[Ground]
        Q3 --- Q11[Output Vo]
        Q8 --- Q11
    end
    subgraph Half["HALF BRIDGE - 2 Active Gauges"]
        H1[Excitation Top] --> H2[R1 Active +Tension]
        H2 --> H3[Node B]
        H3 --> H4[R2 Active -Compression]
        H4 --> H5[Ground]
        H6[Excitation Top] --> H7[R3 Fixed]
        H7 --> H8[Node D]
        H8 --> H9[R4 Fixed]
        H9 --> H10[Ground]
        H3 --- H11[Output Vo]
        H8 --- H11
    end
    subgraph Full["FULL BRIDGE - 4 Active Gauges"]
        F1[Excitation Top] --> F2[R1 Active +T]
        F2 --> F3[Node B]
        F3 --> F4[R2 Active -C]
        F4 --> F5[Ground]
        F6[Excitation Top] --> F7[R3 Active -C]
        F7 --> F8[Node D]
        F8 --> F9[R4 Active +T]
        F9 --> F10[Ground]
        F3 --- F11[Output Vo]
        F8 --- F11
    end
```

> **Design Insight:** Moving from **quarter $\rightarrow$ half $\rightarrow$ full bridge** increases sensitivity by factors of **$2\times$** and **$4\times$** respectively, and provides automatic **temperature compensation** (since adjacent gauges experience identical thermal expansion).

## 4.3 6-Axis F/T Sensor Internal Architecture (Wrist-Mounted)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Flange Robot Wrist] --> B[Cross Beam Flexure 1]
    B --> C[Gauges for Fx Fy Fz]
    C --> D[Central Hub]
    D --> E[Cross Beam Flexure 2]
    E --> F[Gauges for Tx Ty Tz]
    F --> G[Bottom Flange End Effector]
    H[Strain Gauge Array 8x] --> C
    I[Strain Gauge Array 4x] --> F
    J[Signal Processor Inside Hub] --> K[Digital Output Wrench Vector]
```

> **Architecture Note:** A typical 6-axis F/T sensor uses a **cylindrical cross** or **spoked wheel** flexure with **8–16 strain gauges** strategically placed so that the **decoupled measurement matrix** $\mathbf{C}$ can be inverted to extract the 6 wrench components from the gauge readings.

## 4.4 Force Feedback Control Loop (Compliance / Impedance Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Trajectory Xd] --> B[Controller]
    B --> C[Robot Actuator Torques Tau]
    C --> D[Robot Manipulator]
    D --> E[Environment Contact Force Fext]
    E --> F[Force Torque Sensor Reading Fmeas]
    F --> G[Feedback Comparator]
    G --> H[Force Error Fd minus Fmeas]
    H --> I[Compliance Gain Matrix K]
    I --> J[Position Correction Delta X]
    J --> B
    K[Fext] --> D
```

> **Control Interpretation:** The block diagram shows the **hybrid position/force control** loop. The *outer* loop compares the *measured* force $F_{meas}$ with the *desired* force $F_d$, computes a *compliance displacement* $\Delta x = K^{-1} \cdot (F_d - F_{meas})$, and feeds this correction back to the position controller.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define Gauge Factor. What is its typical value for a metallic strain gauge?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> The **Gauge Factor ($GF$)** of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of the **fractional change in resistance** ($\Delta R / R$) to the **applied mechanical strain** ($\varepsilon$):
>
> $$GF = \dfrac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$
>
> It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the sensitivity of the strain gauge. For **metallic foil gauges** (e.g., constantan, nichrome), the typical value is **$GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.1$**, while for **semiconductor gauges** (silicon), $GF$ can range from **$100$ to $200$**.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical value)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: Differentiate between piezoelectric and piezoresistive force sensors.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Piezoresistive | Piezoelectric |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Principle** | Resistance changes under strain | Charge generated under strain |
> | **Static Force** | Can measure (DC response) | Cannot measure (signal decays) |
> | **Sensitivity** | Moderate | Very High |
> | **Use Case** | Static weight, industrial F/T | Vibration, impact, dynamic only |
>
> *(1 mark per correct distinction point, 3 marks for 3 valid differences)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the working principle of a strain gauge-based force sensor. Derive the relationship between the change in resistance and the applied strain. Define the gauge factor. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A strain gauge is bonded to a steel cantilever beam of length $L = 0.15$ m, width $b = 0.025$ m, and thickness $h = 0.003$ m. The gauge factor is $GF = 2.1$, and the bridge excitation is $V_{ex} = 10$ V. Take $E = 200$ GPa. A force of $F = 50$ N is applied at the free end. Calculate:
  (i) Maximum strain $\varepsilon_{max}$
  (ii) Fractional change in resistance $\Delta R / R$
  (iii) Bridge output voltage for a quarter-bridge configuration (7 Marks)

---

> **Model Solution (Part A-a, 7 Marks):**
>
> **Working Principle:** A strain gauge is a thin metallic foil (often arranged in a *zig-zag* pattern) bonded to a flexible structural element (flexure). When an external force deforms the flexure, the gauge experiences the same strain. The strain alters the gauge's length, cross-section, and resistivity, changing its electrical resistance.
>
> **Derivation of $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:**
>
> Starting with the resistance of a uniform conductor:
>
> $$R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$$
>
> Taking the natural logarithm and differentiating:
>
> $$\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \dfrac{dL}{L} - \dfrac{dA}{A}$$
>
> Substitute strain $\varepsilon = dL / L$ and $dA/A = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$ (Poisson effect):
>
> $$\dfrac{dR}{R} = \dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$$
>
> Define Gauge Factor:
>
> $$GF = \dfrac{dR/R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{d\rho/\rho}{\varepsilon}$$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon}$$
>
> **Typical Value:** $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.1$ for metallic gauges (constantan).
>
> *(2 marks for principle, 3 marks for derivation, 1 mark for gauge factor, 1 mark for typical value)*

> **Model Solution (Part A-b, 7 Marks):**
>
> **(i) Maximum Strain** (using cantilever beam bending formula):
>
> $$\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{6 F L}{E b h^2} = \dfrac{6 \times 50 \times 0.15}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.025 \times (0.003)^2}$$
>
> $$\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{45}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.025 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{45}{45{,}000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{max} = 1000 \, \mu\varepsilon}$$
>
> **(ii) Fractional Resistance Change:**
>
> $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \varepsilon_{max} = 2.1 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = 0.0021 \text{ (or 2100 ppm)}}$$
>
> **(iii) Quarter-Bridge Output Voltage:**
>
> $$V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon_{max}}{4} = \dfrac{10 \times 2.1 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4}$$
>
> $$V_o = \dfrac{0.021}{4} = 5.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}$$
>
> $$\boxed{V_o = 5.25 \text{ mV}}$$
>
> *(2 marks each for parts i, ii, iii with correct steps)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **6-axis Force/Torque sensor** used at the wrist of a robotic manipulator. List its typical applications. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the output voltage equation of a **Wheatstone bridge** with one active strain gauge. Show that a full-bridge configuration provides four times the sensitivity of a quarter-bridge. (7 Marks)

---

> **Model Solution (Part B-a, 7 Marks):**
>
> **Architecture of a 6-Axis Wrist F/T Sensor:**
>
> A 6-axis F/T sensor consists of:
>
> 1. **Top flange:** Bolted to the robot wrist (last joint).
> 2. **Cross-beam flexure (or Maltese cross):** A monolithic elastic body with thin sections that deform under load.
> 3. **Strain gauge arrays:** Typically 8–16 gauges bonded to the flexure at strategic locations — usually arranged in 3 sets of 4 gauges (one set per force/torque axis).
> 4. **Signal conditioning electronics:** Amplifier, ADC, microcontroller.
> 5. **Bottom flange:** Connected to the end-effector / gripper.
>
> **Working:** When a wrench $\mathbf{W} = [F_x, F_y, F_z, \tau_x, \tau_y, \tau_z]^T$ is applied, each strain gauge produces a small resistance change. A **decoupling matrix** $\mathbf{C}$ (computed via calibration) inverts the gauge readings to recover the 6 wrench components:
>
> $$\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{C} \cdot \mathbf{V}_{gauges}$$
>
> **Applications:**
> - Robotic assembly (peg-in-hole insertion)
> - Polishing and deburring
> - Force-controlled grasping in cobots
> - Surgical robotics (Da Vinci system)
> - Haptic teleoperation
> - Product testing (button-press force)
>
> *(2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for working, 2 marks for applications)*

> **Model Solution (Part B-b, 7 Marks):**
>
> **Wheatstone Bridge Derivation (Single Active Gauge):**
>
> Standard bridge with $R_1$ active, $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ (fixed). Output voltage:
>
> $$V_o = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right) = V_{ex} \left( \dfrac{R}{R + \Delta R + R} - \dfrac{1}{2} \right)$$
>
> Simplifying with $\Delta R \ll R$:
>
> $$V_o \approx -\dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R}{4R} = -\dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}{4}$$
>
> **Quarter-bridge sensitivity:** $S_{1/4} = V_{ex} \cdot GF / 4$
>
> **Full-Bridge with 4 Active Gauges (2 in tension, 2 in compression):**
>
> Opposing arms change in opposite directions. If two arms have $+\Delta R$ and two have $-\Delta R$, the output becomes:
>
> $$V_{o,full} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$
>
> **Full-bridge sensitivity:** $S_{full} = V_{ex} \cdot GF$
>
> **Sensitivity Ratio:**
>
> $$\dfrac{S_{full}}{S_{1/4}} = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot GF}{V_{ex} \cdot GF / 4} = 4$$
>
> $$\boxed{\text{Full-bridge is 4 times more sensitive than quarter-bridge}}$$
>
> *(3 marks for quarter-bridge derivation, 2 marks for full-bridge derivation, 2 marks for ratio comparison)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in F/T Sensor Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Forgetting that strain $\varepsilon$ is *dimensionless* and is often expressed in $\mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain). Always convert: $1000 \, \mu\varepsilon = 1 \times 10^{-3}$.
> 2. **Sign of Output:** Wheatstone bridge output can be positive or negative depending on which arm strains positively. *Always* state the sign convention in your answer.
> 3. **GF vs. Sensitivity:** The Gauge Factor is a *property of the gauge material*, not the circuit. Bridge sensitivity depends on both $GF$ **and** the bridge configuration.
> 4. **Quarter vs. Full Bridge:** Students often incorrectly assume the full-bridge output is $4 \times$ the *voltage* — but it's $4 \times$ the *sensitivity per unit strain*.
> 5. **Piezoelectric Trap:** Do not claim piezoelectric sensors measure *static* force. The charge leaks — use them only for *dynamic* signals.
> 6. **Cantilever Beam Formula:** Forgetting to square the thickness $h^2$ in the strain equation. This is a frequent arithmetic error.
> 7. **Decoupling Matrix:** In 6-axis sensors, students forget that gauge readings are *coupled* — each gauge is sensitive to *multiple* wrench components, requiring a calibration matrix to separate them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Force and Torque Sensors**

- ✅ **Definition:** F/T sensors measure the 6-component wrench vector $\mathbf{W} = [F_x, F_y, F_z, \tau_x, \tau_y, \tau_z]^T$.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ — the *foundational* relation.
- ✅ **Gauge Factor:** $GF \approx 2$ for metallic, $100$–$200$ for semiconductor.
- ✅ **Bridge Output:** Quarter-bridge $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon / 4$; Full-bridge gives $4 \times$ sensitivity.
- ✅ **Cantilever Strain:** $\varepsilon_{max} = 6FL / (E b h^2)$ — memorize this.
- ✅ **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ (linear elastic region).
- ✅ **Compliance vs. Stiffness:** $C = 1 / K$; high compliance = soft, low compliance = rigid.
- ✅ **Piezoelectric Limitation:** DC response = **NO**; only dynamic signals.
- ✅ **Wheatstone Balance:** Balanced bridge gives $V_o = 0$ when all $R$'s are equal.
- ✅ **Sensor Placement:** Wrist sensor → tool-frame wrench; Joint sensor → joint torque.
- ✅ **Cobot Application:** Force sensing is the *enabler* of safe human-robot collaboration.
- ✅ **Sensitivity Multipliers:** Quarter $\rightarrow$ $1\times$; Half $\rightarrow$ $2\times$; Full $\rightarrow$ $4\times$.
- ✅ **Calibration Matrix:** 6-axis sensors require a $6 \times N$ decoupling matrix $C$ (with $N \geq 6$ gauges).
- ✅ **Force Error Sign:** $\Delta F = F_d - F_{measured}$ is the *input* to the compliance controller.
- ✅ **Compliance Control Law:** $\Delta x = K^{-1} (F_d - F_{meas})$ — adjusts position to maintain desired force.

> **End of Topic Notes — Force and Torque Sensors (PEIET411, Module 3)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Sensors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Velocity Sensors

# ⚙️ Velocity Sensors in Robotics — Module 3

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Velocity Sensor:** A velocity sensor is a *transducing device* that converts the *rate of change of position* (linear or angular) of a mechanical element into a corresponding electrical, optical, or digital output signal proportional to the velocity. In robotic systems, velocity sensors are primarily used to measure the **angular velocity of robot joints** ($\omega$), the **linear velocity of end-effectors** ($v$), and the **wheel speed of mobile robots** ($v_w$), forming a critical feedback element within the inner velocity-control loop of servo drives and motion controllers.

In robotics, velocity information is **essential** for:
- **Closed-loop velocity control** (torque-mode and velocity-mode actuation).
- **Trajectory tracking** (ensuring smooth, predefined motion profiles).
- **Stability and damping** of position-control systems (PD/PID controllers).
- **Safety monitoring** (collision avoidance, jerk control).

---

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy — "The Speedometer of the Robot"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — Your Car's Speedometer:**
> Think of a velocity sensor as the *speedometer of a robot*. Just as a car's speedometer continuously senses how fast the wheels are spinning and converts this into a needle movement (or digital readout), a robotic velocity sensor continuously monitors how fast a motor shaft is rotating (angular velocity $\omega$) or how fast a robot's end-effector is translating (linear velocity $v$). Without it, the robot would be "driving blind," unable to verify whether it is moving at the *right speed* along its commanded trajectory.

### 🏷️ Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Convention)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Angular Velocity** ($\omega$) → measured in **radians/second (rad/s)** or **RPM**.
> - **Linear Velocity** ($v$) → measured in **meters/second (m/s)**.
> - **Resolution** → pulses per revolution (PPR) for encoders.
> - **Sensitivity / Voltage Constant** ($K_e$) → **V/(rad/s)** for tachogenerators.
> - **Bandwidth** → **Hz**, defines the highest measurable velocity fluctuation.
> - **Operating Range** → maximum measurable velocity.
> - **Linearity Error** → typically **±0.1 % to ±0.5 %** of full scale.

---

### 🧭 Classification of Velocity Sensors

| Class | Sensor Type | Output Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Digital** | Incremental Optical Encoder, Quadrature Encoder | Pulse train |
| **Analog** | DC Tachogenerator, Resolver | Voltage / Sine-Cosine |
| **Magnetic** | Hall-Effect Sensor, Variable Reluctance | Digital pulses / Sine |
| **Inertial** | Piezoelectric Vibration Sensor | Charge / Voltage |
| **Optical** | Laser Doppler Vibrometer | Frequency shift |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 2.1 Operational Principles — Why and How

#### **A. Incremental Optical Encoder (Most Common Robotic Velocity Sensor)**

**Why it works:** A slotted disc attached to a rotating shaft alternately *blocks* and *passes* a light beam between an LED source and a photodetector. As the disc rotates, a *pulse train* is generated whose frequency is directly proportional to angular velocity.

**How it works (Step-by-step logic):**
1. A rotating disc with $N$ radial slots is rigidly coupled to the shaft.
2. An **LED** on one side and a **photodiode/phototransistor** on the other form an optical path.
3. Each slot passage → light detected → one pulse emitted.
4. The pulse train is fed to a **frequency-to-voltage converter (F/V)** or a **counter with fixed time-window** to determine angular velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity extraction from encoder pulses** is achieved by **two methods**:
> - **M-method (Frequency counting):** Count pulses over a fixed gate time $T_g$.
> - **T-method (Period measurement):** Measure time between successive pulses using a high-frequency clock.

---

#### **B. DC Tachogenerator**

**Why it works:** A DC generator's induced EMF is governed by **Faraday's Law**, which is fundamentally proportional to the rotational speed of the armature within a magnetic field. Hence, the *generated voltage* becomes a *direct analog readout* of angular velocity.

**How it works:**
1. Permanent magnet provides a steady flux $\Phi$ across the armature.
2. As the armature rotates at $\omega$, conductors cut the flux lines.
3. EMF is generated: $E = K_e \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$.
4. Since $\Phi$ is constant (PM field), $E \propto \omega$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Disadvantages of DC Tachogenerators in modern robotics:** brush wear, voltage ripple, drift with temperature, limited bandwidth. They are *being phased out* in favor of digital encoders but still appear in KTU theory questions.

---

#### **C. Resolver**

A resolver is an **electromagnetic absolute position sensor** that, when processed through a *Resolver-to-Digital Converter (RDC)*, also yields accurate velocity. The primary winding is excited with a sinusoidal carrier, and the two secondary windings (90° apart) produce signals whose *phase and amplitude* encode shaft angle.

---

#### **D. Hall-Effect Sensor**

A small semiconductor device placed near a rotating magnetic wheel (with alternating north/south poles) produces a *digital pulse* each time a magnetic pole passes. Common in **mobile robot wheel encoders**.

---

#### **E. Piezoelectric Velocity Sensor (Seismometer-type)**

Used in *vibration analysis* of robotic structures. A *proof mass* on a piezoelectric element generates a charge proportional to *velocity* when the base vibrates.

---

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulae below are KTU Exam-Frequently-Tested.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f}{N}$ | Angular velocity from encoder pulse frequency $f$, with $N$ slots | rad/s |
| 2 | $v = r \cdot \omega$ | Linear velocity of a wheel/roller of radius $r$ | m/s |
| 3 | $f = \dfrac{N \cdot \omega}{2\pi}$ | Pulse frequency from angular velocity | Hz |
| 4 | $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ | Tachogenerator output EMF (with constant flux) | V |
| 5 | $\omega(k) = \dfrac{\theta(k) - \theta(k-1)}{T_s}$ | Numerical differentiation of position | rad/s |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{\Delta N}{N \cdot T_g} \cdot 60$ | M-method: angular velocity in RPM using gate time $T_g$ (s) | RPM |
| 7 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f_{clk}}{N \cdot M}$ | T-method: angular velocity using $M$ clock pulses | rad/s |
| 8 | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(\theta) \cdot v_{ee}$ | Joint velocities from end-effector Cartesian velocity (Jacobian) | rad/s |
| 9 | $v_{ee} = J(\theta) \cdot \dot{q}$ | Forward differential kinematics | m/s |
| 10 | $\tau_{back-emf} = K_b \cdot \omega$ | Back-EMF torque opposing motor rotation | N·m |

> ⚠️ **Pipe-symbol alert avoided:** All table entries use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of literal $\vert$ to maintain markdown table integrity.

---

### 🌐 Engineering Utility in Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial relevance:**
> - Velocity sensors are integrated into **servo drive loops** (e.g., Siemens SINAMICS, Fanuc, ABB robots).
> - They enable **field-oriented control (FOC)** of BLDC and PMSM motors.
> - They support **collision detection** by sensing abnormal joint velocity transients.
> - In **AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)**, Hall-effect encoders measure wheel odometry for SLAM.
> - In **CNC machines**, velocity feedback enables high-precision contouring.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 1: Angular Velocity from Encoder Pulses (M-method)

**Given:**
- Encoder with $N$ = **500 pulses/revolution (PPR)**.
- Gate time (sampling window) $T_g = 0.1 \, \text{s}$.
- Counted pulses in one gate window: $\Delta N = 250$ pulses.

**Step 1 — Find the pulse frequency $f$.**

The pulse frequency is the *number of pulses per second*:

$$
f = \frac{\Delta N}{T_g}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
f = \frac{250}{0.1}
$$

$$
f = 2500 \, \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert frequency to angular velocity.**

Since each revolution produces $N$ pulses, the number of revolutions per second (in Hz) is $f / N$. The angular velocity in rad/s is:

$$
\omega = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f}{N}
$$

Substituting $f = 2500 \, \text{Hz}$ and $N = 500$:

$$
\omega = 2\pi \cdot \frac{2500}{500}
$$

$$
\omega = 2\pi \cdot 5
$$

$$
\omega = 10\pi \, \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to RPM.**

$$
\omega_{RPM} = \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi}
$$

$$
\omega_{RPM} = 10\pi \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi}
$$

$$
\omega_{RPM} = 300 \, \text{RPM}
$$

> ✅ **Result:** $\omega = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \, \text{rad/s}$ or equivalently **300 RPM**.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Linear Velocity of a Robot's End-Effector (Forward Differential Kinematics)

**Given:**
- A 2-link planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$.
- Joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$ and joint velocities $\dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2$.

**Step 1 — Recall forward kinematics (position).**

$$
x = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate each position equation w.r.t. time.**

Differentiating $x$ with respect to time $t$ using the **chain rule**:

$$
\dot{x} = -L_1 \sin(\theta_1)\dot{\theta}_1 \;-\; L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

Differentiating $y$ with respect to time $t$:

$$
\dot{y} = L_1 \cos(\theta_1)\dot{\theta}_1 \;+\; L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Express in matrix form using the Jacobian $J(\theta)$.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-\left[L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
\left[L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] & L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This compact form is:

$$
v_{ee} = J(\theta) \cdot \dot{q}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert for joint velocities (Inverse differential kinematics).**

For non-singular configurations:

$$
\dot{q} = J^{-1}(\theta) \cdot v_{ee}
$$

> ✅ **Insight:** This derivation is fundamental to KTU problems on **velocity mapping** between Cartesian (task) space and joint space.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3: Tachogenerator EMF Equation from Faraday's Law

**Given:**
- A DC tachogenerator with $P$ poles, $Z$ armature conductors, flux per pole $\Phi$.
- Rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Write the standard EMF equation of a DC machine.**

$$
E = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}
$$

where $N$ is RPM and $A$ is the number of parallel paths.

**Step 2 — Convert RPM to angular velocity $\omega$.**

Since $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, we have $N = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2\pi}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute $N$ into the EMF equation.**

$$
E = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A} \cdot \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the constant group.**

Let $K_e' = \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{2\pi \cdot A}$. Then:

$$
E = K_e' \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 5 — For a permanent-magnet tachogenerator with constant flux.**

Since $\Phi = \text{constant}$, we define a single constant $K_e = K_e' \cdot \Phi$:

$$
E = K_e \cdot \omega
$$

> ✅ **Insight:** This linear relationship is *why* a tachogenerator behaves as an ideal velocity sensor. The constant $K_e$ is the **voltage sensitivity** of the sensor (in V·s/rad).

---

### 🧮 Derivation 4: Back-EMF Induced in a Motor (Velocity Sensor Connection)

For a DC motor used as both actuator *and* velocity sensor:

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's voltage law across the armature circuit.**

$$
V_a = R_a I_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the back-EMF expression $E_b = K_b \omega$.**

$$
V_a = R_a I_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + K_b \omega
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for angular velocity at steady state** ($dI_a/dt = 0$):

$$
\omega = \frac{V_a - R_a I_a}{K_b}
$$

> ✅ **Engineering utility:** Most industrial drives *measure velocity by measuring back-EMF* during PWM off-cycles, eliminating the need for a separate tachogenerator.

---

### 🧮 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A mobile robot uses a Hall-effect encoder on its drive wheel. The encoder has **$N = 60$ pulses/revolution**, the wheel diameter is **$D = 20 \, \text{cm}$**, and the controller counts **$\Delta N = 120$ pulses** in a gate time of **$T_g = 0.05 \, \text{s}$**. Compute (a) the angular velocity of the wheel in rad/s, (b) the linear velocity of the robot in m/s, and (c) the distance traveled in 10 seconds.

**Solution:**

**(a) Angular velocity**

$$
f = \frac{\Delta N}{T_g} = \frac{120}{0.05} = 2400 \, \text{Hz}
$$

$$
\omega = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f}{N} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{2400}{60} = 2\pi \cdot 40
$$

$$
\omega = 80\pi \approx 251.33 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

**(b) Linear velocity**

Radius $r = D/2 = 0.1 \, \text{m}$:

$$
v = r \cdot \omega = 0.1 \cdot 80\pi
$$

$$
v = 8\pi \approx 25.13 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**(c) Distance in 10 s**

$$
d = v \cdot t = 25.13 \cdot 10 = 251.33 \, \text{m}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 📐 Diagram 1: Block Diagram of a Velocity-Sensor-Based Servo Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD["Velocity Command v_ref"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    FB["Feedback Velocity v_fb"] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> CTRL["Velocity Controller PI"]
    CTRL --> DRV["Power Amplifier PWM Driver"]
    DRV --> MOT["DC Servo Motor Actuator"]
    MOT --> SHAFT["Rotating Shaft Load"]
    SHAFT --> VEL["Velocity Sensor Encoder Tachogenerator"]
    VEL --> FB
    VEL --> DISP["Display / Data Logger"]

    classDef cmd fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#0d3a8c;
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#6b5400;
    classDef act fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#7a1f24;
    classDef sens fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#0f5132;

    class CMD,DISP cmd;
    class CTRL,DRV ctrl;
    class MOT,SHAFT act;
    class VEL,FB sens;
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The blue blocks are reference / display units, the yellow blocks are the *control electronics*, the red blocks are the *actuation chain*, and the green blocks form the *velocity feedback loop*. The summing junction (yellow circle) subtracts the measured velocity from the commanded reference — this error is the input to the **PI velocity controller**.

---

### 📐 Diagram 2: Quadrature Encoder Phase Relationship (Velocity + Direction)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DISC["Slotted Disc Rotating at angular velocity omega"] --> LED["LED Light Source"]
    LED --> PH1["Photodiode Channel A"]
    LED --> PH2["Photodiode Channel B shifted by 90 degrees"]
    PH1 --> SIGA["Square Wave Signal A"]
    PH2 --> SIGB["Square Wave Signal B shifted by 90 degrees"]
    SIGA --> DEC["Quadrature Decoder Logic"]
    SIGB --> DEC
    DEC --> DIR["Direction Bit high or low"]
    DEC --> VEL["Velocity Output omega proportional to pulse rate"]
    DEC --> POS["Position Output theta integrated"]

    classDef src fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0d6efd;
    classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#198754;
    class DISC,LED src;
    class VEL,POS,DIR out;
```

> **Engineering Insight:** Two channels (A and B) 90° out of phase allow **direction sensing** — Channel A leads B for clockwise rotation, B leads A for counter-clockwise. The **decoder** derives both velocity (from pulse rate) and incremental position (by counting edge transitions, *four times* the line count = "×4 decoding").

---

### 📐 Diagram 3: DC Tachogenerator Internal Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SHAFTIN["Input Shaft coupled to motor or robot joint"] --> ARM["Armature Rotating at angular velocity omega"]
    ARM --> CUT["Conductors cutting magnetic flux Phi"]
    MAG["Permanent Magnet providing steady flux Phi"] --> CUT
    CUT --> EMF["Induced EMF E proportional to omega"]
    EMF --> BRUSH["Brushes and Commutator"]
    BRUSH --> TERM["Output Terminals Vout"]
    TERM --> LOAD["External Voltmeter or ADC"]

    classDef in fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107;
    classDef mag fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545;
    classDef out fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754;
    class SHAFTIN,ARM,CUT in;
    class MAG,BRUSH mag;
    class EMF,TERM,LOAD out;
```

> **Functional flow:** Mechanical rotation (yellow) → Electromagnetic induction (red) → Analog voltage output (green). The output voltage is the *direct analog readout* of the input angular velocity.

---

### 📐 Diagram 4: Complete Velocity-Sensor Functional Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHY["Physical Domain"]
        P1["Robot Joint / Wheel / End Effector"]
        P2["Mechanical Coupling Shaft"]
    end

    subgraph SEN["Sensor Layer"]
        S1["Velocity Transducer Encoder Tachogenerator Hall Resolver"]
        S2["Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter"]
    end

    subgraph DIG["Digital Processing Layer"]
        D1["Counter or F to V Converter"]
        D2["Microcontroller DSP FPGA"]
        D3["Velocity Calculation Unit"]
    end

    subgraph CTL["Control Layer"]
        C1["PID or PI Controller"]
        C2["Trajectory Generator"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output Layer"]
        O1["Velocity Display RPM rad per s"]
        O2["Actuator Command Signal PWM"]
    end

    P1 --> P2 --> S1 --> S2 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D3 --> C1
    C2 --> C1
    C1 --> O2
    D3 --> O1

    classDef phy fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#dc3545;
    classDef sen fill:#cdeac0,stroke:#198754;
    classDef dig fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#0d6efd;
    classDef ctl fill:#fff1b8,stroke:#ffc107;
    classDef out fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#6610f2;

    class P1,P2 phy;
    class S1,S2 sen;
    class D1,D2,D3 dig;
    class C1,C2 ctl;
    class O1,O2 out;
```

> **Engineering reading:** This is the *end-to-end functional architecture* of a robotic velocity-sensing subsystem, color-coded by domain: **physical (red) → sensor (green) → digital (blue) → control (yellow) → output (purple)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ **Confusing PPR (pulses per revolution) with CPR (counts per revolution).** After ×4 quadrature decoding, **CPR = 4 × PPR**. Students lose marks by not declaring whether they are using PPR or CPR.
> - ❌ **Forgetting unit conversion:** The encoder formula gives rad/s, but the question may ask for **RPM** — multiply by $60/(2\pi)$.
> - ❌ **In tachogenerator EMF derivation**, students forget the parallel-path factor $A$ and lose partial credit.
> - ❌ **Numerical differentiation** of noisy position signals *amplifies noise* — students must mention **low-pass filtering** before differentiation in practical applications.
> - ❌ In Jacobian-based velocity problems, students often forget to verify **$\det J \neq 0$** (non-singular configuration).

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between an **incremental encoder** and an **absolute encoder** as velocity-sensing devices. Which one retains position information after a power cycle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses per unit angle | Unique digital code per shaft position |
| Position after power-off | **Lost** — needs homing | **Retained** (non-volatile) |
| Velocity measurement | By counting pulse rate | By differentiating the unique code |
| Cost & complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Use in robotics | Wheel odometry, joint velocity | Multi-turn joint position |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The **absolute encoder** retains position information after a power cycle because each shaft position has a *unique binary code* stored optically/magnetically. *(Total: 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2.** A DC tachogenerator has a voltage constant $K_e = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$. Compute the output voltage when the shaft rotates at $1500 \, \text{RPM}$.

**Model Answer:**

Step 1 — Convert RPM to rad/s:
$$
\omega = 1500 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

Step 2 — Apply tachogenerator equation:
$$
E = K_e \cdot \omega = 0.05 \cdot 50\pi
$$

$$
E = 2.5\pi \approx 7.854 \, \text{V}
$$

**Answer (3 marks):** $E \approx 7.85 \, \text{V}$

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Module 3 Internal Choice, 14 marks]**

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **pulse frequency** and **angular velocity** for an incremental optical encoder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar robot has Jacobian:
$$
J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin(\theta_1) - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$
If $L_1 = L_2 = 1 \, \text{m}$, $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, and the joint velocities are $\dot{\theta}_1 = 1 \, \text{rad/s}$, $\dot{\theta}_2 = 0.5 \, \text{rad/s}$, compute the **end-effector velocity vector**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

Step 1 — Consider an incremental encoder with $N$ slots per revolution. As the disc completes one full revolution, $N$ light pulses are generated.

Step 2 — The pulse frequency $f$ equals the *number of revolutions per second* times $N$:
$$
f = N \cdot f_{rev}
$$

Step 3 — Angular velocity in revolutions per second:
$$
f_{rev} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}
$$

Step 4 — Substitute back:
$$
f = N \cdot \frac{\omega}{2\pi}
$$

Step 5 — Solve for angular velocity (the velocity-sensor equation):
$$
\boxed{\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot f}{N}}
$$

Step 6 — M-method: If $\Delta N$ pulses are counted in gate time $T_g$, then $f = \Delta N / T_g$:
$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot \Delta N}{N \cdot T_g}
$$

Step 7 — In RPM:
$$
\omega_{RPM} = \frac{60 \cdot \Delta N}{N \cdot T_g}
$$

> ✅ *(7 marks — full derivation awarded)*

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

Step 1 — Evaluate Jacobian elements at the given angles. Let $L_1 = L_2 = 1 \, \text{m}$. Compute needed trig values:
- $\sin(30°) = 0.5$
- $\cos(30°) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$
- $\sin(75°) = \sin(45° + 30°) \approx 0.9659$
- $\cos(75°) = \cos(45° + 30°) \approx 0.2588$

Step 2 — Compute Jacobian entries:

$$
J_{11} = -(1)\sin(30°) - (1)\sin(75°) = -0.5 - 0.9659 = -1.4659
$$

$$
J_{12} = -(1)\sin(75°) = -0.9659
$$

$$
J_{21} = (1)\cos(30°) + (1)\cos(75°) = 0.8660 + 0.2588 = 1.1248
$$

$$
J_{22} = (1)\cos(75°) = 0.2588
$$

So:

$$
J = \begin{bmatrix} -1.4659 & -0.9659 \\ 1.1248 & 0.2588 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 3 — Apply forward differential kinematics:

$$
v_{ee} = J \cdot \dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.4659 & -0.9659 \\ 1.1248 & 0.2588 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the components:

$$
\dot{x} = (-1.4659)(1) + (-0.9659)(0.5) = -1.4659 - 0.4829 = -1.9488 \, \text{m/s}
$$

$$
\dot{y} = (1.1248)(1) + (0.2588)(0.5) = 1.1248 + 0.1294 = 1.2542 \, \text{m/s}
$$

Step 5 — Final end-effector velocity vector and magnitude:

$$
\vec{v}_{ee} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.949 \\ 1.254 \end{bmatrix} \, \text{m/s}
$$

Magnitude:
$$
\vert \vec{v}_{ee} \vert = \sqrt{(-1.949)^2 + (1.254)^2} = \sqrt{3.798 + 1.573} = \sqrt{5.371} \approx 2.318 \, \text{m/s}
$$

> ✅ *(7 marks — full computation awarded)*

---

#### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **DC tachogenerator** as a velocity sensor. Derive its EMF equation from Faraday's law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the working of a **quadrature encoder** for velocity measurement. Explain how direction sensing is achieved and what **×4 decoding** means. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution Outline (Examiner's Reference):**

**(a) DC Tachogenerator [7 marks]:**

- **Block diagram (3 marks):** Stator (PM) → rotor (armature) → commutator + brushes → output voltage terminals. Show coupling to rotating shaft.
- **Working (1 mark):** EMF induced when conductors cut magnetic flux; $E \propto \Phi \omega$.
- **Derivation (3 marks):** Start from standard DC machine EMF, substitute $N = 60\omega / (2\pi)$, simplify to $E = K_e \omega$, where $K_e = PZ\Phi / (2\pi A)$.

**(b) Quadrature Encoder [7 marks]:**

- **Construction (2 marks):** Two photodetectors offset by 90° (one quarter slot pitch).
- **Pulse trains (2 marks):** Channel A and Channel B produce square waves 90° out of phase.
- **Direction sensing (2 marks):** If A leads B → CW rotation; if B leads A → CCW. Implemented via a D-flip-flop or quadrature decoder IC (e.g., LS7184).
- **×4 decoding (1 mark):** Counting both rising and falling edges of both channels gives 4× the resolution per revolution. If base PPR = 500, then effective CPR = 2000 per revolution.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Velocity Sensors (Module 3):**

- ✅ **Velocity** = rate of change of position. **Angular** $\omega = d\theta/dt$ in rad/s; **Linear** $v = dx/dt$ in m/s.
- ✅ **Incremental encoder equation:** $\omega = 2\pi f / N$ — *memorize this formula*.
- ✅ **CPR vs PPR:** After ×4 quadrature decoding, effective resolution is **4× the PPR**.
- ✅ **M-method (frequency counting)** is used for **high speeds**; **T-method (period measurement)** is used for **low speeds** — choose the right method.
- ✅ **DC Tachogenerator:** $E = K_e \cdot \omega$ — voltage is *directly* proportional to angular velocity because flux is held constant by permanent magnets.
- ✅ **Tachogenerator derivation:** Start from Faraday's EMF equation $E = P\Phi Z N / (60A)$, substitute $N = 60\omega/(2\pi)$, and simplify.
- ✅ **Back-EMF in motors:** $E_b = K_b \omega$ — used by sensorless drives to *infer* velocity.
- ✅ **Hall-effect sensors** output a *digital pulse* per magnetic pole passage — common in **AGV wheel odometry**.
- ✅ **Resolver:** Absolute electromagnetic sensor; needs **RDC** chip to extract digital angle and hence velocity.
- ✅ **Jacobian-based velocity mapping:** $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(\theta) v_{ee}$ — requires $\det J \neq 0$.
- ✅ **Velocity-loop bandwidth** typically ranges **100 Hz to 1 kHz** in industrial servo systems.
- ✅ **Numerical differentiation** of position is **noise-sensitive** — always use a **low-pass filter** before differentiating.
- ✅ **Practical robotics trend:** Discrete encoders are *preferable* over analog tachogenerators due to **higher accuracy, digital noise immunity, and absence of brush wear**.
- ✅ **KTU exam tip:** Always *state units explicitly* and *convert to RPM or rad/s* as required by the question.
- ✅ **KTU exam tip:** When asked about "types," structure your answer as a **classification table** with operating principle + advantages/disadvantages for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Encoders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Encoders

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoder (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> An **encoder** is an electro-mechanical or electro-optical feedback sensor that converts the **angular position** (rotary encoder) or **linear displacement** (linear encoder) of a shaft into an analog or digital electrical signal. The signal is then decoded by a counter or controller to determine the **absolute or relative position**, **velocity**, and **direction of rotation** of the shaft.

In the context of **Robotics and Automation**, encoders form the primary element of the **position-sensing loop** in a closed-loop motion control system. They act as the "eyes" of the motor controller, providing real-time positional data essential for trajectory tracking, joint control in robotic manipulators, and wheel odometry in mobile robots.

---

## 1.2 Types of Encoders — KTU Classification

> [!NOTE]
> KTU classifies encoders based on **two orthogonal axes**: (1) Output Type, and (2) Physical Measurement Type.

### A. Based on Output Signal
1. **Incremental Encoder**
   - Generates a fixed number of equally spaced pulses per revolution.
   - Reports only **relative** position changes from a reference (home) point.
   - Requires a counter and a homing routine at startup.

2. **Absolute Encoder**
   - Generates a unique digital code (binary, Gray, BCD) for **every distinct angular position** of the shaft.
   - Reports true **absolute** position even after power loss.

### B. Based on Physical Sensing Principle
1. **Optical Encoder** (most common in KTU problems)
2. **Magnetic Encoder**
3. **Capacitive Encoder**
4. **Inductive / Resolver**
5. **Laser Encoder**

### C. Based on Geometry
1. **Rotary Encoder** — measures shaft angle $\theta$
2. **Linear Encoder** — measures straight-line displacement $x$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Speedometer Cable vs. the Digital Odometer**
> Imagine the old mechanical speedometer cable of a bicycle. It spins proportionally to wheel rotation, and you can "guess" the distance by counting revolutions multiplied by wheel circumference. That is exactly how an **Incremental Encoder** works — it only tells you *how much* rotation occurred since the last reading.
>
> Now imagine a modern digital wristwatch where each second, minute, and hour hand gives you a unique state. Even if the watch loses power, when you put the battery back in, it still shows the correct time because every angle maps to a unique code. That is precisely how an **Absolute Encoder** behaves — every shaft angle has its own unique "fingerprint" word.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution (PPR) | pulses per revolution | **Standard unit:** PPR |
| Resolution (bits) | $n$ bits | Total codes $= 2^n$ |
| Resolution (degrees) | $360^\circ / N$ | Angular step per pulse |
| Line count ($N$) | integer | Number of slots on code wheel |
| Output voltage | 5 V DC (TTL) or 24 V (HTL) | Industrial standard |
| Operating temperature | $-40^\circ\text{C}$ to $+85^\circ\textbf{C}$ | **Industrial Grade** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of an Optical Incremental Encoder

The optical incremental encoder is composed of **four key physical elements**:

1. **Light Source** — typically an **LED** emitting in the infrared (~880 nm) or red (~660 nm) spectrum.
2. **Code Disk (Code Wheel)** — a glass or mylar disk with precisely etched radial slots. The number of slots equals the **line count** $N$.
3. **Photodetector / Photodiode Array** — converts the interrupted light beam into electrical pulses.
4. **Signal Conditioning Electronics** — produces clean square-wave outputs (Channel A and Channel B).

### Operational Logic Steps
- **Step 1:** The LED emits a continuous, collimated beam of light toward the code disk.
- **Step 2:** As the shaft rotates, the **transparent slots** and **opaque bars** of the code disk alternately allow and block the light beam.
- **Step 3:** The photodetector receives a **pulsating light signal**, which is converted into a corresponding **electrical square-wave pulse train**.
- **Step 4:** A second photodetector (offset by **90° electrical** = one-quarter of a slot pitch) generates Channel B, used for **direction discrimination** and **quadrature decoding**.
- **Step 5:** A third detector at the **Index/Reference track** (single slot per revolution) generates the **Z-pulse (Index pulse)**, used to reset the counter to absolute zero once per revolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two channels (A and B)?**
> A single channel cannot tell you the **direction** of rotation. By offsetting Channel B by 90°, the leading/lagging phase relationship tells the controller whether the shaft is rotating clockwise (CW) or counter-clockwise (CCW). This is called **quadrature encoding**.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle of an Absolute Encoder

An absolute encoder uses a code disk with **multiple concentric tracks**, each having a different slot pattern corresponding to a binary bit position. The most common encoding scheme is **Gray Code**, because:

> [!NOTE]
> **Gray Code Property:** Only **one bit changes** between adjacent positions, which **eliminates transition errors** that occur when multiple bits change simultaneously in straight binary (e.g., from 0111 to 1000, where all 4 bits flip and may produce false intermediate codes).

For an $n$-bit absolute encoder:
- Number of unique positions $= 2^n$
- Angular resolution $= \dfrac{360^\circ}{2^n}$

---

## 2.3 Quadrature Decoding — The X1, X2, X4 Methods

When Channel A and Channel B produce two quadrature signals, decoding can occur at **three resolution multipliers**:

| Decoding Mode | Counts per Pulse | Effective Resolution Multiplier |
|---|---|---|
| **X1** | 1 count per full cycle of A | $\times 1$ |
| **X2** | 1 count on each edge of A (rising + falling) | $\times 2$ |
| **X4** | 1 count on every edge of A AND B | $\times 4$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **X4 decoding is the most common in KTU and industry** because it quadruples the resolution without requiring a higher line-count disk.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for the Encoder topic. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every numerical problem.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pulses per revolution (PPR) | $N$ | pulses/rev | Disk line count |
| 2 | Counts per rev (X1) | $N_{X1} = N$ | counts/rev | Basic resolution |
| 3 | Counts per rev (X2) | $N_{X2} = 2N$ | counts/rev | Rising + falling of A |
| 4 | Counts per rev (X4) | $N_{X4} = 4N$ | counts/rev | All edges |
| 5 | Angular resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{N_{X4}}$ | degrees/pulse | After X4 decoding |
| 6 | Angular resolution (radians) | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{2\pi}{N_{X4}}$ | rad/pulse | SI form |
| 7 | Absolute positions ($n$-bit) | $P = 2^n$ | positions | For absolute encoder |
| 8 | Absolute resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360^\circ}{2^n}$ | degrees | Per LSB |
| 9 | Position from pulse count | $\theta(t) = \dfrac{C(t)}{N_{X4}} \times 360^\circ$ | degrees | $C(t)$ = counter value |
| 10 | Angular velocity (discrete) | $\omega = \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{\Delta t}$ | rad/s or RPM | |
| 11 | RPM from frequency | $n = \dfrac{60 \cdot f}{N}$ | RPM | $f$ = pulse frequency in Hz |
| 12 | Linear displacement | $x = \theta \cdot r$ | mm or m | $r$ = wheel radius (mobile robot) |
| 13 | Linear resolution | $x_{res} = \dfrac{\pi D}{N_{X4}}$ | mm/pulse | $D$ = wheel diameter |
| 14 | Gray to Binary conversion | $G_n \to B_n$, where $B_n = G_n$, $B_i = G_i \oplus B_{i+1}$ | bits | XOR chain |
| 15 | Direction logic (Quadrature) | CW: A leads B; CCW: B leads A | — | 90° phase shift |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Encoders are the **backbone of motion control** in:

1. **CNC Machines** — Position feedback for X, Y, Z axes (typically $0.1\,\mu m$ linear resolution).
2. **Industrial Robotic Arms** — Joint angle feedback for each servo motor (e.g., 6-DOF arm = 6 encoders).
3. **Mobile Robots / AGVs** — Wheel odometry for dead-reckoning localization.
4. **BLDC & Servo Motor Commutation** — Combined with Hall sensors for sinusoidal drive.
5. **Printers, Plotters, and 3D Printers** — Closed-loop stepper feedback.
6. **Aerospace** — Resolvers in harsh environments (high temperature, vibration).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Resolution of X4 Quadrature Decoder

**Problem Statement:** An optical incremental encoder has 500 lines on its code disk. Find the effective resolution in degrees, arc-minutes, and radians when operated in **X4 quadrature mode**.

### Given Data
- Line count $N = 500$ pulses per revolution.
- Decoding mode: **X4**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the effective count per revolution in X4 mode.**

In X4 quadrature decoding, every edge (rising and falling) of both Channel A and Channel B produces a count. There are **4 counts per line cycle**.

$$
N_{X4} = 4 \times N
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given value.**

$$
N_{X4} = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ counts/rev}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute angular resolution in degrees.**

The angular resolution is the smallest angle that can be measured per count. Since one full revolution equals $360^\circ$, divide it by the total counts:

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{360^\circ}{N_{X4}}
$$

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{360^\circ}{2000} = 0.18^\circ \text{ per count}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert degrees to arc-minutes.**

One degree contains 60 arc-minutes, so multiply by 60:

$$
\theta_{res} = 0.18^\circ \times 60 = 10.8 \text{ arc-minutes per count}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert degrees to radians.**

One full revolution equals $2\pi$ radians. So the resolution in radians is:

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi}{N_{X4}} = \frac{2\pi}{2000}
$$

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{6.2831853}{2000} \approx 0.003142 \text{ rad per count}
$$

### Final Result

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Angular resolution (degrees) | $\mathbf{0.18^\circ}$ / count |
| Angular resolution (arc-min) | $\mathbf{10.8'}$ / count |
| Angular resolution (radians) | $\mathbf{0.003142 \text{ rad}}$ / count |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RPM from Pulse Frequency

**Problem Statement:** A 1024-PPR encoder is connected to a motor. The pulse frequency measured at Channel A is $12\,288$ Hz. Calculate the motor RPM using **X4 decoding**.

### Given Data
- Line count $N = 1024$ PPR.
- Pulse frequency $f_A = 12\,288$ Hz.
- Decoding mode: **X4**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the pulses per revolution in X4 mode.**

$$
N_{X4} = 4 \times 1024 = 4096 \text{ counts/rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Recall the frequency-to-RPM relationship.**

Each channel produces $f_A$ cycles per second. The time for one full mechanical revolution is determined by how many of these cycles occur per revolution. In X1 mode, one channel cycle = one count, so:

$$
n = \frac{60 \cdot f}{N_{X4}}
$$

Where $n$ is in RPM, $f$ is in Hz, and $N_{X4}$ is total counts per revolution.

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
n = \frac{60 \times 12288}{4096}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the numerator.**

$$
60 \times 12288 = 737280
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by 4096.**

$$
n = \frac{737280}{4096} = 180 \text{ RPM}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{n = 180 \text{ RPM}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Linear Displacement in a Mobile Robot

**Problem Statement:** A differential-drive mobile robot has wheels of diameter $D = 200$ mm. Each wheel is fitted with a 360-PPR incremental encoder operating in **X2 mode**. If the counter records $7200$ counts during a straight-line motion, calculate the **distance traveled** by the robot.

### Given Data
- Wheel diameter $D = 200$ mm.
- Encoder line count $N = 360$ PPR.
- Decoding mode: **X2**.
- Total count $C = 7200$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the counts per revolution in X2 mode.**

$$
N_{X2} = 2 \times N = 2 \times 360 = 720 \text{ counts/rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of revolutions traveled.**

The wheel rotated $C / N_{X2}$ revolutions during the motion:

$$
\text{rev} = \frac{C}{N_{X2}} = \frac{7200}{720} = 10 \text{ revolutions}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linear distance per revolution.**

The circumference of the wheel equals $\pi D$, so each revolution moves the robot by:

$$
L_{1} = \pi \cdot D = \pi \times 200 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total linear distance.**

$$
L = \text{rev} \times L_1 = 10 \times \pi \times 200 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L = 2000\pi \text{ mm} = 2000 \times 3.14159 = 6283.18 \text{ mm}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{L \approx 6.283 \text{ meters}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Velocity from Pulse Train (Discrete-Time Differentiator)

**Problem Statement:** A robot joint encoder produces pulses sampled every $\Delta t = 10$ ms. If the count changes from $C_1 = 1200$ to $C_2 = 1248$ counts (X4 mode, $N = 500$ PPR), find the **joint angular velocity** in rad/s.

### Given Data
- Sampling interval $\Delta t = 10 \text{ ms} = 0.01 \text{ s}$.
- Initial count $C_1 = 1200$, final count $C_2 = 1248$.
- Line count $N = 500$ PPR, X4 mode.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute effective counts per revolution.**

$$
N_{X4} = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ counts/rev}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the change in counts.**

$$
\Delta C = C_2 - C_1 = 1248 - 1200 = 48 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the change in angle in revolutions.**

$$
\Delta \text{rev} = \frac{\Delta C}{N_{X4}} = \frac{48}{2000} = 0.024 \text{ rev}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert revolutions to radians.**
