One revolution $= 2\pi$ radians:

$$
\Delta \theta = 0.024 \times 2\pi = 0.048\pi \text{ rad} \approx 0.1508 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute angular velocity.**

$$
\omega = \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.1508}{0.01}
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega \approx 15.08 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Gray Code to Binary Conversion (4-bit)

**Problem Statement:** A 4-bit absolute encoder returns Gray code $G_3 G_2 G_1 G_0 = 1010$. Convert it to straight binary.

### Given Data
- Gray code word: $G = 1010_2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the Gray-to-Binary rule.**

The MSB of binary equals the MSB of Gray:

$$
B_3 = G_3 = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each subsequent binary bit by XORing the current Gray bit with the previous binary bit.**

$$
B_2 = G_2 \oplus B_3 = 0 \oplus 1 = 1
$$

$$
B_1 = G_1 \oplus B_2 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

$$
B_0 = G_0 \oplus B_1 = 0 \oplus 0 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Write the result.**

$$
\boxed{B = 1100_2 = 12_{10}}
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for KTU Problems

| Given in Question | Apply Formula | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Line count $N$, mode X1 | $\theta = 360/N$ | Resolution (deg) |
| Line count $N$, mode X4 | $\theta = 90/N$ | Resolution (deg) |
| Frequency $f$, line count $N$ | $n = 60f/N$ | RPM (X1 mode) |
| Frequency $f$, line count $N$, X4 | $n = 60f/(4N)$ | RPM (X4 mode) |
| Counts $C$, line count $N$, wheel $D$ | $x = (C \cdot \pi D)/(4N)$ | Linear distance |
| Gray code word | XOR chain from MSB | Binary equivalent |
| $n$-bit absolute | $2^n$ positions | Resolution $= 360/2^n$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Optical Incremental Encoder

The diagram below illustrates the **signal chain** from physical rotation to processed digital output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["LED Light Source"]
    nodeB["Code Disk N Lines"]
    nodeC["Photodetector A"]
    nodeD["Photodetector B"]
    nodeE["Photodetector Z Index"]
    nodeF["Signal Conditioning Amplifier"]
    nodeG["Comparator and Schmitt Trigger"]
    nodeH["Quadrature Decoder Logic"]
    nodeI["Counter Register"]
    nodeJ["Microcontroller or PLC"]
    nodeK["Motor Shaft Input"]
    nodeL["Direction Detection Module"]

    nodeK --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeL --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
```

**Interpretation:** The LED illuminates the code disk mounted on the motor shaft. The photodetectors receive light through the slots, producing three raw signals (A, B, Z). Signal conditioning amplifies and shapes these into clean TTL square waves. The quadrature decoder then resolves direction and increments/decrements the counter, which is read by the controller.

---

## 4.2 Quadrature Signal Waveform — State Transition Diagram

This diagram shows the **four valid states** of a quadrature encoder and the **direction logic** that determines CW vs. CCW rotation.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State01
    State01: State 01 A 0 B 0
    State01 --> State11: CW rotation rising edge of B
    State01 --> State00: CCW rotation falling edge of A
    State11: State 11 A 1 B 0
    State11 --> State10: CW rising edge of A
    State11 --> State01: CCW falling edge of B
    State10: State 10 A 1 B 1
    State10 --> State00: CW falling edge of B
    State10 --> State11: CCW rising edge of A
    State00: State 00 A 0 B 1
    State00 --> State01: CW rising edge of A
    State00 --> State10: CCW falling edge of B
```

**Interpretation:** Only valid Gray-code-like state transitions are permitted (no $01 \to 10$ direct jump). Reading the state sequence in forward order indicates CW rotation; reverse order indicates CCW. The index pulse Z resets the counter to zero exactly once per revolution.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Position Control with Encoder Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeCmd["Setpoint Position Command Theta Ref"]
    nodeComp["Comparator Subtractor"]
    nodeErr["Error Signal e t"]
    nodePID["PID Controller"]
    nodeDrv["Power Amplifier Motor Driver"]
    nodeMot["DC Servo Motor or BLDC"]
    nodeLoad["Robot Joint or Wheel Load"]
    nodeEnc["Encoder on Shaft"]
    nodeFb["Position Feedback Theta Actual"]
    nodeVel["Velocity Calculator Differentiator"]

    nodeCmd --> nodeComp
    nodeFb --> nodeComp
    nodeComp --> nodeErr
    nodeErr --> nodePID
    nodePID --> nodeDrv
    nodeDrv --> nodeMot
    nodeMot --> nodeLoad
    nodeMot --> nodeEnc
    nodeEnc --> nodeFb
    nodeEnc --> nodeVel
    nodeFb --> nodeComp
```

**Interpretation:** This is the **canonical servo loop**. The encoder closes the loop by feeding back the actual joint angle $\theta_{actual}$, which is subtracted from the desired $\theta_{ref}$. The error $e(t)$ drives the PID controller, which commands the amplifier. The encoder also provides velocity via discrete-time differentiation for damping terms.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Tree — Incremental vs. Absolute Encoders

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["Position Sensors"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeInc["Incremental Encoder"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeAbs["Absolute Encoder"]
    nodeInc --> nodeInc1["Reports Relative Position Only"]
    nodeInc --> nodeInc2["Needs Homing on Power Up"]
    nodeInc --> nodeInc3["Lower Cost Simpler Interface"]
    nodeInc --> nodeInc4["Channels A B Z"]
    nodeAbs --> nodeAbs1["Reports True Absolute Position"]
    nodeAbs --> nodeAbs2["Retains Position on Power Loss"]
    nodeAbs --> nodeAbs3["Higher Cost Multi Track Disk"]
    nodeAbs --> nodeAbs4["Output Binary Gray BCD"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short-Answer Conceptual
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**Q: Differentiate between incremental and absolute encoders with one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Position type | Relative (from reference) | True absolute |
| Output | Pulse train (A, B, Z) | Unique digital code per angle |
| Power-loss behavior | Loses position | Retains position |
| Disk complexity | Single track | Multi-track (n-bit) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Example | Wheel odometry on AGV | Robotic arm joint home position |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for each correct row of contrast, plus a valid example)*

---

### Question 2: Short-Answer Conceptual
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Q: Explain the term "quadrature decoding" and list its three modes with the effective count multiplier.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Quadrature decoding** is the method of extracting directional and positional information from two square-wave signals (Channel A and Channel B) that are 90° out of phase. The phase relationship (whether A leads B or B leads A) determines the **direction of rotation**.

The three modes are:
- **X1 mode** — counts only on rising edge of A. Multiplier: $\times 1$.
- **X2 mode** — counts on rising and falling edges of A. Multiplier: $\times 2$.
- **X4 mode** — counts on every edge of both A and B. Multiplier: $\times 4$.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for direction logic, 1 for listing all three modes with multipliers)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of an **optical incremental encoder**. Discuss the role of the **index pulse (Z)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 2000-PPR optical encoder is operated in **X4 quadrature mode** and is mounted on the wheel of a mobile robot. The wheel diameter is $150$ mm. If the encoder generates a pulse train of frequency $f = 8$ kHz:
- (i) Determine the **angular resolution** in degrees and radians per count.
- (ii) Calculate the **wheel RPM**.
- (iii) Compute the **linear velocity** of the robot in m/s. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer for Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):** Marks awarded: 2 marks.

**Explanation (5 marks):**

1. **LED** (1 mark): Emits a continuous, focused infrared/red light beam.
2. **Code Disk** (1 mark): A glass/plastic disk with $N$ equally spaced radial slots rotates with the shaft. Slots alternate light/dark patterns.
3. **Photodetectors** (1 mark): Two photodiodes receive the modulated light, generating sinusoidal voltages. A third detector is on a single-slot index track.
4. **Signal Conditioning** (1 mark): Comparators and Schmitt triggers convert the analog signals into clean TTL square waves (Channels A, B, Z).
5. **Index Pulse Z — Role** (1 mark): The Z pulse occurs **exactly once per revolution**. It is used by the controller to:
   - Reset the position counter to zero (homing).
   - Synchronize absolute and incremental readings.
   - Detect a complete revolution for one-time-per-rev events (e.g., trigger sampling).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $N = 2000$ PPR, mode X4, $D = 150$ mm, $f = 8$ kHz $= 8000$ Hz.

**(i) Angular Resolution (3 marks):**

Counts per revolution in X4 mode:

$$
N_{X4} = 4N = 4 \times 2000 = 8000 \text{ counts/rev}
$$

Resolution in degrees:

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{360^\circ}{8000} = 0.045^\circ \text{ per count}
$$

Resolution in radians:

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi}{8000} = 7.854 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad per count}
$$

**(ii) Wheel RPM (2 marks):**

$$
n = \frac{60 f}{N_{X4}} = \frac{60 \times 8000}{8000} = 60 \text{ RPM}
$$

**(iii) Linear Velocity (2 marks):**

Wheel circumference:

$$
C = \pi D = \pi \times 0.150 = 0.4712 \text{ m/rev}
$$

Linear velocity:

$$
v = \frac{C \times n}{60} = \frac{0.4712 \times 60}{60} = 0.4712 \text{ m/s}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **absolute encoder** using Gray code. Why is Gray code preferred over straight binary? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A robotic manipulator joint uses an **8-bit absolute encoder**.
- (i) How many **distinct positions** can it resolve?
- (ii) What is its **angular resolution** in degrees and arc-seconds?
- (iii) If the shaft rotates through $3.5$ full revolutions, what is the **decimal output** (using Gray code if MSB starts at 0)? *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Answer for Question B

#### Part (a) — Absolute Encoder with Gray Code (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Multi-track code disk with $n$ concentric rings, each ring corresponding to one bit of position.
- Each track has a unique pattern of transparent/opaque segments.
- $n$ photodetectors, one per track, read simultaneously.
- Output is an $n$-bit parallel word.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Each shaft angle aligns with a unique combination of light/dark on the tracks.
- The combination is decoded into a binary/Gray word that is the absolute position.

**Why Gray Code is Preferred (2 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In straight binary, multiple bits can change simultaneously between adjacent positions (e.g., $0111 \to 1000$). Due to manufacturing tolerances and electronic skew, intermediate invalid codes can be momentarily produced, causing **transition errors**. In Gray code, only **one bit changes** between adjacent positions, making it inherently **error-free** for single-step transitions.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Distinct Positions (2 marks):**

$$
P = 2^8 = 256 \text{ positions}
$$

**(ii) Angular Resolution (2 marks):**

In degrees:

$$
\theta_{res} = \frac{360^\circ}{256} = 1.40625^\circ
$$

In arc-seconds:

$$
\theta_{res} = 1.40625^\circ \times 3600'' = 5062.5'' \approx 1^\circ 24' 22.5''
$$

**(iii) Decimal Output (3 marks):**

For $3.5$ revolutions starting from MSB $= 0$, the encoder has completed $3$ full cycles of 256 positions and is now at the **128th position** of the 4th cycle.

Position value:

$$
\text{position} = 3 \times 256 + 128 = 896
$$

In 8-bit binary: $896 = 1110000000_2$. Convert to Gray code (XOR with shifted self):

- Binary:  $1110000000$
- Shift right: $0111000000$
- XOR: $\mathbf{1001000000_G}$

Decimal of Gray word = 576 (for verification via Gray-to-binary if needed).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the X4 multiplier** — KTU frequently tests the difference between PPR (disk line count) and counts/rev. Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Wrong frequency-to-RPM formula** — The formula is $n = 60f/N$. Some students incorrectly use $n = 60f/(4N)$ when not in X4 mode. Always check which decoding mode is specified.
> 3. **Confusing radius and diameter** in linear-distance problems. The circumference is $\pi D$ (diameter), NOT $\pi r$ (radius).
> 4. **Gray-to-binary conversion errors** — Always start from the MSB: $B_n = G_n$, then XOR downward. A single XOR slip cascades to all lower bits.
> 5. **Ignoring the Index Pulse** in descriptive answers — KTU examiners specifically look for the role of the Z pulse. Omitting it loses a full mark.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency** — Always convert mm to m before reporting linear velocity in m/s.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Encoder** = electromechanical sensor for position/velocity feedback.
- [ ] **Two main types:** Incremental (relative, A/B/Z) and Absolute (unique code per angle).
- [ ] **Optical encoder components:** LED, code disk, photodetector, signal conditioner.
- [ ] **Index pulse Z:** one pulse per revolution; used for homing/reset.
- [ ] **Quadrature decoding modes:** X1 (×1), X2 (×2), X4 (×4) — X4 is industry standard.
- [ ] **PPR (N)** is the disk line count, **NOT** the effective count.
- [ ] **Counts/rev in X4** = $4N$.
- [ ] **Angular resolution** $= 360^\circ / N_{eff}$ or $2\pi / N_{eff}$ rad.
- [ ] **RPM formula:** $n = 60 f / N_{eff}$.
- [ ] **Linear distance:** $L = (C_{count} \cdot \pi D) / N_{eff}$.
- [ ] **Gray code** is used in absolute encoders because only 1 bit changes per step.
- [ ] **Direction detection:** A leads B → CW; B leads A → CCW.
- [ ] **Velocity derivation:** $\omega = \Delta\theta / \Delta t$ via discrete-time differentiation of count.
- [ ] **Resolver** is a rugged, brushless electromagnetic alternative used in harsh environments.
- [ ] **KTU key values:** Industrial TTL = **5 V**, Operating temp = **$-40^\circ$C to $+85^\circ$C**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vision Sensors. Classification of Robots: Work Envelope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Vision Sensors. Classification of Robots: Work Envelope # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Vision Sensors in Robotics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Vision Sensor** (also called a Machine Vision System or Robotic Vision System) is a non-contact, electro-optical sensing device that captures, digitizes, and processes visual information from a robot's operating environment, enabling the robot to perform tasks such as object recognition, localization, inspection, and guidance. A vision sensor transforms **light** (photons) into **electrical signals** that are then digitized and processed using image processing algorithms. It essentially acts as the *eyes* of a robot. ### Conceptual Analogy 🧠 Think of a vision sensor like a **digital camera with a brain**. A normal camera just records pictures. A vision sensor not only captures the image but also **understands** it — it can find edges, measure distances, recognize objects, and guide the robot's hand to pick up the right item. It's the difference between a tourist taking random photos and a quality inspector who knows exactly what defects to look for. ### Classification of Vision Sensors | Type | Description | Depth Perception | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | **1-D Vision** | Analyzes a single line of pixels | No | Web inspection (paper, cloth) | | **2-D Vision** | Captures a flat image (x, y) | No (monocular) | Pick-and-place, sorting | | **3-D Vision** | Captures spatial coordinates (x, y, z) | Yes (stereo/laser) | Bin picking, welding seam tracking | | **Smart/Intelligent Camera** | Camera + on-board processor | Varies | Inline inspection, barcode reading | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The 2-D and 3-D vision systems are the most relevant for industrial robotics. Students must understand the difference between **monocular** (single camera) and **stereo** (two cameras) depth recovery. ### Key Technical Specifications (Must Know) - **Resolution:** Measured in **pixels** (e.g., 640 × 480 VGA, 1920 × 1080 HD). - **Field of View (FOV):** Angular or spatial extent of the scene captured. - **Frame Rate:** Number of images captured per second (**fps**). - **Pixel Depth:** Number of bits per pixel (typically **8-bit** grayscale = 256 levels). - **Focal Length (f):** Distance between lens and image sensor, measured in **mm**. - **Working Distance:** Distance from lens to object under inspection. --- ## 1.2 Classification of Robots Based on Work Envelope > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** The **Work Envelope** (also called **Workspace** or **Reach Envelope**) of a robot is the three-dimensional region in space that can be reached by the robot's end-effector (wrist or tool mounting plate), considering all physical joint limits and mechanical constraints. ### Conceptual Analogy 🤖 Imagine drawing in the air with your fingertip while keeping your elbow fixed. The cloud of points your finger can trace forms a **sphere** (since your arm is a fixed-length link). Now add a shoulder joint, elbow joint, and wrist — the combined reachable region becomes a much more complex 3-D shape. That complex 3-D shape is the **work envelope** of a robot arm. ### Why is Work Envelope Important? > [!NOTE] > The work envelope directly determines: > 1. **Cell Layout** — where to place conveyors, fixtures, and safety fencing. > 2. **Task Suitability** — which robot fits which application (e.g., palletizing vs. assembly). > 3. **Reachability Analysis** — whether all target points are kinematically accessible. > 4. **Dead Zones / Singularities** — regions the robot simply cannot reach or where it loses a DOF. The four classical work envelope geometries classified by **robot kinematic structure** are: | # | Robot Type | Joint Configuration | Work Envelope Shape | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Cartesian (Gantry)** | 3 Prismatic (L, L, L) | Rectangular cuboid box | | 2 | **Cylindrical** | 1 Revolute + 2 Prismatic (R, L, L) | Hollow cylinder | | 3 | **Spherical (Polar)** | 2 Revolute + 1 Prismatic (R, R, L) | Spherical sector | | 4 | **Articulated (Anthropomorphic)** | 3 Revolute (R, R, R) | Irregular sphere-like lobe | | 5 | **SCARA** | 2 Revolute + 1 Prismatic (R, R, L) | Horizontal disc-like volume | | 6 | **Parallel (Delta)** | Multiple parallel linkages | Dome-shaped volume | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Vision Sensor — Functional Components A robotic vision system consists of **five core building blocks**: 1. **Illumination Source** — Provides controlled, consistent lighting (LED, halogen, laser, structured light). Bad lighting = bad data. 2. **Imaging Lens** — Focuses light onto the sensor. Determines FOV and magnification. 3. **Image Sensor** — Converts light to electrical signals. Two technologies: - **CCD (Charge-Coupled Device):** High quality, low noise, used in metrology. - **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor):** Faster, cheaper, lower power, used in smart cameras. 4. **Frame Grabber / Digitizer** — Converts analog video signal to digital pixel array. 5. **Image Processing Unit** — Runs algorithms (edge detection, pattern matching, blob analysis, OCR). ### The Pinhole Camera Model (Foundation of Vision Math) > [!IMPORTANT] > The simplest mathematical model of a vision sensor is the **pinhole camera**. It uses perspective projection to map a 3-D world point $(X, Y, Z)$ to a 2-D image point $(u, v)$. ### Lens Equation (Thin Lens Formula) The relationship between object distance, image distance, and focal length: $$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i}$$ Where: - $f$ = focal length of the lens (**mm**) - $d_o$ = object distance from lens (**mm**) - $d_i$ = image distance from lens (**mm**) ### Field of View (FOV) Equation For a sensor of width $W$ and height $H$: $$\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}} = 2 \cdot d_o \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\alpha_h}{2}\right)$$ Where $\alpha_h$ is the **horizontal angular FOV** given by: $$\alpha_h = 2 \cdot \arctan\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** In a pick-and-place cell, a vision sensor with a smaller FOV gives higher resolution per pixel (better measurement accuracy) but covers less area — so the camera must be placed closer to the target. --- ## 2.2 Work Envelope — Kinematic Foundations The work envelope is mathematically defined by the **forward kinematics** mapping: $$\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q}), \quad \mathbf{q} \in \mathcal{Q} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$$ Where: - $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, z)$ is the end-effector position in Cartesian space. - $\mathbf{q} = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n)$ is the joint variable vector. - $\mathcal{Q}$ is the **joint space**, bounded by joint limits $q_{i,\min} \le q_i \le q_{i,\max}$. - The set of all reachable $\mathbf{x}$ forms the **dexterous** or **reachable workspace**. ### Envelope Sub-Categories | Workspace Type | Definition | |---|---| | **Reachable Workspace** | All points the end-effector can reach in **any orientation**. | | **Dexterous Workspace** | Points reachable in **all possible orientations** (subset of reachable). | | **Total Workspace** | Union of reachable + dexterous (used loosely in textbooks). | > [!NOTE] > For a 6-DOF robot, the dexterous workspace is usually much smaller than the reachable workspace — typically a small region near the geometric center. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Vision + Work Envelope) | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Thin lens equation | $\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i}$ | $f, d_o, d_i$ in **mm** | | 2 | Magnification | $M = -\frac{d_i}{d_o} = \frac{h_i}{h_o}$ | Sign: negative = inverted image | | 3 | Horizontal FOV | $\alpha_h = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right)$ | $W$ = sensor width, $f$ = focal length | | 4 | Image size from object | $h_i = f \cdot \frac{h_o}{d_o}$ | $h_o$ = object height | | 5 | Pixel size | $p = \frac{W}{N_{\text{pixels}}}$ | $p$ in **µm/pixel** | | 6 | Ground Sampling Distance (GSD) | $\text{GSD} = \frac{p \cdot d_o}{f}$ | Spatial resolution at object plane | | 7 | Stereo depth (baseline $b$, disparity $d$) | $Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d}$ | $f, b, d$ in same units | | 8 | Cartesian envelope volume | $V = 2L_x \cdot 2L_y \cdot 2L_z$ | $L_x, L_y, L_z$ = stroke lengths | | 9 | Cylindrical envelope | $V = \pi r_{\max}^2 \cdot L_z$ (excluding inner cylinder) | $r_{\max}$ = max radial reach | | 10 | Spherical envelope | $V = \frac{2}{3} \pi r^3$ (quarter sphere for 2R+L) | $r$ = max reach | | 11 | Articulated reach | $r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$ | Sum of link lengths | | 12 | Articulated reach (min) | $r_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert + L_3$ | Inner unreachable sphere | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE (for KTU Board Exam scripts):** When writing joint limits or absolute values in your answer sheet, use $\vert x \vert$ notation with vertical bars — but **in markdown tables above I used $\vert$ to avoid table-breaking pipes**. In your exam answer, write cleanly as $|x|$ or $\lvert x \rvert$. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Domain | Application | |---|---| | **Automotive Welding** | Vision-guided seam tracking on curved body panels (3-D laser line sensors) | | **Pharmaceutical Packaging** | 2-D vision verifies tablet count, label presence, and expiry date | | **E-commerce Warehousing** | 3-D bin-picking robots pick from unsorted totes using stereo vision | | **PCB Inspection** | High-resolution 2-D vision detects solder defects, missing components | | **Agricultural Robotics** | Multi-spectral vision for fruit ripeness and disease detection | | **Work Envelope Use** | Cell designers use envelope CAD overlays to validate robot selection **before procurement** | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Field of View from First Principles We derive the horizontal angular field of view using **basic similar triangles** from the pinhole geometry. **Step 1 — Setup the geometry.** Place the pinhole at the origin. The image plane lies at distance $f$ behind the pinhole. A sensor of width $W$ spans from $-W/2$ to $+W/2$ along the horizontal axis on the image plane. **Step 2 — Identify the right triangle.** A ray from the optical center to the edge of the sensor makes an angle $\alpha_h / 2$ with the optical axis. The opposite side is $W/2$ and the adjacent side is $f$. **Step 3 — Apply the tangent ratio.** $$\tan\left(\frac{\alpha_h}{2}\right) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{W/2}{f}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the half-angle.** $$\frac{\alpha_h}{2} = \arctan\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Multiply both sides by 2.** $$\alpha_h = 2 \arctan\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$ **Step 6 — Convert angular FOV to linear FOV at object distance $d_o$.** Using the same triangle logic, the linear horizontal extent on the object plane is: $$\begin{aligned} \text{FOV}_{\text{linear}} &= 2 \cdot d_o \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\alpha_h}{2}\right) \\ &= 2 \cdot d_o \cdot \tan\left[\arctan\left(\frac{W}{2f}\right)\right] \\ &= 2 \cdot d_o \cdot \frac{W}{2f} \\ &= \frac{d_o \cdot W}{f} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The linear FOV is **directly proportional** to object distance and **inversely proportional** to focal length. Halving the focal length doubles the FOV (wider view, smaller objects). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Stereo Vision Depth Recovery Stereo vision uses two cameras separated by a horizontal distance $b$ (the **baseline**). The depth $Z$ of a world point is recovered from the **disparity** $d$. **Step 1 — Define disparity.** Disparity is the pixel difference of the same world point in the left and right images: $$d = u_L - u_R$$ **Step 2 — Set up similar triangles in the stereo rig.** For the left camera: $$u_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$ For the right camera: $$u_R = f \cdot \frac{X - b}{Z}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the disparity.** $$\begin{aligned} d &= u_L - u_R = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - b}{Z} \\ d &= f \cdot \frac{X - (X - b)}{Z} \\ d &= \frac{f \cdot b}{Z} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for depth $Z$.** $$Z = \frac{f \cdot b}{d} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** **Smaller disparity** → **larger depth** (farther object). Disparity approaches infinity for points at the focal plane and zero for infinitely far points. This is why stereo vision **fails** for very distant objects (the disparity becomes sub-pixel). --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Work Envelope Geometry for Each Robot Type We derive the **reachable workspace equations** for the four classical robot architectures. ### 3.3.1 Cartesian Robot (PPP) **Configuration:** Three mutually orthogonal prismatic joints with strokes $L_x, L_y, L_z$ along the $X, Y, Z$ axes. **Forward Kinematics:** $$x = q_1, \quad y = q_2, \quad z = q_3$$ **Joint Limits:** $$0 \le q_1 \le L_x, \quad 0 \le q_2 \le L_y, \quad 0 \le q_3 \le L_z$$ **Work Envelope:** $$V_{\text{Cartesian}} = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z \quad \text{(rectangular cuboid)} \quad \blacksquare$$ ### 3.3.2 Cylindrical Robot (RPP) **Configuration:** One revolute joint $\theta_1$ about $Z$, then two prismatic joints (radial $r$, vertical $z$). **Forward Kinematics:** $$x = r \cos\theta_1, \quad y = r \sin\theta_1, \quad z = z$$ **Joint Limits:** $$0 \le \theta_1 \le 360°, \quad r_{\min} \le r \le r_{\max}, \quad 0 \le z \le L_z$$ **Work Envelope Volume (full revolution $\theta_1 = 360°$):** $$V_{\text{Cyl}} = \pi \left(r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2\right) \cdot L_z \quad \blacksquare$$ > Usually $r_{\min} = 0$, simplifying to $V = \pi r_{\max}^2 L_z$. ### 3.3.3 Spherical / Polar Robot (RRP) **Configuration:** Revolute about $Z$ (azimuth $\theta_1$), revolute about horizontal axis (elevation $\theta_2$), prismatic (radial $r$). **Forward Kinematics:** $$x = r \cos\theta_2 \cos\theta_1, \quad y = r \cos\theta_2 \sin\theta_1, \quad z = r \sin\theta_2$$ **Joint Limits:** $$0 \le \theta_1 \le 360°, \quad -\alpha \le \theta_2 \le \beta, \quad 0 \le r \le r_{\max}$$ For full $\theta_1$ sweep, the envelope is a **hollow spherical sector**. **Work Envelope Volume:** $$V_{\text{Sph}} = \frac{2}{3} \pi r_{\max}^3 \left(1 - \cos\theta_{2,\max}\right) \quad \text{(for symmetric elevation range)} \quad \blacksquare$$ ### 3.3.4 Articulated / Anthropomorphic Robot (RRR) **Configuration:** Three revolute joints — shoulder yaw, shoulder pitch, elbow pitch — with link lengths $L_1$ (shoulder-to-elbow) and $L_2$ (elbow-to-wrist). **Planar Reach Analysis (2-D simplification):** With the base at origin and the arm in the $X$-$Y$ plane, the wrist position is: $$r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2}$$ Where $\theta_2$ is the elbow angle. **Joint Limits:** $\theta_2 \in [\theta_{2,\min}, \theta_{2,\max}]$, typically $[-150°, +150°]$. **Maximum Reach:** $$r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 \quad \text{(fully extended arm)} \quad \blacksquare$$ **Minimum Reach (Inner Dead Zone):** $$r_{\min} = \lvert L_1 - L_2 \rvert \quad \text{(arm fully folded)} \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!NOTE] > **Dead Zone:** All points with $r < r_{\min}$ from the base **cannot be reached** by the wrist, no matter how the joints are configured. This is a critical "blind spot" in cell layout. ### 3.3.5 SCARA Robot (RRP) **Configuration:** Two parallel revolute joints (shoulder + elbow) for $X$-$Y$ motion, one prismatic joint for $Z$. **Work Envelope:** - **In $X$-$Y$ Plane:** Same as a 2-D articulated arm — an annular region between $r_{\min}$ and $r_{\max}$. - **In $Z$ Direction:** Cylindrical extrusion of stroke length $L_z$. $$V_{\text{SCARA}} = \pi \left(r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2\right) \cdot L_z \quad \blacksquare$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **SCARA special feature:** Although the planar shape resembles an articulated robot, the *Z-axis is decoupled* (purely vertical prismatic). This makes SCARA **extremely fast and rigid** for vertical assembly tasks (e.g., PCB screw-driving). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Robot by Envelope | Task Profile | Best Robot Type | Envelope Justification | |---|---|---| | Pick from conveyor, place in box at same height | Cartesian | Rectangular box matches rectangular layout | | Loading/unloading a cylindrical lathe | Cylindrical | Lathe has a cylindrical access region | | Spray painting a car body interior | Spherical / Articulated | Curved shell surfaces need spherical reach | | Tight-tolerance PCB assembly | SCARA | Flat disc workspace + vertical Z motion | | Heavy palletizing of tall stacks | Articulated (6-DOF) | Large irregular lobe with overhead reach | | High-speed food packaging (light objects) | Delta (parallel) | Dome over conveyor, very fast | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Robotic Vision System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ILLUMINATION SOURCE] --> B[SCENE / WORKPIECE] B --> C[IMAGING LENS] C --> D[IMAGE SENSOR CCD OR CMOS] D --> E[FRAME GRABBER AND A/D CONVERTER] E --> F[IMAGE PROCESSING UNIT] F --> G{DECISION ALGORITHM} G -->|Pass| H[ROBOT CONTROLLER COMMAND] G -->|Fail| I[REJECT SIGNAL] H --> J[ROBOT ACTUATOR] I --> K[DIVERTER TO REJECT BIN] F --> L[DISPLAY AND HMI FOR OPERATOR] classDef sensor fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#003366 classDef process fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#155724 classDef output fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#856404 classDef reject fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#721c24 class A,C,D sensor class E,F,G process class H,J,L output class I,K reject ``` > **Figure 4.1 — Block Diagram of a Robotic Vision Inspection Cell** showing the complete signal flow from illumination to actuator command, with a feedback branch to the HMI and a reject path for failed parts. --- ## 4.2 Work Envelope Geometries — Side-by-Side Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph ENVELOPES["WORK ENVELOPE SHAPES BY ROBOT TYPE"] direction LR S1["CARTESIAN PPP
Rectangular Cuboid
Vx = Lx . Ly . Lz"] S2["CYLINDRICAL RPP
Hollow Cylinder
Vy = pi . rmax^2 . Lz"] S3["SPHERICAL RRP
Spherical Sector
Vs = 2/3 . pi . rmax^3"] S4["ARTICULATED RRR
Irregular Spherical Lobe
rmax = L1 + L2 + L3"] S5["SCARA RRP
Disc with Vertical Stroke
Vs = pi . rmax^2 . Lz"] end T1["Assembly,
Pick-Place"] -.-> S1 T2["Lathe Tending,
Coating"] -.-> S2 T3["Welding,
Spray Paint"] -.-> S3 T4["Palletizing,
Welding"] -.-> S4 T5["PCB Assembly,
Screwdriving"] -.-> S5 classDef shape fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd,color:#01579b classDef task fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#ff8f00,color:#e65100 class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 shape class T1,T2,T3,T4,T5 task ``` > **Figure 4.2 — Mapping of Robot Architectures to Typical Tasks** using the characteristic 3-D envelope shape of each kinematic configuration. --- ## 4.3 Vision-Guided Robotic Pick-and-Place Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant WObj as WORKPIECE on CONVEYOR participant Cam as VISION SENSOR 2D participant IPC as IMAGE PROCESSING COMPUTER participant RC as ROBOT CONTROLLER participant Rob as ROBOT ARM Note over WObj,Rob: CYCLE START WObj->>Cam: Object enters FOV Cam->>Cam: Trigger / capture image Cam->>IPC: Send digital image data IPC->>IPC: Threshold, Edge Detect, Blob Analysis IPC->>IPC: Calculate X, Y, Angle of Object IPC->>RC: Transmit object pose Xc Yc Theta RC->>RC: Transform to robot base frame RC->>RC: Inverse Kinematics Solver RC->>Rob: Joint commands q1 q2 q3 q4 q5 q6 Rob->>WObj: Move to pick position Rob->>Rob: Gripper close, vacuum ON Rob->>Rob: Move to place position Rob->>Rob: Gripper open, vacuum OFF Note over WObj,Rob: CYCLE END -> Next Part ``` > **Figure 4.3 — Sequence Diagram of Vision-Guided Pick-and-Place Operation** showing the chronological handshakes between the vision sensor, image processor, robot controller, and the physical arm. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (2-Mark & 3-Mark Conceptual) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]** **List any three advantages of using a vision sensor in a robotic work cell. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Non-contact measurement** — Eliminates mechanical wear and contamination of delicate parts. 2. **High flexibility** — A single vision system can inspect/identify many part types with software change, reducing changeover time. 3. **Improved quality & repeatability** — Provides consistent inspection 24/7, eliminating human fatigue errors. 4. *(Bonus point)* Provides **rich 2-D/3-D data** that simple proximity sensors cannot. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **Differentiate between Cartesian and Cylindrical work envelopes. (3 Marks)** | Feature | Cartesian | Cylindrical | |---|---|---| | **Joint Type** | 3 Prismatic (L, L, L) | 1 Revolute + 2 Prismatic (R, L, L) | | **Envelope Shape** | Rectangular cuboid | Hollow cylinder | | **Reach Direction** | Linear along 3 axes | Rotation + linear radial + vertical | | **Typical Use** | Pick-and-place, CNC loading | Lathe tending, die casting | | **Reachability** | All corners are reachable | Inner cylinder is a **dead zone** | --- ## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 + 7 marks. You are required to answer **ONE full question** (either Q.A or Q.B). --- ### ✅ CHOICE A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3, Set A]** **Q.A.** *(a) Explain the functional block diagram of a robotic vision system with a neat sketch. List the differences between CCD and CMOS image sensors. *(7 Marks)* **(b) A machine vision camera has a sensor of width 8.8 mm and focal length 12 mm. It is used to inspect a workpiece placed 300 mm from the lens. Calculate (i) the horizontal field of view, (ii) the angular FOV, and (iii) the magnification if the image distance is 12.5 mm. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Solution for Q.A(a): A robotic vision system consists of **five sequential blocks**: 1. **Illumination** — LED ring light, dome light, or laser line projector. 2. **Imaging Optics** — Lens with adjustable aperture and focus. 3. **Image Sensor** — CCD or CMOS array. 4. **Frame Grabber** — A/D conversion, pixel digitization. 5. **Image Processing Unit** — Runs edge detection, thresholding, pattern matching, OCR. **CCD vs CMOS Comparison:** | Parameter | CCD | CMOS | |---|---|---| | Signal Readout | Serial (bucket-brigade) | Per-pixel amplifier (parallel) | | Noise Level | **Low** | Higher (more on-chip circuitry) | | Power Consumption | **High** | **Low** (10× less) | | Speed | Slower | **Faster** | | Cost | **High** | **Low** | | Sensitivity | **Higher** | Lower | | Application | Metrology, scientific | Smart cameras, mobile, IoT | #### Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Given:** - Sensor width $W = 8.8$ mm - Focal length $f = 12$ mm - Object distance $d_o = 300$ mm - Image distance $d_i = 12.5$ mm **Step 1 — Calculate angular FOV using similar triangles.** $$\begin{aligned} \tan\left(\frac{\alpha_h}{2}\right) &= \frac{W/2}{f} = \frac{8.8/2}{12} = \frac{4.4}{12} \\ \tan\left(\frac{\alpha_h}{2}\right) &= 0.3667 \\ \frac{\alpha_h}{2} &= \arctan(0.3667) = 20.13° \\ \alpha_h &= 2 \times 20.13° = 40.26° \end{aligned}$$ > **Angular FOV $\alpha_h \approx 40.26°$** **Step 2 — Calculate linear (horizontal) FOV at object plane.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{FOV}_{\text{linear}} &= 2 \cdot d_o \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\alpha_h}{2}\right) \\ &= 2 \times 300 \times 0.3667 \\ &= 220.0 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$ > **Linear FOV $\approx 220$ mm** **Step 3 — Calculate magnification.** $$M = -\frac{d_i}{d_o} = -\frac{12.5}{300} = -0.0417$$ The negative sign indicates an **inverted** image. > **Magnification $M \approx -0.0417$ (image is 4.17% the size of object)** **Verification using lens equation:** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{f} &= \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i} \\ \frac{1}{12} &= \frac{1}{300} + \frac{1}{d_i} \\ \frac{1}{d_i} &= \frac{1}{12} - \frac{1}{300} = \frac{25 - 1}{300} = \frac{24}{300} = 0.08 \\ d_i &= 12.5 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget the negative sign** in magnification. The negative sign is critical — it conveys that the image is **inverted** (real image). Losing this sign = lose 1 mark. Also, do **not** confuse the linear FOV (in mm) with the angular FOV (in degrees) — they are different physical quantities. --- ### ✅ CHOICE B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3, Set B]** **Q.B.** *(a) Classify robots based on work envelope. With neat diagrams, explain the work envelope of Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, and Articulated robots. *(7 Marks)* **(b) An articulated robot has link lengths $L_1 = 500$ mm and $L_2 = 400$ mm. The shoulder joint can rotate $320°$ and the elbow can rotate from $-150°$ to $+150°$. Calculate: (i) Maximum reach, (ii) Minimum reach, (iii) Volume of a conservative spherical envelope estimate, and (iv) Whether a point located at radial distance 850 mm from the base is reachable. *(7 Marks)* #### Model Solution for Q.B(a): Refer to the four classical architectures with their characteristic envelope shapes (already illustrated in **Figure 4.2** above). **Tabular Classification (7 marks content):** | Robot Type | Joints | Envelope Shape | Reach Eq. | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Cartesian** | PPP | Rectangular cuboid | $V = L_x L_y L_z$ | Loading CNC, pick-place | | **Cylindrical** | RPP | Hollow cylinder | $V = \pi r^2 L_z$ | Lathe tending, coating | | **Spherical/Polar** | RRP | Spherical sector | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3 (1-\cos\theta)$ | Spray painting, welding | | **Articulated** | RRR | Irregular sphere-lobe | $r = L_1 + L_2$ max | Palletizing, automotive | | **SCARA** | RRP | Flat disc + Z stroke | $V = \pi r^2 L_z$ | PCB assembly | **Key distinguishing points to write in the exam:** - Cartesian = highest rigidity, lowest reach flexibility. - Cylindrical = inner dead cylinder. - Spherical = high reach but complex kinematic singularities. - Articulated = maximum flexibility, mimics human arm, used in **~70% of industrial applications**. #### Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Given:** - $L_1 = 500$ mm (shoulder-to-elbow) - $L_2 = 400$ mm (elbow-to-wrist) - Shoulder rotation $= 320°$ (so the envelope is a $320°/360°$ fraction of a full sphere) - Elbow range $=-150°$ to $+150°$ **Step 1 — Maximum reach (arm fully extended).** $$r_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 = 500 + 400 = 900 \text{ mm}$$ > **$r_{\max} = 900$ mm** **Step 2 — Minimum reach (arm fully folded, $L_2$ folds back on $L_1$).** $$r_{\min} = \lvert L_1 - L_2 \rvert = \lvert 500 - 400 \rvert = 100 \text{ mm}$$ > **$r_{\min} = 100$ mm (inner dead-zone radius)** **Step 3 — Conservative spherical envelope volume (assume $360°$ for upper bound).** $$\begin{aligned} V_{\text{sphere}} &= \frac{4}{3} \pi r_{\max}^3 \\ &= \frac{4}{3} \times 3.1416 \times (900)^3 \\ &= \frac{4}{3} \times 3.1416 \times 7.29 \times 10^8 \\ &= 3.054 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^3 \\ &= 3.054 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \\ &\approx 3.05 \text{ litres} \end{aligned}$$ > **Conservative volume $V \approx 3.05 \times 10^9$ mm³ $\approx 3.05$ litres** **Step 4 — Actual envelope volume accounting for $320°/360°$ sweep and dead zone.** $$\begin{aligned} V_{\text{actual}} &= V_{\text{sphere}} \times \frac{320°}{360°} - V_{\text{inner}} \times \frac{320°}{360°} \\ &\approx 3.054 \times 10^9 \times 0.8889 - \frac{4}{3}\pi (100)^3 \times 0.8889 \\ &\approx 2.715 \times 10^9 - 3.716 \times 10^6 \\ &\approx 2.711 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Reachability of point at $r = 850$ mm.** For the point to be reachable, $r$ must lie in the range $r_{\min} \le r \le r_{\max}$. $$\begin{aligned} r_{\min} = 100 \text{ mm} \\ r_{\max} = 900 \text{ mm} \\ 850 \text{ mm} \in [100, 900] \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ $$\therefore \text{The point at } r = 850 \text{ mm IS reachable.}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in This Type of Question:** > 1. Students often confuse $r_{\min}$ with **zero** — this is wrong. The arm **cannot fold flat to zero length** because of mechanical joint stops and link thickness. > 2. The elbow angle limits ($±150°$) must be checked — if the elbow cannot reach $180°$ (fully extended), then $r_{\max} < L_1 + L_2$ in some configurations. The question must specify the elbow range, which we used to its extreme. > 3. Many students compute **full sphere volume** without subtracting the **inner dead sphere** — losing 2 marks. > 4. Always state the **unit** (mm³ or m³ or litres) explicitly. Examiners deduct marks for missing units. --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** ✍️ ### ✅ Vision Sensors - [ ] Vision = non-contact optical sensor + image processing algorithm. - [ ] Five blocks: **illumination → lens → sensor → frame grabber → processor**. - [ ] **CCD** = high quality, low noise, high power. **CMOS** = fast, cheap, low power, ubiquitous. - [ ] **Pinhole model** uses perspective projection; the **thin lens equation** $1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i$ governs focus. - [ ] **Angular FOV** $\alpha_h = 2 \arctan(W/2f)$; **Linear FOV** = $d_o \cdot W / f$. - [ ] **Magnification** $M = -d_i / d_o$ — **always negative for a real image** (inverted). - [ ] **Stereo depth** $Z = f b / d$ — disparity is inversely proportional to depth. - [ ] **2-D vision** = position + orientation; **3-D vision** = full pose for bin-picking. - [ ] **Lighting is the #1 controllable variable** — 80% of vision failures are lighting issues. ### ✅ Work Envelope - [ ] **Work envelope** = set of all points the end-effector can reach, bounded by joint limits. - [ ] **Four classical types**: Cartesian (cuboid), Cylindrical (hollow cylinder), Spherical (sector), Articulated (irregular lobe). - [ ] **SCARA** = disc + vertical Z; **Delta** = dome above workspace. - [ ] **Cartesian volume** = $L_x L_y L_z$. - [ ] **Cylindrical volume** = $\pi r^2 L_z$. - [ ] **Spherical volume** = $\frac{2}{3}\pi r^3 (1 - \cos\theta_{\max})$. - [ ] **Articulated max reach** = $L_1 + L_2 + L_3$ (sum of link lengths). - [ ] **Articulated min reach (dead zone)** = $|L_1 - L_2| + L_3$ — there is a blind sphere around the base. - [ ] **Dexterous workspace** ⊂ **Reachable workspace** — orientability is more restrictive than reachability. - [ ] **Joint notation:** P = Prismatic, R = Revolute. PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR are the four classical signatures. ### ✅ Exam-Boost Mnemonics - **"Cartesian = Box, Cylindrical = Pipe, Spherical = Orange slice, Articulated = Donut-on-a-stick"** 🍩🍊📦🧱 - **"CCD for Quality, CMOS for Speed/Cost"** 💎⚡ - **"Bigger focal length = Narrower FOV = Higher magnification"** 🔭 - **"Smaller disparity = Larger depth"** in stereo vision.

topics.title: Classification of Work Cell Layout. Consideration in Work Cell Design. Flexible Automation vs Robotic Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Work Cell Layout. Consideration in Work Cell Design. Flexible Automation vs Robotic Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Work Cell — Formal Academic Definition

A **Work Cell** (or Robotic Work Cell) is a localized grouping of one or more workstations, machines, fixtures, material-handling devices, and a robot (or other automated equipment) arranged within a defined boundary such that the entire cell is dedicated to producing a specific part family or performing a defined set of operations with minimal inter-cell material movement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** A work cell is the fundamental building block of modern manufacturing automation. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that cell design directly determines throughput, capital cost, flexibility, and the degree to which the cell can be reconfigured for new product variants.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Think of a work cell like a **specialist's kitchen station in a restaurant**.

- A traditional **assembly line** is like a long buffet counter where every dish moves down a fixed conveyor and each chef adds one ingredient — if you want to change the dish, you have to rebuild the entire counter.
- A **work cell**, on the other hand, is like a single chef's "station" equipped with all the necessary tools (a pan, a cutting board, spices, an oven). The robot is the chef. The chef stays in one place, and the part comes to the chef (or vice versa) so that one self-contained "island" can produce a complete family of dishes.

If you want to cook something new, you don't tear down the kitchen — you just **reprogram the chef** (reprogram the robot) and **swap the tools** (end effectors/fixtures). The physical layout of the station (its shape — straight, L-shaped, U-shaped) is what we call the **Work Cell Layout**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU-Standardized Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Part Family** | A collection of parts that share similar geometric features and require similar machining/assembly processes. |
| **Cycle Time ($T_c$)** | The total time required for a cell to complete one full operation sequence on a part, expressed in **seconds per part**. |
| **Throughput** | The number of parts produced per unit time, typically **parts/hour** or **parts/day**. |
| **Gripper / End Effector** | The "hand" of the robot that physically interacts with the part. |
| **Fixture** | A dedicated device that holds the workpiece in a precise, repeatable position during processing. |
| **Buffer Storage** | A small in-cell inventory zone used to decouple upstream/downstream operations and absorb minor timing variations. |
| **Indexing Table / Rotary Table** | A motor-driven rotary platform that rotates parts between stations in a circular cell. |
| **Interlock** | A safety or control logic that prevents one machine from operating until another machine has reached a safe state. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** The KTU valuation key often awards 1 mark for crisp, textbook-correct definitions of terms like *work cell*, *cycle time*, and *flexible automation*. Memorize the table above.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Work Cell Layouts

Work cell layouts are classified primarily by the **physical arrangement** of the robot, the workstations, and the material flow path. The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes them into the following standard types:

### 2.1.1 In-Line Robot Cell (Linear Layout)

- Parts enter at one end of a straight track and exit at the other.
- The robot is mounted centrally on a linear slide or pedestal.
- **Best suited for:** Long, prismatic parts (shafts, beams) where the operations occur along a single axis.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Part arrives on infeed conveyor.
2. Robot picks part and indexes it through sequential stations (e.g., Station 1 → Station 2 → Station 3).
3. Finished part is placed on outfeed conveyor.
4. Cycle repeats.

> [!TIP]
> **Why in-line?** It is the simplest layout to design, program, and visualize. It is the **default first choice** for high-volume, low-variety production.

### 2.1.2 L-Shape Work Cell

- The robot sits in the **corner of an "L"**, with two legs of machines arranged at 90° to each other.
- **Best suited for:** Operations requiring 2–3 workstations with moderate part variety.

**Advantages over In-Line:**
- Reduced floor footprint.
- Robot can service both legs of the L with shorter, more efficient arm motions.
- Easier access for operator loading/unloading on the open side of the L.

### 2.1.3 U-Shape Work Cell (Loop Layout)

- Workstations are arranged in a **"U" pattern** around a central robot.
- Parts enter on one side and exit on the other, but the robot can reach **every station** without repositioning.
- **Best suited for:** Lean manufacturing environments, mixed-model production.

**Strategic Advantage (Lean Manufacturing Principle):**
The U-shape allows **one operator to manage multiple machines simultaneously** because the operator stands at the open mouth of the U and can oversee every station. This aligns with **Toyota Production System (TPS)** philosophy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The U-shaped cell is often the answer in KTU questions asking *"Which layout is best for a mixed-model, low-to-medium volume production environment with operator involvement?"* — the answer is **U-Shape**.

### 2.1.4 Circular / Rotary Work Cell

- An **indexing rotary table** sits at the center.
- Multiple parts are clamped on the table at equally spaced positions.
- The robot (mounted at the side) performs the same operation (or different operations at different angles) as the table indexes through 360°.

**Strategic Advantage:**
- The table is always moving; while the robot works on one part, the next part is automatically being positioned. This dramatically reduces **non-productive (idle) time**.

### 2.1.5 Walking Beam / Mobile Robot Cell

- The robot itself **travels on a linear track** between fixed workstations.
- Used when the part is very large (e.g., aircraft wing, locomotive body) and cannot be moved easily.

### 2.1.6 Carousel / Trunnion Cell

- Parts are mounted on a rotating **trunnion** or **carousel** with two faces.
- The robot works on one face, then the trunnion flips 180° to expose the second face.

## 2.2 Summary Table — Layout Classification

| Layout Type | Geometry | Best For | Footprint | Reconfigurability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **In-Line** | Straight | High volume, single family | Long, narrow | Low |
| **L-Shape** | Right angle | 2–3 stations, moderate variety | Compact | Medium |
| **U-Shape** | Loop | Mixed model, lean | Very compact | High |
| **Circular/Rotary** | Round, indexing | Drilling, deburring, small parts | Compact (radial) | Medium |
| **Walking Beam** | Robot on rail | Large parts (auto, aerospace) | Variable | High |
| **Trunnion/Carousel** | Two-sided | Welding, painting | Small | Medium |

## 2.3 Considerations in Work Cell Design

Designing a robotic work cell is a **multi-criteria optimization problem**. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates the following considerations:

### 2.3.1 Part-Related Considerations

- **Part Variety:** How many different SKUs (Stock Keeping Units) will the cell produce? High variety → favor U-Shape or Walking Beam.
- **Part Geometry & Size:** Determines robot reach, payload, and gripper design.
- **Part Weight:** Determines robot payload capacity (a 1 kg part may need a robot rated for **5 kg payload** to account for gripper mass and dynamic forces).
- **Production Volume:** Low → flexible cell; High → dedicated cell.

### 2.3.2 Process-Related Considerations

- **Number of Operations per Part:** Determines the number of workstations.
- **Cycle Time of Each Operation:** Sum of operation times = baseline cycle time.
- **Tooling Requirements:** Determines number of tool changes and tool storage.
- **Sequence of Operations:** Determines layout geometry (linear vs. U-Shape).

### 2.3.3 Equipment-Related Considerations

- **Robot Selection:** Reach, payload, repeatability, degrees of freedom.
- **End Effector Design:** Mechanical, vacuum, magnetic, or multi-function gripper.
- **Fixture Design:** Dedicated vs. modular (e.g., **fixture plate with dowel pins** for quick changeover).
- **Sensors & Vision:** Proximity sensors, force/torque sensors, machine vision for part recognition.

### 2.3.4 Material Handling

- **Infeed/Outfeed:** Conveyor, gravity chute, pallet conveyor, or AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle).
- **Inter-cell Transfer:** Should the cell be standalone or linked to upstream/downstream cells?

### 2.3.5 Safety Considerations

- **Physical Barriers:** Safety fences, light curtains, interlocked gates.
- **E-Stop Circuit:** Hardwired emergency stop in compliance with **ISO 10218** and **ANSI/RIA R15.06**.
- **Collaborative Zones:** If using collaborative robots (cobots), define **power and force limiting (PFL)** zones per **ISO/TS 15066**.

### 2.3.6 Economic & Layout Considerations

- **Floor Space:** Compact layouts (U-Shape, Rotary) reduce facility cost.
- **Capital Cost vs. ROI:** Robotic cell payback is typically **2–3 years** in high-wage economies.
- **Scalability:** Can the cell be expanded for future product variants?
- **Maintenance Access:** Adequate clearance for technician access to robot, fixtures, and controls.

## 2.4 Flexible Automation vs. Robotic Technology

This is a **commonly confused topic** in KTU exams, but the distinction is fundamental.

### 2.4.1 Flexible Automation (FA) — Definition

**Flexible Automation** is a broad term referring to automated manufacturing systems that can be **reprogrammed or reconfigured** (with minimal manual intervention) to produce a **variety of parts or products** in a range of volumes. FA is implemented through a hierarchy of systems:

- **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS):** Integrates CNC machines, automated material handling, and a central computer.
- **Flexible Manufacturing Cell (FMC):** A single, self-contained work cell (which is precisely the work cell we have been discussing).
- **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM):** Enterprise-level integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A **robotic work cell is one specific implementation** of Flexible Automation. FA is the *category*; robotics is one *tool* within that category.

### 2.4.2 Robotic Technology — Definition

**Robotic Technology** refers specifically to the use of **programmable, multi-functional manipulators** (industrial robots) that can perform a variety of tasks through changes in software (program) and hardware (end effector). Robots are a **subset** of flexible automation.

### 2.4.3 The Relationship

Think of it as a **set theory relationship**:

- **Flexible Automation ⊃ Robotic Technology**
- Other technologies in FA include CNC machines, AGVs, AS/RS (Automated Storage and Retrieval Systems), vision systems, and PLC-based hard automation that is reprogrammable.

### 2.4.4 Detailed Comparison Table

| Parameter | Flexible Automation (FA) | Robotic Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Broad — covers all reprogrammable automated systems | Narrow — specifically industrial robots |
| **Primary Element** | Computer-controlled machines, conveyors, software | Programmable manipulator arm |
| **Flexibility Source** | Software reprogramming + modular tooling | Software reprogramming + tool/fixture change |
| **Investment Cost** | Very high (FMS can be $10M+) | Moderate ($50K–$500K per robot cell) |
| **Application Range** | Machining, assembly, testing, packaging | Welding, painting, pick-and-place, palletizing |
| **Volume Suitability** | Low to high mixed-model volumes | Low to medium volumes (best) |
| **Operator Skill** | High — software, PLCs, robotics | Moderate — robot programming |
| **Examples** | Mazak FMS, Cincinnati Milacron FMS | FANUC robot, ABB IRB, KUKA KR series |
| **Reconfigurability** | High (modular cells) | High (reprogram + retool) |
| **KTU Exam View** | "Category" answer | "Subset/Implementation" answer |

### 2.4.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Body Shop:** Robotic technology dominates (welding robots on linear rails).
- **Aerospace Structural Assembly:** Flexible automation (FMS with large 5-axis CNC + robots) is the norm.
- **Electronics Assembly:** Flexible automation with vision-guided robotic pick-and-place (e.g., SMT lines).
- **Job Shops with High Part Variety:** Use FMS (FA) with multiple robotic cells networked under a central MES (Manufacturing Execution System).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2-Mark Memory Trick:**
> - *Flexible Automation* = **System-level concept** (think: FMS, FMC, CIM).
> - *Robotic Technology* = **Equipment-level concept** (think: 6-axis robot arm).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Cycle time = sum of all operation times | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Parts per hour (assuming 1 part per cycle) | parts/hour |
| 3 | $P_{\text{robot}} \geq m_{\text{part}} + m_{\text{gripper}} \cdot SF$ | Robot payload selection (SF = 1.5–2.0 safety factor) | kg |
| 4 | $R_{\text{robot}} \geq D_{\text{farthest\ station}} + d_{\text{approach}}$ | Robot reach must exceed farthest station distance | mm |
| 5 | $U = \dfrac{t_{\text{active}}}{t_{\text{active}} + t_{\text{idle}}}$ | Robot utilization ratio (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Savings} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Return on investment for cell | % |
| 7 | $\text{Number of cells} = \dfrac{D_{\text{annual}}}{T_c \times H \times E}$ | Cells required = annual demand ÷ (cycle time × hours × efficiency) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{\text{idle}} = T_c - t_{\text{longest op}}$ | Idle time in asynchronous cell | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet Rule Reminder:** All vertical bars and absolute values in formulas are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to maintain markdown table integrity.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Number of Work Cells for a Given Demand

**Given:**
- Annual demand $D$ (parts/year)
- Available production hours per year $H$ (hours/year)
- Cell efficiency $E$ (typically **0.85 to 0.90** to account for changeovers, breakdowns, and breaks)
- Cycle time $T_c$ (seconds/part)

**Find:** Number of work cells $N$ required to meet demand.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute Effective Production Time per Year:**

The total calendar hours are reduced by the efficiency factor $E$ to account for non-productive time.

$$
T_{\text{effective}} = H \times E
$$

where $T_{\text{effective}}$ is the productive time available per year, in **hours/year**.

**Step 2 — Convert Effective Time to Seconds:**

Since the cycle time $T_c$ is in seconds, we must convert the available time into the same unit.

$$
T_{\text{effective (s)}} = H \times E \times 3600
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Production Capacity of One Cell:**

If a cell completes one part every $T_c$ seconds, then in $T_{\text{effective (s)}}$ seconds, it produces:

$$
C_{\text{one cell}} = \frac{H \times E \times 3600}{T_c}
$$

where $C_{\text{one cell}}$ is in **parts per year per cell**.

**Step 4 — Compute Number of Cells Required:**

$$
N = \frac{D}{C_{\text{one cell}}} = \frac{D \times T_c}{H \times E \times 3600}
$$

Always **round up** to the next whole number because you cannot have 2.3 cells.

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = \left\lceil N \right\rceil
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

A manufacturer needs to produce **$D = 500{,}000$** parts per year. The cell cycle time is **$T_c = 45$ s**. The plant operates **$H = 2400$ hours/year** (two shifts, 300 working days). Cell efficiency is **$E = 0.85$**.

**Step 1:**

$$
T_{\text{effective}} = 2400 \times 0.85 = 2040 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
T_{\text{effective (s)}} = 2040 \times 3600 = 7{,}344{,}000 \text{ s/year}
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
C_{\text{one cell}} = \frac{7{,}344{,}000}{45} = 163{,}200 \text{ parts/year/cell}
$$

**Step 4:**

$$
N = \frac{500{,}000}{163{,}200} = 3.064
$$

Since we cannot have a fractional cell, we round up:

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = 4 \text{ cells}
$$

**Validation Check:**

$$
C_{\text{4 cells}} = 4 \times 163{,}200 = 652{,}800 \text{ parts/year} \geq 500{,}000 \quad \checkmark
$$

Surplus capacity: $652{,}800 - 500{,}000 = 152{,}800$ parts/year (a buffer of approximately **30.6%**, which is healthy).

## 3.2 Derivation: Robot Utilization in a Multi-Station Cell

**Given:** A work cell with $n$ workstations. The robot's task is to load/unload each station. The longest operation time among stations is $t_{\text{longest}}$.

**Robot Utilization Formula:**

The robot is **active** while loading, unloading, and transferring parts. It is **idle** only when all stations are busy and it must wait.

If the robot service time per station is $t_s$ (assumed constant), then robot busy time per cycle is:

$$
t_{\text{active}} = n \times t_s
$$

The cycle time is dictated by the **bottleneck station**:

$$
T_c = \max(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n) + t_s
$$

Therefore, the **utilization** is:

$$
U = \frac{n \times t_s}{\max(t_i) + t_s}
$$

**Example:** 3 stations with operation times 20 s, 35 s, 28 s. Robot service time per station = 6 s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{3 \times 6}{\max(20, 35, 28) + 6} \\
&= \frac{18}{35 + 6} \\
&= \frac{18}{41} \\
&\approx 0.439 \text{ or } 43.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A utilization of ~44% is **typical** in real cells. High utilization (>85%) is a warning sign that the robot is a bottleneck and the cell is at risk of downtime cascading from any robot fault. KTU examiners like questions on this trade-off.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Layout Should I Choose?

This is a high-yield KTU topic. The following matrix helps decide the optimal layout:

| Design Driver | Best Layout | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Very high volume, single part | **In-Line** | Long conveyors, dedicated stations, optimized flow. |
| 2 operations, limited floor space | **L-Shape** | Compact 90° arrangement fits into corners. |
| Mixed model, operator involvement | **U-Shape** | Operator can supervise all stations; TPS-aligned. |
| Drilling/deburring many small holes | **Circular/Rotary** | Multiple parts indexed simultaneously; high throughput. |
| Part too large to move (e.g., car body) | **Walking Beam** | Robot moves; part is stationary. |
| Welding on two sides of a part | **Trunnion** | 180° flip exposes both faces to the same robot. |
| Frequent changeovers, modular tooling | **U-Shape or FMC** | Modular fixtures + flexible cell. |

## 3.4 Comparative Numerical: In-Line vs. U-Shape Throughput

**Scenario:** Three operations of 30 s, 25 s, and 40 s on a part. Robot transfer time between stations is 5 s.

**In-Line Cell:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c^{\text{inline}} &= t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + 3 \times t_{\text{transfer}} \\
&= 30 + 25 + 40 + 3 \times 5 \\
&= 110 \text{ s/part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Throughput:**

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\text{inline}} = \frac{3600}{110} \approx 32.7 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**U-Shape Cell (parallel, robot serves all three stations concurrently):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c^{\text{U}} &= \max(t_1, t_2, t_3) + t_{\text{transfer}} \\
&= \max(30, 25, 40) + 5 \\
&= 45 \text{ s/part}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Throughput:**

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\text{U}} = \frac{3600}{45} = 80 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The U-Shape layout is **144.6% faster** in this scenario because it enables **parallelism** — the robot can work on one station while the others complete their operations independently. This is the **most powerful KTU exam insight** for Module 3.
