# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Work Cell. List any four classifications of work cell layouts.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

A **work cell** is a localized grouping of machines, fixtures, material-handling devices, and a robot (or other automated equipment) arranged within a defined boundary to produce a family of parts with minimal inter-cell material flow.

Four classifications of work cell layouts (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the list with one-line descriptions):

1. **In-Line Cell** — stations arranged linearly along a single axis; suitable for high-volume single-product families.
2. **L-Shape Cell** — stations arranged at 90° around a corner-mounted robot; compact 2–3 station layout.
3. **U-Shape Cell** — stations arranged in a loop around a central robot; ideal for mixed-model lean production.
4. **Rotary/Circular Cell** — parts indexed on a rotating table; ideal for high-throughput small-part operations.

*(Examiner's Note: Any four standard layout names with one-line correctness will earn full marks. Students who write only the names without justification lose 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Flexible Automation and Robotic Technology in two points.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Parameter | Flexible Automation (FA) | Robotic Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | A broad system-level concept encompassing all reprogrammable automated systems (FMS, FMC, CIM). | A specific equipment-level technology using programmable multi-functional manipulators (industrial robots). |
| **Relationship** | A "category" or "superset." | A "subset" of FA — robots are one tool used to implement FA. |
| **Investment** | Very high ($1M–$10M+ for a full FMS). | Moderate ($50K–$500K per robot cell). |

(3 marks: 1 for the distinction, 1 for the relationship/scope, 1 for the cost/engineering perspective.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper for 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the different types of robotic work cell layouts. Explain any **two** layouts in detail with neat sketches and suitable applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the important **considerations in the design of a robotic work cell**. Highlight the role of safety considerations and the relevant safety standards.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Classification and Detailed Explanation of Two Layouts [7 marks]**

**Classification (2 marks — full list with one-line each):**

1. In-Line Cell
2. L-Shape Cell
3. U-Shape Cell
4. Rotary/Circular Cell
5. Walking Beam Cell
6. Trunnion/Carousel Cell

**Detailed Layout 1: In-Line Cell (2.5 marks)**

- **Geometry:** Stations are placed along a straight line. The robot is mounted centrally on a pedestal or on a linear slide.
- **Operation:** Parts enter on an infeed conveyor. The robot picks the part and indexes it through Station 1, Station 2, Station 3, ... and finally places it on the outfeed conveyor.
- **Advantages:** Simple to design, program, and visualize. Good for high-volume, low-variety production.
- **Disadvantages:** Long floor footprint. Limited flexibility for adding new stations.
- **Applications:** Welding of long beams, painting of shafts, automated assembly of small appliances.

**Detailed Layout 2: U-Shape Cell (2.5 marks)**

- **Geometry:** Stations are arranged in a "U" loop around a central robot.
- **Operation:** Parts enter on one side of the U and exit on the other. The robot reaches all stations without repositioning. Often combined with an operator standing at the open mouth of the U.
- **Advantages:** Compact footprint, supports mixed-model production, lean manufacturing (TPS-aligned), single-operator oversight of multiple machines, high reconfigurability.
- **Disadvantages:** More complex robot programming; harder to physically interlock.
- **Applications:** Mixed-model automotive sub-assembly, lean job-shop production, medical device assembly.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** Sketches should be drawn neatly; even a hand-drawn-looking ASCII or rough block diagram in the answer booklet earns full credit if labeled correctly.

---

**(b) Considerations in Work Cell Design [7 marks]**

**1. Part-Related (1.5 marks):**
Part variety, geometry, size, weight, and annual production volume. These drive robot payload, reach, and gripper design.

**2. Process-Related (1.5 marks):**
Number of operations, cycle time of each operation, tooling requirements, and sequence of operations. These determine the number of workstations and the layout geometry.

**3. Equipment-Related (1 mark):**
Robot selection (reach, payload, repeatability, DOF), end effector design, fixture design (dedicated vs. modular), and sensor/vision integration.

**4. Material Handling (1 mark):**
Infeed/outfeed mechanisms (conveyor, pallet, AGV), buffer storage sizing, and inter-cell transfer policy.

**5. Safety Considerations — Highlighted (2 marks):**
- **Physical Safety:** Safety fences, light curtains, interlocked gates, safety mats.
- **E-Stop Circuit:** Hardwired emergency stop in compliance with **ISO 10218-1/-2** (Industrial robot safety) and **ANSI/RIA R15.06** (North American robot safety).
- **Collaborative Operation:** If using cobots, define **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** zones per **ISO/TS 15066**.
- **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO):** For maintenance, in compliance with **ISO 14118**.
- **Risk Assessment:** Mandatory per **ISO 12100** before commissioning.

---

### Question B: (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper for 2024 Scheme, Module 3, Internal Choice Option]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is **Flexible Automation**? Compare and contrast **Flexible Automation and Robotic Technology** in a detailed tabular format. Give **two real-world examples** of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturer needs to produce **$D = 300{,}000$** parts per year using a robotic work cell. The cell cycle time is **$T_c = 60$ seconds/part**. The plant operates for **$H = 2000$ hours/year** (single shift, 250 working days × 8 hours). The cell efficiency is **$E = 0.90$**. Calculate the **number of work cells required**. If the cell efficiency drops to **$0.80$** due to changeovers, how many cells are needed then? Comment on the cost implications.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Flexible Automation vs. Robotic Technology [7 marks]**

**Definition of Flexible Automation (1 mark):**
Flexible Automation (FA) is a manufacturing approach in which automated systems can be **reprogrammed or reconfigured** (with minimal manual intervention) to produce a **variety of parts or products** in a range of volumes. It encompasses FMS, FMC, and CIM.

**Detailed Comparison Table (4 marks — distributed as 0.5 mark per row):**

| Parameter | Flexible Automation (FA) | Robotic Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | System-level (entire factory) | Equipment-level (single robot cell) |
| **Hierarchy** | Superset / Category | Subset / Implementation tool |
| **Flexibility Source** | Software + modular tooling + reconfigurable cells | Software + end effector change |
| **Examples of Hardware** | CNC machines, AGVs, AS/RS, robots | 6-axis arms, SCARA, Delta, Cobots |
| **Investment** | Very high ($1M–$10M+) | Moderate ($50K–$500K per cell) |
| **Operator Skill** | High (PLCs, software, vision) | Moderate (robot programming) |
| **Volume Suitability** | Low to high mixed | Low to medium mixed (best) |
| **Lead Time for Changeover** | Hours to days | Minutes to hours |

**Real-World Examples (2 marks — 1 mark per example):**

- **FA Example 1:** Mazak Integrex FMS — integrates 5-axis CNC, pallet changers, and AGVs in a single FMS for mixed-volume aerospace parts.
- **FA Example 2:** Siemens Electronics Works (Amberg) — fully CIM-integrated SMT line producing 17 million SIMATIC units per year with ~75% automation.
- **Robotic Tech Example 1:** FANUC R-2000iC 6-axis robot welding truck frames on an automotive body shop line.
- **Robotic Tech Example 2:** ABB YuMi cobot assembling small electronics (e.g., smartphone components) on a single workstation.

---

**(b) Numerical Cell Sizing [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Effective Production Time:**

$$
T_{\text{effective}} = H \times E = 2000 \times 0.90 = 1800 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Seconds:**

$$
T_{\text{effective (s)}} = 1800 \times 3600 = 6{,}480{,}000 \text{ s/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity per Cell:**

$$
C_{\text{one cell}} = \frac{6{,}480{,}000}{60} = 108{,}000 \text{ parts/year/cell}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of Cells (Efficiency 0.90):**

$$
N_1 = \frac{300{,}000}{108{,}000} = 2.78 \Rightarrow N_{\text{actual},1} = 3 \text{ cells}
$$

**Step 5 — Recalculate with E = 0.80:**

$$
T_{\text{effective,2}} = 2000 \times 0.80 = 1600 \text{ hours/year}
$$

$$
T_{\text{effective,2 (s)}} = 1600 \times 3600 = 5{,}760{,}000 \text{ s/year}
$$

$$
C_{\text{cell,2}} = \frac{5{,}760{,}000}{60} = 96{,}000 \text{ parts/year/cell}
$$

$$
N_2 = \frac{300{,}000}{96{,}000} = 3.125 \Rightarrow N_{\text{actual},2} = 4 \text{ cells}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost Implications (1 mark):**

- Increasing from **3 cells to 4 cells** means an additional capital investment of approximately **$150{,}000$–$500{,}000$** (one robot cell, including integration, fixturing, and safety).
- The **10% drop in efficiency** (from 0.90 to 0.80) translated into a **33% increase** in the number of cells required.
- This demonstrates the **high sensitivity of cell design to efficiency assumptions**; in real projects, conservative efficiency values (0.75–0.80) are recommended to avoid under-sizing.
- **Mitigation strategies:** Implement SMED (Single-Minute Exchange of Die), preventive maintenance, and standardized changeover procedures to recover efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing FA and Robotics:** Many students write them as synonyms. They are **not** the same. FA is a **category**; robotics is a **subset**. Loss: 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up cell counts:** A cell count of 2.78 must become 3, not 2. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring units in cell-sizing calculations:** Writing cycle time in minutes but hours in $H$ without conversion is a frequent error. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **Listing layout names without justification:** Naming "U-Shape" without explaining *why* it suits mixed-model production loses 1 mark in Part B.
> 5. **Skipping safety standards:** Writing "safety fences are needed" without naming **ISO 10218** and **ANSI/RIA R15.06** loses 1 mark in 7-mark questions.
> 6. **In Part A (3-mark), writing only a single line:** KTU Part A answers must be at least 4–5 lines; one-liners cap at 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read This the Night Before the Exam):**

- [ ] A **work cell** is a self-contained, localized grouping of machines + robot + material handling for a part family.
- [ ] **Six layout types** to memorize: **In-Line, L-Shape, U-Shape, Rotary/Circular, Walking Beam, Trunnion/Carousel.**
- [ ] **In-Line** = high volume, single family. **U-Shape** = mixed model + lean. **Rotary** = small parts + high throughput. **Walking Beam** = large parts.
- [ ] **U-Shape** enables **parallelism**: cycle time = max(operation times) + transfer, not the sum.
- [ ] **Design considerations:** Part variety, geometry, weight, volume, number of operations, cycle time, robot reach/payload, end effector, fixture, sensors, material handling, safety, cost, floor space.
- [ ] **Safety standards:** **ISO 10218** (industrial robot), **ANSI/RIA R15.06** (North America), **ISO/TS 15066** (cobot collaboration), **ISO 12100** (risk assessment), **ISO 14118** (LOTO).
- [ ] **Flexible Automation ⊃ Robotic Technology** — FA is the system, robotics is a tool.
- [ ] **Cell sizing formula:** $N = \dfrac{D \times T_c}{H \times E \times 3600}$ — always **round up**.
- [ ] **Efficiency $E$** is typically **0.85–0.90**; lower $E$ = more cells = higher capital cost.
- [ ] **Robot utilization** $U = \dfrac{n \cdot t_s}{\max(t_i) + t_s}$ — high $U$ (>85%) = robot is the bottleneck (a warning sign).
- [ ] **Payback period** for a robotic cell is typically **2–3 years** in high-wage economies.
- [ ] Always state **units** in numerical answers; **always justify** layout choices in descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spray Painting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spray Painting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Robotic Spray Painting** is a specialized industrial painting process in which a computer-controlled, multi-axis articulated robotic manipulator equipped with an end-effector spray gun atomizes and deposits paint, varnish, lacquer, or other coating materials onto a workpiece surface in a precisely programmed trajectory. The robot is taught to follow a continuous, uniform path that guarantees consistent film thickness, edge coverage, and minimal overspray.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Tag:** Spray painting is classified under **Material Handling / Material Application** applications of industrial robots (as per KTU 2024 PEIET411 syllabus, Module 4 – Industrial Robot Applications). The paint spray gun is treated as a non-gripper end-effector specifically designed for fluid atomization rather than mechanical grasping.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

Imagine you are painting a steel chair with a hand-held spray can. To get an even coat, you must:
1. Keep your hand **the same distance** from the chair as you move it.
2. Move your wrist at a **constant speed**.
3. **Overlap** each stroke so you don't leave stripes.
4. Avoid tilting the can, so the spray pattern stays uniform.

Now imagine doing that for **8 hours a day, every day, in a booth full of toxic fumes**, painting 200 car doors. A human gets tired, his wrist shakes, his speed varies, and he breathes solvents. A **robot spray painter** never tires, never shakes, and always keeps the gun at exactly **150 mm** from the surface, moving at **500 mm/s**, at exactly **90°** to the surface — for 20 years.

The robot essentially acts as a **"perfect, tireless human wrist"** that memorizes the path once and reproduces it identically, millions of times.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants

The following are the **industry-standard process parameters** every KTU examiner expects you to recognize:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standoff Distance (Gun to Workpiece) | **100 – 250** | **mm** |
| Spray Gun Traverse Speed | **300 – 1000** | **mm/s** |
| Atomizing Air Pressure | **2 – 4** | **bar** |
| Fan Pattern Width | **50 – 300** | **mm** |
| Wet Film Thickness per Pass | **15 – 35** | **µm** |
| Transfer Efficiency (Air Spray) | **30 – 40** | **%** |
| Transfer Efficiency (Electrostatic) | **70 – 90** | **%** |
| Trigger Pull Response Time | **< 50** | **ms** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Transfer Efficiency** is the ratio of paint actually deposited on the workpiece to the total paint atomized. It is the single most important economic metric of a spray painting process — higher efficiency means less paint wastage and lower volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions.

## 1.4 Why Robots are Used in Spray Painting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (Module 4, 14-Mark Application Question):** Examiners frequently ask *why robots are preferred* over manual painters. Memorize these 5 reasons:
>
> 1. **Uniformity & Repeatability** — identical film thickness on every unit.
> 2. **Worker Safety** — humans are removed from isocyanate, epoxy, and solvent fumes (carcinogenic).
> 3. **Higher Transfer Efficiency** — electrostatic robots can exceed **85%**, vs. **30%** for hand spray.
> 4. **Higher Throughput** — cycle times reduced by 30–50%.
> 5. **Programming for Complex Geometry** — car bodies, aircraft fuselages have compound curves inaccessible by simple jig motions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Spray Painting Process — Operating Principle

The spray painting robot operates on a continuous, closed-loop, **servo-controlled trajectory** with the spray gun held perpendicular to the local surface normal. The process has four sequential subsystems:

1. **Paint Feed Subsystem** — pressurizes and delivers paint from a reservoir through a hose to the spray gun at a regulated flow rate `Q_p`.
2. **Atomization Subsystem** — uses compressed air (or hydraulic pressure) to break the liquid paint stream into a fine, uniform cloud of droplets of mean diameter `d_d`.
3. **Pattern Shaping Subsystem** — uses air jets (called *horn air* / *fan air*) to flatten the conical droplet cloud into an elliptical *fan pattern* of width `W_f`.
4. **Deposition & Trajectory Subsystem** — the robot moves the gun along a programmed path with **constant standoff distance** `h` and **constant gun velocity** `v_g`.

## 2.2 Atomization Mechanisms (Core Theory)

### 2.2.1 Air (Conventional) Atomization
A high-velocity stream of compressed air (typically **2–4 bar**) shears a low-velocity liquid stream emerging from the fluid nozzle. The aerodynamic shear forces overcome the liquid's surface tension, fragmenting it into droplets. Droplet size follows an empirical relationship:

$$
d_d \;\propto\; \frac{\sigma^{0.5}}{\rho_a^{0.5} \cdot v_a}
$$

where:
- `d_d` = mean droplet diameter (µm)
- `σ` = surface tension of paint (N/m)
- `ρ_a` = density of atomizing air (kg/m³)
- `v_a` = velocity of atomizing air (m/s)

> **Insight:** Increasing atomizing air velocity `v_a` produces *finer droplets*, which give a *smoother finish* but *lower transfer efficiency* (more bounce-off and overspray).

### 2.2.2 Airless (Hydraulic) Atomization
Paint is forced through a small orifice (**0.2 – 0.5 mm**) at very high pressure (**100 – 250 bar**). The sudden pressure drop at the exit causes the liquid to disintegrate into droplets. **No atomizing air** is used — hence "airless." This produces a *higher transfer efficiency* (~50%) and is suited for high-viscosity paints.

### 2.2.3 Electrostatic Atomization
The spray gun nozzle is charged to a high negative DC potential (**–30 kV to –100 kV**), while the workpiece is grounded. Atomized paint droplets pick up negative charge and are attracted along electric field lines to the grounded workpiece. This gives the **wrap-around effect**, coating the *back side* of edges — a phenomenon impossible with purely mechanical spraying.

## 2.3 Spray Cone Geometry

The fan-shaped spray pattern can be modeled as a **sector of an annulus** when projected onto the workpiece. The spray cone has a half-angle `α` (typically 5°–15°) and a width `W_f` at standoff distance `h`:

$$
W_f \;=\; 2 \, h \, \tan(\alpha)
$$

The **deposition area per unit time** (footprint swept by the gun) is:

$$
A_{\text{sweep}} \;=\; v_g \cdot W_f
$$

where `v_g` is the gun traverse speed (mm/s).

## 2.4 Paint Flow Rate and Film Thickness

The volumetric flow rate of paint leaving the nozzle is `Q_p` (mm³/s or cm³/s). When deposited over the swept area, the **wet film thickness** per pass is:

$$
t_{\text{film}} \;=\; \frac{Q_p}{v_g \cdot W_f}
$$

where:
- `t_film` = wet film thickness (µm or mm)
- `Q_p` = paint volume flow rate (mm³/s)
- `v_g` = gun traverse speed (mm/s)
- `W_f` = spray pattern width on the workpiece (mm)

> **Why this matters (the "Why" behind the formula):** If a KTU problem gives you the gun speed and pattern width, you can solve for either flow rate or required number of passes to achieve a specified dry film thickness (DFT). After solvent evaporation, dry film thickness is:
>
> $$
> t_{\text{DFT}} \;=\; t_{\text{film}} \times (\text{Volume Solids Fraction})
> $$

## 2.5 Overlap Coverage

To eliminate striping, consecutive parallel passes of the robot must overlap. If the fan width is `W_f` and the offset between successive passes is `Δy`, the **overlap ratio** is:

$$
R_{\text{ov}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \frac{\Delta y}{W_f}
$$

Industry standard: **30%–50% overlap** (i.e., `R_ov` between **0.30** and **0.50**).

## 2.6 Standoff Distance and Spray Pattern Distortion

The spray fan is symmetric **only when** the gun is held perpendicular to the surface at the rated standoff `h_0` (typically **200 mm**). Tilting the gun by angle `θ` from the normal **distorts** the elliptical pattern into a teardrop shape, producing non-uniform film. KTU robots enforce **gun attitude control** to keep `θ ≤ 5°`.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula reference** for any KTU numerical on spray painting. Memorize these.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fan Pattern Width | $W_f = 2 h \tan(\alpha)$ | mm | Width of spray on workpiece |
| 2 | Swept Area Rate | $A_{\text{sweep}} = v_g \cdot W_f$ | mm²/s | Area painted per second |
| 3 | Wet Film Thickness | $t_{\text{film}} = \dfrac{Q_p}{v_g \cdot W_f}$ | µm | Thickness deposited per pass |
| 4 | Dry Film Thickness | $t_{\text{DFT}} = t_{\text{film}} \times V_s$ | µm | Thickness after solvent loss |
| 5 | Overlap Ratio | $R_{\text{ov}} = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta y}{W_f}$ | dimensionless | Fractional overlap of passes |
| 6 | Spray Cone Half-Angle | $\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{W_f}{2h}\right)$ | degrees/rad | Atomization geometry |
| 7 | Droplet Diameter | $d_d \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{\sigma}}{\sqrt{\rho_a}\, v_a}$ | µm | Atomization quality |
| 8 | Number of Passes | $n_{\text{pass}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{DFT,req}}}{t_{\text{DFT,per pass}}}$ | integer | Coating buildup cycles |
| 9 | Cycle Time | $T_c = \dfrac{L_{\text{path}}}{v_g} + n_{\text{trig}} \cdot t_{\text{trig}}$ | s | Total painting time per part |
| 10 | Transfer Efficiency | $\eta_t = \dfrac{m_{\text{deposited}}}{m_{\text{sprayed}}} \times 100$ | % | Material utilization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Film Thickness from Flow Rate

**Problem:** A robot spray gun has a volumetric flow rate `Q_p = 1200 mm³/s`. The gun is traversed at `v_g = 500 mm/s` and produces a fan pattern of width `W_f = 150 mm` on the workpiece. Compute the **wet film thickness** deposited per pass.

### Step 1 — Identify Given Data
We list the parameters in standard SI-like units:

$$
Q_p = 1200 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}, \qquad v_g = 500 \;\text{mm/s}, \qquad W_f = 150 \;\text{mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Governing Equation
From the KTU Formula Sheet, the wet film thickness equation is:

$$
t_{\text{film}} \;=\; \frac{Q_p}{v_g \cdot W_f}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerically
We substitute the values into the equation. Note the unit cancellation logic:

$$
t_{\text{film}} \;=\; \frac{1200 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}}{(500 \;\text{mm/s}) \cdot (150 \;\text{mm})}
$$

$$
t_{\text{film}} \;=\; \frac{1200 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}}{75000 \;\text{mm}^2/\text{s}}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the Units and Compute
The seconds cancel, and one factor of mm cancels between numerator and denominator, leaving mm:

$$
t_{\text{film}} \;=\; \frac{1200}{75000} \;\text{mm} \;=\; 0.016 \;\text{mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Micrometres
Since paint film thickness is conventionally reported in micrometres, we multiply by 1000:

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{film}} \;=\; 16 \;\mu\text{m}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A single pass deposits a 16 µm wet film. To achieve a typical automotive topcoat of 40 µm dry film thickness with a paint of 50% volume solids, you need:
>
> $$
> t_{\text{DFT,per pass}} = 16 \times 0.50 = 8 \;\mu\text{m}
> $$
>
> $$
> n_{\text{pass}} = \frac{40}{8} = 5 \;\text{passes}
> $$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Spray Cone Half-Angle from Geometry

**Problem:** A spray gun is held at a standoff distance `h = 200 mm`. The fan pattern width measured on a flat calibration plate is `W_f = 120 mm`. Determine the **spray cone half-angle** `α`.

### Step 1 — Geometry Setup
The fan pattern is symmetrical about the gun axis. The standoff `h` is the perpendicular distance from the nozzle to the workpiece. Half of the fan width `W_f/2` is the lateral spread of the spray at distance `h`.

### Step 2 — Trigonometric Relationship
From the right triangle formed by the gun axis, the standoff, and the fan edge:

$$
\tan(\alpha) \;=\; \frac{W_f / 2}{h}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values
We substitute the given values into the trigonometric equation:

$$
\tan(\alpha) \;=\; \frac{120 / 2}{200} \;=\; \frac{60}{200} \;=\; 0.300
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the Half-Angle
Taking the inverse tangent of both sides:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \arctan(0.300)
$$

$$
\alpha \;=\; 16.699^\circ
$$

### Step 5 — Final Answer with Engineering Comment

$$
\boxed{\alpha \;\approx\; 16.7^\circ}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A half-angle of 16.7° is consistent with a medium-fan industrial spray gun (typical range 5°–20°). Wider angles produce thinner, more diffuse coatings; narrower angles concentrate paint for narrow features like edges.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Overlap Ratio and Pass Spacing

**Problem:** A robot is to paint a flat panel of width `B = 900 mm`. The spray fan width is `W_f = 200 mm`. The required overlap ratio is `R_ov = 0.50` (50%). Calculate (a) the **offset distance** between successive passes, and (b) the **number of parallel passes** required.

### Part (a) — Offset Distance
From the KTU Formula Sheet:

$$
R_{\text{ov}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\Delta y}{W_f}
$$

Rearranging for the offset:

$$
\frac{\Delta y}{W_f} \;=\; 1 - R_{\text{ov}}
$$

$$
\Delta y \;=\; W_f \cdot (1 - R_{\text{ov}})
$$

Substituting `W_f = 200 mm` and `R_ov = 0.50`:

$$
\Delta y \;=\; 200 \cdot (1 - 0.50) \;=\; 200 \cdot 0.50
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta y \;=\; 100 \;\text{mm}}
$$

### Part (b) — Number of Passes
The first pass covers a strip of width `W_f = 200 mm`. Each subsequent pass adds a strip of width `Δy = 100 mm` (since the overlap discards the inner 100 mm). To cover total width `B = 900 mm`:

$$
n_{\text{pass}} \;=\; 1 + \frac{B - W_f}{\Delta y}
$$

$$
n_{\text{pass}} \;=\; 1 + \frac{900 - 200}{100} \;=\; 1 + \frac{700}{100} \;=\; 1 + 7
$$

$$
\boxed{n_{\text{pass}} \;=\; 8 \;\text{passes}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Cycle Time Computation

**Problem:** A robot must paint a car door. The total programmed path length is `L_path = 4500 mm`. The gun traverse speed is `v_g = 600 mm/s`. The trigger must be pulsed **6 times** during the cycle to avoid painting the window cutout, each pulse having a response time `t_trig = 80 ms`. Calculate the **total cycle time** `T_c`.

### Step 1 — Compute Travel Time
The continuous motion time is the path length divided by the gun speed:

$$
t_{\text{travel}} \;=\; \frac{L_{\text{path}}}{v_g} \;=\; \frac{4500 \;\text{mm}}{600 \;\text{mm/s}} \;=\; 7.5 \;\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Trigger Delay Contribution
There are 6 trigger operations, each incurring an 80 ms delay. Convert to seconds:

$$
t_{\text{trig,total}} \;=\; n_{\text{trig}} \cdot t_{\text{trig}} \;=\; 6 \cdot 0.080 \;\text{s} \;=\; 0.48 \;\text{s}
$$

### Step 3 — Sum Both Contributions
The total cycle time is the sum of motion time and trigger dead time:

$$
T_c \;=\; t_{\text{travel}} + t_{\text{trig,total}} \;=\; 7.5 + 0.48
$$

$$
\boxed{T_c \;=\; 7.98 \;\text{s}}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** In real paint booths, this cycle time of ~8 seconds determines the line throughput. Trigger delays (~6% of cycle here) are non-negligible and high-end robots use **fast solenoid valves** (response < 20 ms) to minimize them.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture of Robotic Spray Painting Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Operator HMI Teach Pendant] --> node2[Robot Controller CNC]
    node2 --> node3[Servo Drive Amplifiers]
    node3 --> node4[6 Axis Articulated Arm]
    node4 --> node5[End Effector Spray Gun]
    node5 --> node6[Atomizing Air Supply 3 bar]
    node5 --> node7[Paint Reservoir Pressurized]
    node5 --> node8[Fan Air Pattern Shaping Jets]
    node9[Electrostatic Generator negative 60 kV] --> node5
    node10[Workpiece Conveyor Grounded] --> node11[Workpiece Car Door Panel]
    node4 --> node11
    node12[Exhaust Ventilation Booth] --> node5
    node13[Safety Light Curtain] --> node2
    node14[Fume Scrubber Water Wash] --> node12
    node2 --> node15[Path Trajectory Program]
```

## 4.2 Spray Atomization Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Liquid Paint In] --> nodeB[High Pressure Pump 100 bar]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Fine Orifice Nozzle 0.3 mm]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sudden Pressure Drop]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Liquid Sheet Disintegrates]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Droplet Cloud Forms]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Fan Air Jets Shape Pattern]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Elliptical Fan Pattern Deposits on Workpiece]
    nodeI[Electrostatic Charge negative 60 kV] --> nodeF
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Wet Film Thickness 15 to 35 micrometre]
```

## 4.3 Path Planning Strategy for Spray Painting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[CAD Model of Workpiece] --> nodeStep1[Generate Surface Mesh Triangulation]
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2[Extract Surface Normal Vectors]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3[Offset Surface by Standoff h 200 mm]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4[Generate Parallel Tool Paths]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[Apply Overlap Ratio 50 percent]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6[Optimize Path Sequence TSP]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7[Output Robot Program APT or Native]
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8[Simulate Offline in 3D Software]
    nodeStep8 --> nodeStep9{Simulation Pass}
    nodeStep9 --> nodeStep10[Download to Robot Controller]
    nodeStep10 --> nodeStep11[Production Run Dry Test]
    nodeStep11 --> nodeStep12[Wet Test with Paint on Dummy]
    nodeStep12 --> nodeStep13[Production Approval]
```

## 4.4 End-Effector Spray Gun Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeG1[Paint Inlet Hose] --> nodeG2[Fluid Needle Valve]
    nodeG2 --> nodeG3[Fluid Nozzle Orifice]
    nodeG4[Atomizing Air Inlet] --> nodeG5[Air Cap Horn Jets]
    nodeG6[Fan Air Inlet] --> nodeG7[Air Cap Side Jets]
    nodeG3 --> nodeG8[Spray Pattern Elliptical Fan]
    nodeG5 --> nodeG8
    nodeG7 --> nodeG8
    nodeG9[Trigger Solenoid 24 VDC] --> nodeG2
    nodeG10[Electrostatic Electrode Ring] --> nodeG8
    nodeG11[Gun Body Aluminum] --> nodeG2
    nodeG11 --> nodeG5
    nodeG11 --> nodeG7
```

## 4.5 Overlap Coverage Pattern Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[Pass 1 Width Wf] --> nodeO1[Overlap Region 50 percent]
    nodeP2[Pass 2 Width Wf] --> nodeO1
    nodeO1 --> nodeP3[Pass 3 Width Wf]
    nodeP3 --> nodeO2[Overlap Region 50 percent]
    nodeP4[Pass 4 Width Wf] --> nodeO2
    nodeO2 --> nodeP5[Pass 5 Width Wf]
    nodeP5 --> nodeEnd[Uniform Coating Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The mermaid flowcharts above map the **functional architecture** of (a) the entire paint cell, (b) the fluid atomization sequence, (c) the offline-programming workflow, (d) the spray gun component hierarchy, and (e) the overlap pattern geometry. Each block represents a subsystem whose interaction produces a uniform painted surface.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define "Transfer Efficiency" in the context of robotic spray painting. Why is electrostatic spraying preferred for metallic paints?**

**Model Answer:**

Transfer efficiency is the ratio (expressed as a percentage) of the mass of paint actually deposited on the workpiece to the total mass of paint atomized by the spray gun.

$$
\eta_t \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{deposited}}}{m_{\text{sprayed}}} \times 100
$$

For conventional air spray, `η_t ≈ 30–40 %`. For electrostatic spray, `η_t ≈ 70–90 %`. Electrostatic spraying is preferred for **metallic paints** because:
1. Metallic flakes in the paint are dielectric and must be attracted to the grounded workpiece — otherwise gravity causes them to settle unevenly.
2. The electrostatic field orients the flakes parallel to the surface, producing the characteristic *metallic sparkle* and *flop effect*.
3. The wrap-around effect coats recessed areas (door handle pockets, wheel arches) that mechanical spray cannot reach.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four advantages of using a robot for spray painting over a human operator.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Consistent film thickness** — robots maintain exact standoff distance, gun attitude, and traverse speed; humans cannot sustain this for long durations.
2. **Worker safety** — operators are removed from exposure to toxic isocyanates and VOC fumes (legal compliance with OSHA / Factory Act norms).
3. **Higher transfer efficiency** — electrostatic robots achieve up to **90%**, saving 50–60% paint cost versus manual spraying.
4. **Higher productivity** — robot cycle times are 30–50% shorter and production runs for 20+ years without fatigue.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation**, each Module question of 14 marks offers an **internal choice**: students answer **either Question A OR Question B**. Both options are presented below.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a robotic spray painting cell**, identifying the role of each major subsystem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A robotic spray gun atomizes paint at a flow rate of `Q_p = 1800 mm³/s`. The gun is moved at `v_g = 600 mm/s` at a standoff distance `h = 200 mm` from a flat workpiece. The fan pattern half-angle is `α = 15°`. The paint contains **60% volume solids**. Determine:
- (i) the fan pattern width `W_f` on the workpiece,
- (ii) the wet film thickness per pass,
- (iii) the dry film thickness per pass,
- (iv) the number of passes required to achieve a specified dry film thickness of 50 µm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks Architecture:**

A spray painting cell has 6 functional subsystems. Refer to Section 4.1 diagram above.

1. **Robot Manipulator (6-axis articulated)** — provides XYZ + 3 rotations for gun attitude control. **(1 mark)**
2. **End-Effector Spray Gun** — atomizes, shapes, and deposits paint; includes fluid nozzle, air cap, electrostatic electrode. **(1 mark)**
3. **Paint Delivery System** — pressurized reservoir, regulator, hose, fluid filter. **(1 mark)**
4. **Compressed Air Supply** — atomizing air (3 bar), fan/shaping air (2 bar), regulated and filtered. **(1 mark)**
5. **Electrostatic Generator** — high-voltage DC source (–30 to –100 kV) for charged-droplet attraction. **(1 mark)**
6. **Safety & Ventilation** — booth enclosure, exhaust fan, fume scrubber, light curtain interlocks, fire suppression. **(1 mark)**
7. **Controller & HMI** — robot controller stores the path program; HMI allows operator to load programs and monitor cycle. **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical:**

**(i) Fan Pattern Width:**
Using the geometry formula:

$$
W_f \;=\; 2 h \tan(\alpha) \;=\; 2 \cdot 200 \cdot \tan(15^\circ)
$$

Since `tan(15°) = 0.2679`:

$$
W_f \;=\; 400 \cdot 0.2679 \;=\; 107.18 \;\text{mm}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Wet Film Thickness:**
Using the film formula:

$$
t_{\text{film}} \;=\; \frac{Q_p}{v_g \cdot W_f} \;=\; \frac{1800}{600 \cdot 107.18}
$$

$$
t_{\text{film}} \;=\; \frac{1800}{64308} \;=\; 0.02799 \;\text{mm} \;=\; 27.99 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Dry Film Thickness:**
Multiply by volume solids fraction `V_s = 0.60`:

$$
t_{\text{DFT,per pass}} \;=\; t_{\text{film}} \cdot V_s \;=\; 27.99 \cdot 0.60 \;=\; 16.79 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**(iv) Number of Passes:**

$$
n_{\text{pass}} \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{t_{\text{DFT,required}}}{t_{\text{DFT,per pass}}} \right\rceil \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{50}{16.79} \right\rceil \;=\; \lceil 2.978 \rceil \;=\; 3 \;\text{passes}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative]`

**(a)** Compare **Air Spray, Airless Spray, and Electrostatic Spray** techniques in terms of atomization principle, transfer efficiency, finish quality, and typical application. Use a comparison table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **critical process parameters** that must be controlled in a robotic spray painting operation. Explain how each parameter affects the coating quality. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Air Spray (Conventional) | Airless (Hydraulic) | Electrostatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atomization Principle | High-velocity air shears liquid stream | High-pressure liquid forced through small orifice (no air) | Air/shear atomization + electrostatic charging of droplets |
| Atomizing Air Pressure | 2–4 bar | None (hydraulic 100–250 bar paint pressure) | 2–3 bar + 30–100 kV electrostatic |
| Transfer Efficiency | 30–40 % | 45–55 % | 70–90 % |
| Finish Quality | Very high (fine droplets) | Moderate to good | Excellent (wrap-around) |
| Droplet Size | 5–50 µm | 50–200 µm | 10–80 µm |
| Suitable Paint Viscosity | Low to medium | Medium to high (gel coats, primers) | Low to medium (must be dielectric) |
| Typical Application | Automotive topcoats, clear coats | Primers, adhesives, high-viscosity industrial paints | Automotive basecoats, clear coats, metallic paints |
| Overspray | High | Moderate | Very low |
| Cost of Equipment | Low | Medium | High |

**Part (b) — Critical Process Parameters (7 Marks):**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often *list* parameters but fail to explain the **mechanism of influence**. Each parameter below must be tied to its *effect on coating*.

1. **Standoff Distance `h`** — if too small, paint pools and runs; if too large, droplets dry in flight (orange peel). Optimal: **150–250 mm**. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Gun Traverse Speed `v_g`** — too slow → sagging; too fast → dry spray and stripes. Inversely proportional to film thickness. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Atomizing Air Pressure** — too high → fine droplets, low transfer efficiency (overspray), slow drying; too low → coarse droplets, orange peel. **(1 mark)**

4. **Gun Attitude Angle** — must be **≤ 5° from surface normal**. Tilting distorts the fan pattern into teardrop shape, causing uneven film on edges. **(1 mark)**

5. **Paint Flow Rate `Q_p`** — must match gun speed and pattern width to give target film thickness; over-supply causes runs; under-supply causes starved coating. **(1 mark)**

6. **Trigger Timing** — on/off transitions must be precise to avoid overspray past part edges; response time must be **< 50 ms**. **(0.5 mark)**

7. **Environmental Factors** — booth temperature (20–25 °C), humidity (60–70% RH), and airflow velocity (0.3–0.5 m/s) affect drying and overspray behavior. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Spray Painting Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — mixing mm and µm without explicit conversion. *Always state the unit at every step.*
> 2. **Forgetting Volume Solids** — dry film thickness is NOT equal to wet film thickness; multiply by solids fraction.
> 3. **Ignoring Pass Rounding** — number of passes must be the **ceiling** (next integer), not rounded down. 2.978 passes → 3, not 2.
> 4. **Standoff ≠ Pattern Width** — many students confuse the gun-to-workpiece distance `h` with the on-part fan width `W_f`; they are related by `W_f = 2h tan α`, not equal.
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams** — 7-mark architecture questions demand a *neat labelled block diagram*. Skipping the diagram costs at least 2 marks.
> 6. **Overlap Confusion** — overlap is the fraction of common area, NOT the offset. `R_ov = 0.50` means offset is `W_f/2`, not `W_f`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- **Definition:** Robotic spray painting = programmed multi-axis robot + atomizing spray gun end-effector depositing coating on workpiece.
- **Four Atomization Methods:** Air (3 bar), Airless (200 bar paint), Electrostatic (–60 kV), Rotary Bell (high-speed bell for automotive OEM).
- **Five Key Process Parameters:** Standoff `h` (200 mm), Gun Speed `v_g` (500 mm/s), Air Pressure (3 bar), Pattern Width `W_f`, Fan Angle `α` (15°).
- **Three Master Equations:**
  - Fan width: $W_f = 2h \tan\alpha$
  - Film thickness: $t = Q_p / (v_g W_f)$
  - Overlap: $R_{ov} = 1 - \Delta y / W_f$
- **Transfer Efficiency Order:** Air (30%) < Airless (50%) < Electrostatic (85%).
- **Five Robot Advantages:** Uniformity, Safety, Efficiency, Throughput, Complex Geometry.
- **Overlap Standard:** 30–50% (offset = half pattern width).
- **Trigger Response Time:** < 50 ms.
- **Standoff Range:** 100–250 mm (industry standard).
- **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_c = L_{\text{path}}/v_g + n_{\text{trig}} \cdot t_{\text{trig}}$.
- **Dry Film Thickness:** $t_{\text{DFT}} = t_{\text{wet}} \times V_s$ (volume solids fraction).
- **Pass Count:** Always round **UP** to next integer.
- **Gun Attitude:** Maintain perpendicular (`θ ≤ 5°`) to avoid pattern distortion.
- **Safety:** Booth ventilation + electrostatic grounding of workpiece + light curtain interlocks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spot-Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Spot-Welding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Spot Welding** is a resistance welding process in which two or more metal sheets are joined together by the localized application of heat and pressure at discrete points. The heat is generated by passing a high-amperage electric current (typically **3,000 A to 20,000 A**) through the workpiece stack-up for a very short duration (**0.1 s to 2.0 s**), causing the metal at the interface to melt and fuse, forming a solid-state or fusion **weld nugget**. In robotic automation, spot welding is performed by an articulated robot arm carrying a **welding gun (gun-as-end-effector)**, which the robot positions over the workpiece to deposit a series of individual spot welds along a seam. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Spot Welding):** > Spot welding is the most widely used robotic welding process in the **automotive body-in-white (BIW) assembly line**, accounting for approximately **90% of all welds** in a typical car body. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to understand the *process parameters, gun design, robot kinematics, and seam-tracking requirements* unique to this process. > [!NOTE] > **Key Process Variables (KTU-Standard Notation):** > - **I** = Welding current (kA) — primary heat source > - **t** = Weld time (cycles or seconds) > - **F** = Electrode force (N or kgf) > - **R** = Interface resistance (μΩ) > - **d** = Weld nugget diameter (mm) > - **s** = Sheet thickness (mm) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Press-and-Sizzle" Sandwich Imagine holding two cold buttered slices of bread (the two metal sheets) together with a pair of hot tongs (the **electrodes**). The tongs squeeze the bread with a firm, steady pressure (the **electrode force F**). At the same instant, a burst of high electric current flows *only through the point where the tongs touch the bread* (the **contact interface**), instantly heating that small zone so much that the bread melts and fuses into one piece. When the tongs release and the current stops, the melted zone cools and solidifies — leaving a tiny, strong "dot" joining the two slices. That "dot" is the **weld nugget**. The tongs are the **spot-welding electrodes**; the act of squeezing is the **electrode force**; the burst of heat is **I²R heating**; and the tongs being moved from one spot to another by your hand is the **robot motion**. ## 1.3 Why Robots Perform Spot Welding - **Repetitive, high-volume welds** (a car body needs ~ 4,000–5,000 spot welds). - **Hazardous environment** — high current, heat, sparks, fumes, electromagnetic fields. - **Precision placement** — robot repeatability ±0.1 mm over thousands of cycles. - **Multi-axis access** — articulated 6-axis robots can reach into car cavities a human cannot. - **Consistent weld quality** — closed-loop adaptive control of current, force, and time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constant to Remember (Bold per protocol):** The fundamental physics law governing spot welding is **Joule's First Law of Heating**: the heat generated is proportional to the **square of the current**, the **resistance**, and the **time** of current flow. This is the single most testable equation in KTU examinations on this topic. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of Spot Welding — Step-by-Step Logic Spot welding can be decomposed into **four sequential sub-processes** that occur within a fraction of a second: 1. **Squeeze Stage** — The robot-driven gun closes, electrodes press the two sheets together with force **F**, establishing good metal-to-metal contact and crushing any surface oxides/insulating coatings. *No current flows yet.* 2. **Weld (Heat) Stage** — A high-ampere current **I** is pulsed through the electrode-workpiece-electrode path. Because the **highest electrical resistance in the path is at the sheet-to-sheet interface** (R_interface > R_bulk), most of the I²R heat is generated right at the faying surface, melting the metal into a molten pool called the **weld nugget**. 3. **Hold (Forge) Stage** — Current is switched off, but the electrode force **F** is maintained. The molten nugget solidifies under pressure, producing a fine-grained, porosity-free fusion zone with a surrounding **heat-affected zone (HAZ)**. 4. **Off / Index Stage** — Electrodes retract; the robot moves to the next programmed weld coordinate, and the cycle repeats. > [!NOTE] > **Why the interface gets hottest (the "Why" behind the process):** > The total resistance between the two electrodes is the *sum* of: 2 × (electrode-to-sheet contact resistance) + 2 × (bulk sheet resistance) + 1 × (sheet-to-sheet interface contact resistance). Of these, the **interface contact resistance** is roughly 5–10× larger than the others because of microscopic asperities and oxide films. Hence, by Ohm's law, **I²R** dissipation is concentrated exactly where we want the nugget to form. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Engineering Use | |---|--------------------|----------------|-------|-----------------| | 1 | **Joule Heat Generated** | $Q = I^{2}\,R\,t$ | Joules (J) | Heat input at the weld interface | | 2 | **Heat required to form nugget** | $Q_{req} = m\,(c\,\Delta T + L)$ | J | Minimum energy to melt the volume | | 3 | **Weld Current vs Sheet Thickness** (Steel) | $I \approx 8000\sqrt{s}$ to $10000\sqrt{s}$ | A, s in mm | Empirical sizing rule | | 4 | **Weld Time vs Sheet Thickness** | $t \approx (10\text{ to }20) \times s$ | cycles (1 cycle = 1/60 s) | Empirical sizing rule | | 5 | **Electrode Force vs Sheet Thickness** | $F \approx 2000\text{ to }4000 \times s$ | N, s in mm | Prevents expulsion (splash) | | 6 | **Minimum Nugget Diameter** | $d_{min} = 4\sqrt{s} + 1.5$ | mm, s in mm | AWS / ISO quality threshold | | 7 | **Nugget Tensile Shear Load** | $P = \dfrac{\pi\,d^{2}}{4}\,\tau_{shear}$ | N | Joint strength check | | 8 | **Ohm's Law (primary loop)** | $V = I\,R$ | V, A, Ω | Secondary voltage sizing | | 9 | **Power dissipated at nugget** | $P_{nugget} = I^{2}\,R_{interface}$ | W | Heat-rate calculation | | 10 | **Robot Weld Pitch (typical BIW)** | $p = 25\text{ to }50$ | mm | Body-in-white design rule | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Alert:** All empirical formulae above use **sheet thickness *s* in millimetres**. Plugging *s* in metres will produce a wildly wrong answer — this is a *very common* error in KTU answer scripts and is penalised. ## 2.3 The Three Control Variables (the KTU "Iron Triangle") Every spot-weld quality metric is a function of the three primary parameters: - **Current (I)** — Too low → cold, weak, undersized nugget (stick-weld). Too high → metal **expulsion** (splash), porosity, electrode pitting. - **Time (t)** — Too short → incomplete fusion. Too long → excessive HAZ, electrode mushrooming, sheet burn-through. - **Force (F)** — Too low → arcing, splash, voids. Too high → indentations, under-thickness, electrode tip flattening. These three are **strongly coupled** — changing one requires re-tuning the others. Modern robotic spot-welding controllers use **adaptive closed-loop control**, where the controller measures the *dynamic resistance* curve during the weld and adjusts I, t, or F in real time to compensate for sheet-thickness variation, coating changes, or electrode wear. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Application | Why Spot Welding? | |----------|-------------|-------------------| | **Automotive (BIW)** | Car body panels, door frames, floor pans | Speed, no filler metal, automation-friendly | | **Home Appliances** | Washing-machine drums, refrigerator cabinets | Mass production | | **Sheet Metal Fabrication** | Enclosures, cabinets | Low-cost joining | | **Aerospace (selective)** | Secondary structural panels, ducting | Where applicable alloys permit | | **Battery Manufacturing** | EV battery pack tabs & busbars | Precise, low-heat-input joining | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Heat Generated in the Weld Nugget (Joule's Law) We derive the governing heat-input equation from first principles using Ohm's Law and the definition of electrical energy. **Starting Point:** A DC current *I* flows for time *t* through a resistance *R*. The electrical power dissipated is: $$P = V \cdot I$$ By Ohm's law, the voltage across the resistance is $V = I\,R$. Substituting: $$P = I \cdot R \cdot I = I^{2}\,R$$ This is the instantaneous power (Watts) converted to heat at the interface. Since current is held essentially constant during the weld pulse, the total **heat generated** *Q* in time *t* is: $$\begin{aligned} Q &= P \cdot t \\ &= I^{2}\,R \cdot t \\ \therefore Q &= I^{2}\,R\,t \quad \text{(Joules)} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** Doubling the welding current **quadruples** the heat. Doubling the time only doubles the heat. This is why KTU examiners love the statement: *"Current is the dominant control variable in spot welding."* --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Minimum Energy Required to Form a Weld Nugget The nugget is a cylindrical molten volume of diameter *d* and height ≈ *2s* (passing through two sheets of thickness *s*). The mass of the molten metal is: $$\begin{aligned} m &= \rho \cdot V_{nugget} \\ &= \rho \cdot \frac{\pi\,d^{2}}{4}\,(2s) \end{aligned}$$ where $\rho$ is the density of steel (≈ 7850 kg/m³). The energy required to take this mass from ambient temperature $T_0$ to the melting point $T_m$ and then fully melt it is: $$\begin{aligned} Q_{req} &= m \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0) \;+\; m \cdot L \\ &= m\bigl[c\,(T_m - T_0) + L\bigr] \end{aligned}$$ where: - $c$ = specific heat (≈ 460 J/(kg·K) for steel) - $L$ = latent heat of fusion (≈ 272,000 J/kg for steel) - $T_m - T_0$ ≈ 1500 − 25 = 1475 K **Substituting and simplifying** the energy balance (assuming no losses, ideal case): $$\begin{aligned} I^{2}\,R\,t \;=\; \rho\,\frac{\pi d^{2}}{2}\,s\,\bigl[c\,(T_m - T_0) + L\bigr] \end{aligned}$$ This is the **idealised energy balance** — in practice, heat losses to the electrodes, water-cooling, and radiation account for **40–60%** of the total input, so the actual required current is higher. KTU problems sometimes include a *thermal efficiency* $\eta$ (typically 0.5–0.8) on the LHS. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Empirical Weld Current as a Function of Sheet Thickness For low-carbon steel sheets, AWS C1.1 and ISO 5821 provide the empirical rule: $$I \;=\; k \cdot \sqrt{s}$$ where *k* is an empirical constant (kA per √mm) — typically **8 to 10 kA** for standard projection welding. **Derivation of the √s dependence** (dimensional / physical argument): Heat required to melt the nugget scales with the *volume* of the nugget, which itself scales as $d^{2}\,s$. For acceptable weld geometry, $d \propto \sqrt{s}$. Substituting: $$\begin{aligned} Q_{req} \;\propto\; d^{2}\,s \;\propto\; (\sqrt{s})^{2}\,s \;=\; s^{2} \end{aligned}$$ Since $Q = I^{2}\,R\,t$ and the interface resistance $R \propto 1/s$ (thicker sheet → lower contact resistance), with time *t* also held proportional to *s*: $$\begin{aligned} I^{2} \;\propto\; \frac{Q_{req}}{R\,t} \;\propto\; \frac{s^{2}}{(1/s)\cdot s} \;=\; s \\ \therefore I \;\propto\; \sqrt{s} \end{aligned}$$ Hence the empirical square-root law is *physically justifiable* — a common KTU 14-mark sub-question. --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Minimum Nugget Diameter (Quality Criterion) AWS and KTU-adopted standards specify that the minimum acceptable nugget diameter for a single spot weld in low-carbon steel of thickness *s* (mm) is: $$\begin{aligned} d_{min} \;=\; 4\sqrt{s} \;+\; 1.5 \quad \text{(mm)} \end{aligned}$$ **Numerical Check (s = 1 mm):** $$\begin{aligned} d_{min} &= 4\sqrt{1} + 1.5 \\ &= 4(1) + 1.5 \\ &= 5.5 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$ **Numerical Check (s = 2 mm):** $$\begin{aligned} d_{min} &= 4\sqrt{2} + 1.5 \\ &= 4(1.414) + 1.5 \\ &= 5.656 + 1.5 \\ &= 7.156 \text{ mm} \approx 7.2 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation V — Joint Shear Strength of a Single Spot Weld The static shear load *P* a single spot weld can carry is governed by shear failure of the nugget in single-plane shear: $$\begin{aligned} A_{shear} &= \frac{\pi\,d^{2}}{4} \\ P &= A_{shear}\cdot \tau_{allow} \\ \therefore P &= \frac{\pi\,d^{2}}{4}\,\tau_{allow} \quad \text{(N)} \end{aligned}$$ For low-carbon steel nugget, $\tau_{allow} \approx 200 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$ (a commonly tabulated value). **Numerical Example:** For a nugget of *d* = 6 mm in 1.2 mm sheet: $$\begin{aligned} A_{shear} &= \frac{\pi (6)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 36}{4} = 28.27 \text{ mm}^{2} \\ P &= 28.27 \times 200 = 5654 \text{ N} \approx 5.65 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **single-weld load capacity** — a car's body panel may carry 50–100 such welds in parallel. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Robot Spot-Welding Parameter Trade-offs | Parameter ↑ | Nugget Size | Penetration | Indentation | Electrode Wear | Splash Risk | Joint Strength | |-------------|------------|-------------|-------------|----------------|-------------|----------------| | Current *I* ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | – | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ to a max, then ↓ | | Time *t* ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | – | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ to a max, then ↓ | | Force *F* ↑ | ↓ (initially) | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (eliminates voids) | | Tip diameter ↑ | ↓ current density | shallow | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | | Sheet thickness ↑ | needs ↑I, ↑F, ↑t | – | – | – | – | ↑ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall (Examiner's Note):** Increasing **all three** parameters simultaneously does *not* improve weld quality — it leads to **expulsion, burn-through, and rapid electrode mushrooming**. Optimisation is a coupled, non-linear problem solved in practice by **design of experiments (DOE)** and **adaptive resistance-welding controllers** (e.g., Bosch Rexroth PSQ, Medar TPC). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram A — Physical Build-up of a Spot Weld (Cross-Section) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A["UPPER ELECTRODE (Water-Cooled Cu Alloy Tip)"] UE["Cu Electrode
Dome Radius 50-100 mm
Force F Applied"] end subgraph B["WORKPIECE STACK"] S1["Sheet 1 (thickness s)"] S2["Sheet 2 (thickness s)"] end subgraph C["FUSION ZONE DETAIL"] NUG["Weld Nugget
Diameter d
Penetration 2s"] HAZ["Heat Affected Zone
HAZ Ring"] PM["Parent Metal
Unaffected"] end subgraph D["LOWER ELECTRODE"] LE["Cu Electrode
Return Path
Water Cooled"] end ARROW1["Current I flows
3-20 kA"] -.-> UE UE --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> LE LE -.-> ARROW1 UE -->|"Force F"| S1 S2 -->|"Reaction F"| LE S1 --- NUG S2 --- NUG NUG --- HAZ HAZ --- PM ``` ## 4.2 Diagram B — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robotic Spot-Welding Cell ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CTRL["ROBOT CONTROLLER LAYER"] PLC["Main PLC
Safety I-O"] WLD["Weld Timer
Controller"] ADAPT["Adaptive Module
Dynamic Resistance Feedback"] end subgraph ROB["ROBOT SUBSYSTEM"] RARM["6-Axis Articulated Arm
Repeatability plus or minus 0.1 mm"] TEACH["Teach Pendant
Seam Programming"] TEACH --> RARM end subgraph GUN["WELDING GUN (END EFFECTOR)"] TRANS["Servo or Pneumatic Transformer
50 Hz MFDC or AC"] EL1["Upper Electrode
Domed Tip"] EL2["Lower Electrode
Domed Tip"] FORCE["Electrode Force Actuator
F 2000 to 6000 N"] COOL["Water Cooling Manifold
4 to 8 L per min"] end subgraph WP["WORKPIECE STATION"] FIXT["Weld Fixture
Clamp and Locate"] PART["Sheet Metal Assembly
Body in White"] end PLC --> WLD WLD --> ADAPT ADAPT --> TRANS PLC --> RARM RARM --> GUN TRANS --> EL1 EL2 --> TRANS FORCE --> EL1 FIXT --> PART EL1 --> PART EL2 --> PART COOL --> EL1 COOL --> EL2 ``` ## 4.3 Diagram C — Process Flow of a Single Spot-Weld Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB START([Robot Reaches Coordinate XY]) --> SQZ["SQUEEZE STAGE
Electrodes close
Apply Force F
Duration 50 to 200 ms"] SQZ --> WLD["WELD STAGE
Current I pulsed
Heat I squared R t
Duration 100 to 500 ms"] WLD --> HOLD["HOLD STAGE
Current off
Force F maintained
Duration 50 to 200 ms"] HOLD --> RET["RETRACT STAGE
Electrodes open
Robot moves to next point"] RET --> QUAL{"Adaptive
Quality
Check"} QUAL -->|"Pass"| NEXT["Index to next weld point"] QUAL -->|"Fail"| ALARM["Alarm and Log
Re-weld or Reject"] NEXT --> START ALARM --> NEXT ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define spot welding. List any four process parameters that control weld quality.** **Model Answer:** Spot welding is a resistance welding process in which two overlapping metal sheets are joined by the localised heat generated when a large electric current is passed through the interface under applied electrode pressure. The localised melt solidifies to form a weld nugget. Four controlling process parameters: 1. **Welding current (I)** — primary heat source. 2. **Weld time (t)** — duration of current flow. 3. **Electrode force (F)** — prevents splash, ensures contact. 4. **Electrode tip geometry** — affects current density and indentation. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Why is current called the dominant control variable in spot welding? Justify with reference to Joule's law.** **Model Answer:** According to Joule's law, $Q = I^{2}Rt$. The heat generated is proportional to the **square** of the current, but only linearly to time *t* and resistance *R*. Therefore, a small change in current produces a much larger change in heat input than an equivalent fractional change in time. This squared dependence makes current the most sensitive and dominant parameter for nugget formation. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions are framed as *Module Internal Choice* — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both options below carry equal marks (typically 7 + 7 sub-parts). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper Adapted] **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **servo-driven robotic spot-welding gun**. Explain the role of each major component. (7 marks) **(b)** For spot welding two low-carbon steel sheets each of **thickness 1.5 mm**, calculate: 1. the recommended welding current (use $I = 9000\sqrt{s}$), 2. the weld time (use $t = 15 \times s$ cycles, 1 cycle = 1/60 s), 3. the minimum nugget diameter (use $d_{min} = 4\sqrt{s} + 1.5$ mm), 4. the joint shear load (use $\tau_{allow} = 200$ N/mm²). (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Construction of a Servo-Operated Spot-Welding Gun:** The gun is mounted on the robot's wrist flange and consists of the following: | Component | Function | |-----------|----------| | **Welding Transformer** | Steps down mains voltage to a few volts, supplies kA-level current (AC, MFDC, or DC). | | **Servo Motor & Ball-Screw Actuator** | Provides programmable, repeatable electrode force (replaces legacy pneumatic cylinders). | | **Upper and Lower Electrodes** | Cu-alloy (RWMA Class 2) water-cooled tips; concentrate current and apply force at point. | | **Throat / Frame** | 'C'-type or X-type rigid arm that transmits force from actuator to electrodes. | | **Water-Cooling Manifold** | Circulates chilled water through tips to prevent overheating and mushrooming. | | **Weld Timer Card** | Synchronises squeeze, weld, hold, and off timings; interfaces with robot I/O. | | **Tip-Dressing Sensor** | Monitors electrode wear; triggers auto-dressing or alarms. | **Working:** The robot positions the gun tips over the weld point. The PLC sends a fire command → servo motor closes the gun with force *F* (squeeze) → weld timer switches on the SCR/IGBT for time *t* → current pulse I²Rt → nugget forms → current off, hold pressure maintained → electrodes retract → robot indexes to next point. #### Model Solution — Part (b) Given: *s* = 1.5 mm. **Step 1 — Welding Current:** $$\begin{aligned} I &= 9000 \times \sqrt{1.5} \\ &= 9000 \times 1.2247 \\ &= 11022 \text{ A} \approx 11.02 \text{ kA} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Weld Time:** $$\begin{aligned} t_{cycles} &= 15 \times s = 15 \times 1.5 = 22.5 \text{ cycles} \\ t_{sec} &= \frac{22.5}{60} = 0.375 \text{ s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Minimum Nugget Diameter:** $$\begin{aligned} d_{min} &= 4\sqrt{1.5} + 1.5 \\ &= 4(1.2247) + 1.5 \\ &= 4.899 + 1.5 \\ &= 6.399 \text{ mm} \approx 6.4 \text{ mm} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Joint Shear Load:** $$\begin{aligned} A_{shear} &= \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (6.4)^{2}}{4} = 32.17 \text{ mm}^{2} \\ P &= 32.17 \times 200 = 6434 \text{ N} \approx 6.43 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper Adapted] **(a)** Derive the relationship $Q = I^{2}Rt$ for heat generated in spot welding. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain with diagrams the **four sequential stages** of the robotic spot-weld cycle. For each stage, give the typical time window and the physical / electrical events occurring. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Assumptions:** 1. DC current (or RMS value for AC). 2. Resistance *R* is constant during the weld pulse. 3. Current *I* is held constant by the weld timer's closed-loop control. 4. Heat is generated only at the workpiece interface (electrode-water cooling removes heat from the electrodes, not from the nugget zone). **Derivation:** Electrical power dissipated in a resistance *R* carrying current *I* is: $$P = V \cdot I$$ By Ohm's law: $$V = I \cdot R \quad \Rightarrow \quad P = I \cdot R \cdot I = I^{2}\,R$$ Total heat in time *t*: $$Q = P \cdot t = I^{2}\,R\,t \quad \text{(Joules)}$$ **Substitution interpretation for spot welding:** $$Q_{nugget} = I^{2}\,R_{interface}\,t$$ This is the *useful* heat that goes into nugget formation. In practice, only a fraction $\eta$ (thermal efficiency, 0.5–0.8) reaches the nugget: $$Q_{nugget} = \eta \cdot I^{2}\,R\,t$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) The four stages of the spot-weld cycle (see Diagram C in Section 4) are: | Stage | Time Window | Physical / Electrical Event | |-------|-------------|------------------------------| | **1. Squeeze** | 50 – 200 ms | Electrodes close; force *F* builds; surface asperities flatten; contact resistance stabilises. No current flows. | | **2. Weld (Heat)** | 100 – 500 ms | High current *I* (3–20 kA) flows; heat $I^{2}Rt$ melts the interface; nugget grows. | | **3. Hold (Forge)** | 50 – 200 ms | Current off; force *F* maintained; nugget solidifies under pressure; grains refine; voids close. | | **4. Off / Index** | 200 – 500 ms | Electrodes retract; robot moves to next weld coordinate; cooling water flushes electrode tips. | **Key observation for KTU:** The *squeeze* and *hold* times are essential even though no current flows — they prevent expulsion, ensure metallurgical soundness, and stabilise the dynamic-resistance curve that the adaptive controller uses for quality monitoring. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Units slip-up** — Plugging sheet thickness in metres instead of millimetres in empirical formulae. (Penalty: full mark on that sub-part.) > 2. **Cycle vs second confusion** — "Weld time" in industry is often quoted in *cycles* (60 Hz mains), but KTU numerical answers may want either. Always show the conversion: 1 cycle = 1/60 s. > 3. **Forgetting thermal efficiency η** — The ideal $I^{2}Rt$ equation gives the *generated* heat, not the *useful* heat. In quantitative KTU problems, if a thermal efficiency is mentioned in the question, it **must** appear on the LHS. > 4. **Confusing nugget penetration (2s) with sheet thickness (s)** — The nugget *passes through both sheets*, so its height is approximately *2s*, not *s*. This is a frequent marker of shallow understanding. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Spot welding = Resistance welding at a point.** Heat = $I^{2}Rt$ (Joule's law). The interface has the highest resistance, so the nugget forms there. - **Three control variables:** Current (*I*), Time (*t*), Force (*F*) — strongly coupled, optimised together. - **Current dominates** because of the squared dependence. Time is a linear knob. Force prevents splash/expulsion. - **Four cycle stages:** Squeeze → Weld → Hold → Off/Index. Squeeze and Hold are *no-current* stages that are critical for quality. - **Empirical sizing rules (steel, *s* in mm):** - $I \approx 9000\sqrt{s}$ A - $t \approx 15 s$ cycles - $F \approx 3000 s$ N - $d_{min} = 4\sqrt{s} + 1.5$ mm - **Robot spot welding** uses a *welding gun as end-effector*, typically **6-axis articulated arm**, ±0.1 mm repeatability, with **servo or pneumatic** force actuation. - **Modern systems use adaptive control** — dynamic resistance feedback adjusts I, t, F in real time to compensate for sheet variation and electrode wear. - **Automotive BIW** uses ~4,000–5,000 spot welds per car body; pitch is typically 25–50 mm. - **Likely KTU question stems:** "Derive $Q = I^2Rt$", "Explain the four stages", "Calculate weld current/time/strength", "Compare servo vs pneumatic gun", "Explain adaptive control", "List advantages of robotic spot welding". - **Standards referenced in KTU:** AWS C1.1, ISO 5821, RWMA electrode-classification (Class 2 Cu-Cr for steel). - **Always write units and show the conversion of cycles → seconds** in numerical answers. - **Remember the four sub-parts of a standard KTU 14-mark answer:** (i) diagram, (ii) principle/formula, (iii) numerical, (iv) application/limitation. ---

topics.title: Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Arc Welding** is a fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is produced by heating them to the welding temperature with an electric arc established between an electrode and the workpiece (or between two electrodes). The arc serves as the intense, concentrated heat source, with temperatures at the arc core typically reaching **6,000 °C to 20,000 °C** (often quoted around **7,000 K to 20,000 K** at the plasma core). In the context of **Robotics and Automation (PEIET411)**, arc welding is one of the most commercially dominant robotic applications, accounting for approximately **20–25%** of all industrial robot installations worldwide, primarily in the automotive, shipbuilding, and structural fabrication sectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Phrasing Expected in Exams):**
> *Arc welding is a fusion welding process that uses an electric arc, struck between a consumable/non-consumable electrode and the base metal, as the source of heat to melt and join two or more metal parts together.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to melt a small piece of butter on a cold steel spoon using a lighter flame. You must hold the flame very close and steady, otherwise the heat dissipates too quickly. Now, replace the lighter with a **focused electrical lightning bolt (the arc)** and the butter with two thick steel plates. The electric arc is essentially *controlled, sustained lightning* — a stream of charged plasma particles flowing between two points of different electrical potential. 

For an engineering student, the simplest way to picture it is:
- **Electrode (positive or negative terminal)** = the "pen" that draws the weld
- **Workpiece (opposite terminal)** = the "paper" being written on
- **Electric Arc** = the intensely hot bridge of plasma (ionized gas) connecting pen to paper
- **Shielding Gas** = an invisible "umbrella" that protects the molten puddle from rain (atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen), preventing rust-like defects (oxidation, porosity)

The **robot's role** in this analogy is to act as a **flawless, tireless, geometrically perfect human hand** holding that "pen" and tracing the joint path with sub-millimeter accuracy at a constant speed.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Arc Temperature (Core) | **15,000 – 20,000** | °C |
| Arc Voltage (Typical GMAW) | **18 – 32** | Volts (V) |
| Welding Current (GMAW) | **100 – 600** | Amperes (A) |
| Argon Purity (for TIG/MIG) | **99.99%** | % |
| Travel Speed Range | **5 – 100** | mm/s |
| Deposition Efficiency | **65 – 95%** | % |
| Power Conversion Factor (η) | **0.7 – 0.85** (thermal) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** When answering KTU questions, always distinguish between **arc welding as a process** and **robotic arc welding as an automation system**. The robot is *not* a heat source — it is a *positioning and process-parameter controller* for the welding torch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Arc Formation — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The creation of a stable welding arc follows a precise electrical sequence. For the KTU 2024 scheme, you must understand the "Why" and "How" of each step:

1. **Initiation (Arc Striking):** 
   - **Why?** Air is normally an insulator (~10⁶ Ω·m); you need a path.
   - **How?** The electrode is brought close to the workpiece (contact mode in SMAW, high-frequency mode in TIG, or short-circuit mode in MIG). When the gap is small enough (~1–2 mm), the **dielectric strength of air breaks down**, and electrons begin to flow.
2. **Ionization & Plasma Formation:**
   - The high current density (~10⁶ A/m²) and electron collision with gas molecules strip electrons from atoms, creating a **plasma column** (a quasi-neutral, electrically conductive ionized gas).
3. **Heat Generation Zones:**
   - The arc has three distinct thermal zones:
     * **Cathode Region:** Emits electrons (heat = ~36% of total).
     * **Anode Region:** Receives electrons, forms a small "anode spot" (heat = ~42% of total).
     * **Arc Column (Plasma):** The luminous, resistive middle (heat = ~22% of total).
4. **Sustained Discharge:** 
   - The power source (Constant Current / Constant Voltage) maintains a stable V–I characteristic despite the constantly changing arc length as the robot moves the torch.
5. **Material Transfer (for consumable electrode processes):**
   - Modes include **Short-circuit transfer, Globular transfer, Spray transfer**, and **Pulsed spray transfer** — chosen based on current, voltage, and shielding gas.

### 2.2 Major Arc Welding Variants (KTU Favourite)

| Abbreviation | Full Name | Electrode Type | Shielding | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SMAW** | Shielded Metal Arc Welding | Consumable (flux-coated stick) | From flux coating | Field construction, repair |
| **GMAW / MIG** | Gas Metal Arc Welding | Continuous consumable wire | External inert/active gas (Ar/CO₂) | **Most common in robotics** |
| **GTAW / TIG** | Gas Tungsten Arc Welding | Non-consumable (Tungsten) | Inert gas (Argon) | Aluminium, stainless, precision |
| **SAW** | Submerged Arc Welding | Consumable wire | Granular flux blanket | Heavy plates, shipbuilding |
| **FCAW** | Flux-Cored Arc Welding | Continuous tubular consumable | Self-shielded or gas-shielded | Structural steel, outdoor work |
| **PAW** | Plasma Arc Welding | Tungsten + constricted arc | Argon + secondary gas | High-precision thin materials |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **robotic automation**, **GMAW (MIG/MAG)** dominates because of its **continuous wire feed compatibility** with automated torch manipulation. TIG is used for high-precision aluminium, and SAW for long straight seams on heavy plates.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formatting Rule Applied:** All absolute-value or magnitude operators use `\vert` instead of the pipe character `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Arc Power (Heat Source Power)** | $P_{arc} = V \cdot I$ | $V$ = arc voltage (V), $I$ = current (A) | Total electrical power into the arc |
| 2 | **Heat Input (per unit length)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ (kJ/mm) | $\eta$ = process efficiency, $S$ = travel speed (mm/min) | Most critical KTU formula — controls HAZ and distortion |
| 3 | **Heat Input (in kJ/inch)** | $HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S_{in} \cdot 1000}$ | $S_{in}$ = travel speed (in/min) | Imperial version (rare in KTU) |
| 4 | **Deposition Rate (mass-based)** | $DR = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot v_f \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_d$ | $d$ = wire diameter, $v_f$ = wire feed speed, $\rho$ = density, $\eta_d$ = deposition efficiency | Production-rate calculation |
| 5 | **Deposition Rate (empirical)** | $DR \approx k \cdot I$ | $k$ = constant, $I$ = current | Quick estimation |
| 6 | **Current Density** | $J = \dfrac{I}{A_e} = \dfrac{4I}{\pi d^2}$ | $A_e$ = electrode cross-section | Determines transfer mode |
| 7 | **Arc Length (approx.)** | $L_a \approx a + bV$ | $a,b$ = process constants | For robotic height control |
| 8 | **Welding Cost (per unit length)** | $C = \dfrac{c_e \cdot t}{L} + c_g + c_l$ | $c_e$=electrode cost, $t$=time, $L$=length, $c_g$=gas, $c_l$=labour | Economic feasibility in automation |
| 9 | **Robot Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{L_{seam}}{v_{weld}} + T_{setup}$ | $L_{seam}$=joint length, $v_{weld}$=weld speed | Robot cell design |
| 10 | **Heat-Affected Zone (rule of thumb)** | $\text{HAZ} \propto \sqrt{HI}$ | — | Predicts mechanical degradation zone |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Robotic MIG welding of car body-in-white (BIW) — 3,000–5,000 spot welds *replaced* by continuous arc welds for lighter, stiffer frames.
- **Shipbuilding:** Submerged arc welding of 20 mm-thick steel plates; single-pass vs. multi-pass heat input calculations decide quality.
- **Aerospace:** Plasma/TIG welding of thin-walled titanium and aluminium alloys, where excessive heat input causes burn-through.
- **Pipeline Construction:** Automated flux-cored arc welding (FCAW) for long-distance oil/gas pipelines using track-mounted or crawler robots.
- **Heat Input Control:** Critical in creep-resistant steels (e.g., ASTM A387) and HSLA steels where too much heat input destroys mechanical properties — the robotic system's precision is what makes this controllable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Equation in Robotic Arc Welding

This is the **single most asked derivation in the KTU Robotics & Automation module on arc welding.** We will derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the total electrical energy delivered to the arc per unit time (Power).**
The instantaneous electrical power consumed by the arc is given by the product of arc voltage and current.

$$
P_{arc} = V \cdot I
$$

where:
- $P_{arc}$ = arc power in **Watts (W)**
- $V$ = arc voltage in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = welding current in **Amperes (A)**

**Step 2 — Account for Thermal Efficiency of the Process.**
Not all electrical energy is converted to useful heat at the workpiece. A fraction is lost as radiation, spatter, and convection. The **process thermal efficiency** $\eta$ is empirically determined.

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I
$$

Typical values:
- GMAW (MIG): $\eta \approx 0.85$
- GTAW (TIG): $\eta \approx 0.60$
- SMAW (Stick): $\eta \approx 0.80$
- SAW: $\eta \approx 0.95$

**Step 3 — Convert Power to Energy per Unit Length.**
Energy $E$ delivered to the workpiece while the torch travels a small distance $dx$ at speed $S$ is:

$$
dE = P_{useful} \cdot dt = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot dt
$$

Since the torch travels distance $dx$ in time $dt$, we have $dt = \dfrac{dx}{S}$, where $S$ is the travel speed. Substituting:

$$
dE = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{dx}{S}
$$

**Step 4 — Express Heat Input per Unit Length.**
Heat input per unit length is the derivative of energy with respect to distance:

$$
HI = \dfrac{dE}{dx} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
$$

**Step 5 — Unit Conversion to kJ/mm (Standard KTU/Kincaid Form).**
Industrial standards report $HI$ in **kJ/mm** with $S$ in **mm/min** and $V, I$ in standard SI. Multiplying numerator and denominator appropriately:

$$
HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \times 1000} \;\;\; \text{[kJ/mm]}
$$

The factor 60 converts the per-minute speed to per-second, and 1000 converts Joules to kilojoules. **This is the canonical KTU form.**

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$
\boxed{HI = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}, \quad \text{where } S \text{ is in mm/min}}
$$

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example.**
A robotic GMAW cell operates at $V = 28$ V, $I = 250$ A, $S = 600$ mm/min, with $\eta = 0.85$. Find the heat input.

$$
\begin{aligned}
HI &= \dfrac{0.85 \times 28 \times 250 \times 60}{1000 \times 600} \\
   &= \dfrac{0.85 \times 28 \times 15000}{600000} \\
   &= \dfrac{357000}{600000} \\
   &= 0.595 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This value is typical for **structural steel robotic welding** (acceptable range: 0.5–1.5 kJ/mm for thin to medium plates).

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Deposition Rate from Wire Feed Parameters

Used in **robotic production rate optimization problems.**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the wire (cylindrical consumable).**

$$
A_{wire} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

where $d$ is the wire diameter.

**Step 2 — Volume of wire consumed per unit time.**

$$
\dot{V}_{wire} = A_{wire} \cdot v_f = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_f
$$

where $v_f$ is the wire feed speed (length/time).

**Step 3 — Mass deposition rate, accounting for spatter loss.**

$$
