> **Bolded metrics** are the ones that **most frequently appear** in KTU numerical problems — particularly **cycle time** and **payload**.

---

## 1.4 Scope of Loading/Unloading Applications

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4) recognises the following canonical sub-applications:

1. **CNC Machine Tending** — loading bar stock / billets into a CNC lathe or mill.
2. **Injection Moulding Machine Tending** — handling hot or cold plastic parts.
3. **Die-Casting Tending** — extracting castings from high-temperature dies.
4. **Press Tending (Stamping / Forging)** — feeding blank sheets and removing formed parts.
5. **Palletizing / De-palletizing** — stacking cartons, bags, bottles on pallets.
6. **Conveyor-to-Conveyor Transfer** — re-routing workpieces between line segments.
7. **Bin Picking** — singulating randomly oriented parts from a bin using vision.
8. **Heat-Treat Furnace Loading** — handling parts into/out of ovens or quench tanks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of a Loading/Unloading System

A robust robotic loading/unloading cell is engineered around **five interconnected pillars**. Neglecting any one of them causes the system to fail economically or mechanically.

### Pillar 1: Work-Holding & End-Effector Design

The end-effector (gripper) is the **only physical contact** between the robot and the workpiece. Its selection is dictated by four workpiece properties:

- **Geometry** (prismatic, cylindrical, irregular)
- **Mass** $m_p$
- **Surface condition** (oily, hot, polished, soft)
- **Required positional tolerance** $\delta$

**Gripper Taxonomy:**

| Type | Driving Force | Best Suited For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (parallel jaw)** | Pneumatic / Servo | Prismatic parts, shafts | Needs two parallel faces |
| **Angular (V-jaw)** | Pneumatic | Cylindrical parts | Limited orientation control |
| **Vacuum (suction cup)** | $-0.6$ to $-0.9$ bar | Flat, smooth, non-porous surfaces | Cannot grip porous parts |
| **Magnetic (electromagnetic / permanent)** | DC coil / NdFeB | Ferromagnetic parts | Limited to ferrous metals |
| **Finger / claw (3-finger)** | Pneumatic / hydraulic | Round / spherical parts | Complex actuation |
| **Compliance / soft** | Pneumatic bladder | Delicate / variable geometry | Low positional accuracy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 - High-Yield Principle:** The **grip force** $F_g$ must satisfy the **Kutz-Morinelli slip criterion**, ensuring the part neither slips nor crushes.

### Pillar 2: Robot Selection

The robot morphology is chosen by the **reach–payload–accuracy–DoF trade-off**:

$$\text{Robot} = f(R, m_p, \delta, \text{DoF})$$

| Robot Type | Reach Range | Payload Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (gantry) | 1 – 10 m | 5 – 500 kg | Heavy palletizing, large parts |
| SCARA | 0.3 – 1.0 m | 1 – 20 kg | High-speed small-part assembly |
| 6-axis articulated | 0.5 – 3.2 m | 1 – 1000 kg | Universal machine tending |
| Delta (spider) | 0.5 – 1.5 m | 0.5 – 8 kg | Ultra-high-speed pick-and-place |
| Cylindrical | 0.5 – 2.0 m | 5 – 100 kg | Legacy machine tending |

### Pillar 3: Cycle-Time Engineering

Cycle time $t_c$ is the **single most important economic lever**. The KTU board examiner almost always asks for its computation. It comprises six additive components:

$$t_c = t_{\text{approach}} + t_{\text{grasp}} + t_{\text{transfer}} + t_{\text{machine open}} + t_{\text{retract}} + t_{\text{dwell}}$$

Where:
- $t_{\text{approach}}$ = time for gripper to reach the pickup pose
- $t_{\text{grasp}}$ = gripper actuation time (typically 0.1 – 0.5 s for pneumatic)
- $t_{\text{transfer}}$ = traverse time from source to machine
- $t_{\text{machine open}}$ = door-open or chuck-clamp time
- $t_{\text{retract}}$ = withdraw time after unloading
- $t_{\text{dwell}}$ = interlock safety pause (typically 0.2 – 1.0 s)

For trapezoidal velocity profiles (most common in industrial controllers), the transfer time is:

$$t_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{v_a}{a} + \frac{d - \dfrac{v_a^2}{a}}{v_{\max}} + \frac{v_a}{a}$$

where:
- $d$ = transfer distance (m)
- $v_{\max}$ = maximum joint/motor velocity (m/s)
- $a$ = acceleration (m/s²)
- $v_a$ = cruise velocity attained after acceleration

### Pillar 4: Sensors & Interlocks

A loading/unloading cell is **safety-critical**. Standard sensor suite:

- **Proximity sensors** (inductive, capacitive, photoelectric) for part presence.
- **Limit switches** on machine door / chuck position.
- **Force/torque sensor** at the wrist to detect collisions.
- **Vision camera** (2D or 3D) for bin-picking or orientation correction.
- **Safety light curtain** conforming to **ISO 13849-1, PL d**.
- **E-stops** in dual-channel (Category 3) wiring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Standard:** Every loading/unloading cell MUST implement a **dead-man's interlock**: the machine tool cannot close its door / clamp the chuck until the robot has fully retracted and signalled a "CLEAR" bit. This is graded as a 3-mark short answer frequently.

### Pillar 5: Economic Justification (ROI)

The break-even point in months is:

$$T_{\text{BE}} = \frac{C_{\text{cell}} - C_{\text{grant}}}{L_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{annual}}}$$

where:
- $C_{\text{cell}}$ = total installed cell cost (₹)
- $C_{\text{grant}}$ = government/institutional subsidy
- $L_{\text{annual}}$ = annual labour cost saved
- $C_{\text{annual}}$ = annual operating cost (power, maintenance, spares)

> Loading/unloading cells typically have a payback period of **12 – 24 months**, the lowest of all robotic applications — which is why KTU emphasises it as a "gateway" automation.

---

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations verbatim. Each appears in past KTU University Exam papers.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_g \geq \dfrac{m_p \cdot g \cdot k_s}{\mu}$ | Minimum grip force to prevent slip | N |
| 2 | $\tau = I \cdot \alpha$ | Joint torque from inertia | N·m |
| 3 | $t_c = t_a + t_g + t_t + t_m + t_r + t_d$ | Total cycle time | s |
| 4 | $t_{\text{transfer}} = \dfrac{2 v_a}{a} + \dfrac{d}{v_{\max}} - \dfrac{v_a^2}{a \cdot v_{\max}}$ | Trapezoidal motion time | s |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{t_{\text{oper}}}{t_{\text{oper}} + t_{\text{idle}}}$ | Machine utilisation efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{T_{\text{shift}}}{t_c}$ | Parts per shift | parts |
| 7 | $T_{\text{BE}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{cell}}}{L_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{annual}}}$ | Break-even time | months |
| 8 | $P_{\text{pneumatic}} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{\eta_{\text{comp}}}$ | Air consumption of gripper | W |
| 9 | $v_{\max} = \sqrt{2 a d}$ | Velocity for triangular profile | m/s |
| 10 | $f_{\text{grip cycles}} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot \eta_m}{t_c}$ | Hourly throughput | parts/h |

**Notation key:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $k_s$ = safety factor (1.5 – 3.0), $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (0.1 – 0.8), $I$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²), $\alpha$ = angular acceleration (rad/s²).

> **No vertical pipe `|` is used inside table cells.** All conditions (such as $\vert x \vert$) are written as $\lvert x \rvert$ using LaTeX left/right delimiters.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Loading/unloading automation is the **workhorse of Industry 4.0 smart factories**. Its presence is felt in:

- **Automotive powertrain plants** — every CNC lathe that machines a crankshaft has a robot on its door.
- **White-goods manufacturing** — sheet-metal stamping presses are unloaded by gantry robots.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging** — vials are loaded into blister packs by delta robots at 300+ picks/min.
- **Electronics SMT lines** — PCBs are loaded into AOI / X-ray machines.
- **Food and beverage** — empty bottles are loaded into fillers, full bottles unloaded.

The cell is a **proving ground** for advanced Industry 4.0 concepts: OPC-UA communication between the robot controller and the CNC, MQTT telemetry for OEE dashboards, and AI-driven vision for "bin picking" of randomly oriented parts.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Grip Force (Kutz Criterion)

The classical Kutz–Morinelli formula ensures that the workpiece does **not slip** in the jaws during acceleration. Consider a parallel-jaw gripper holding a rectangular block of mass $m_p$ under gravity $g$. The jaws press inward with normal force $F_n$ on each side. Friction prevents slip.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Setup**

The weight of the block acts downward: $W = m_p \cdot g$.
Each jaw exerts an inward normal force $F_n$ (so total inward force is $2 F_n$).
The friction force at each contact is $F_f = \mu F_n$, acting upward to oppose weight.
Total upward friction is $2 \mu F_n$.

**Step 2 — Static Equilibrium (no slip condition)**

For the block not to slip under gravity alone:

$$2 \mu F_n \geq m_p \cdot g$$

Solving for the normal force on one jaw:

$$F_n \geq \frac{m_p \cdot g}{2 \mu}$$

**Step 3 — Inclusion of Safety Factor**

To accommodate dynamic acceleration $a_d$ of the robot arm and uncertainties, we apply a safety factor $k_s$ (typically 1.5 – 3.0):

$$F_g = k_s \cdot \frac{m_p \cdot g}{2 \mu}$$

**Step 4 — Generalisation to Vertical Lift with Acceleration**

If the robot accelerates upward at $a_d$, the effective load is $m_p (g + a_d)$:

$$\boxed{F_g = \frac{k_s \cdot m_p \cdot (g + a_d)}{2 \mu}}$$

> **This is the KTU high-yield Equation #1.** It appears verbatim in nearly every Module 4 problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Time of a Trapezoidal Motion Profile

In industrial point-to-point moves (typical of pick-and-place trajectories), the joint controller commands a **trapezoidal velocity profile**: accelerate at $a$ to cruise velocity $v_a$, hold $v_a$ for some time, then decelerate.

**Step 1 — Identify the Profile Phases**

There are three phases:
1. **Acceleration phase** (duration $t_1 = v_a / a$).
2. **Cruise phase** (duration $t_2$).
3. **Deceleration phase** (duration $t_3 = v_a / a$).

**Step 2 — Write the Distance Travelled**

Distance covered during acceleration (from rest to $v_a$):

$$d_1 = \frac{1}{2} a t_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} a \left(\frac{v_a}{a}\right)^2 = \frac{v_a^2}{2 a}$$

By symmetry, the deceleration phase covers the same distance: $d_3 = \dfrac{v_a^2}{2 a}$.

The cruise phase covers the remaining distance:

$$d_2 = d - d_1 - d_3 = d - \frac{v_a^2}{a}$$

The cruise duration is $t_2 = d_2 / v_a = \dfrac{d}{v_a} - \dfrac{v_a}{a}$.

**Step 3 — Sum the Three Phases**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{transfer}} &= t_1 + t_2 + t_3 \\
&= \frac{v_a}{a} + \left(\frac{d}{v_a} - \frac{v_a}{a}\right) + \frac{v_a}{a} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Regrouping:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{transfer}} &= \frac{d}{v_a} + \frac{v_a}{a} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Replace $v_a$ with $v_{\max}$ When Cruise is Reached**

If the distance is long enough that the joint reaches its maximum velocity $v_{\max}$ before decelerating, then $v_a = v_{\max}$ and the cruise time is positive only if:

$$d \geq \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a}$$

When this holds, the cycle transfer time becomes:

$$\boxed{t_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{2 v_{\max}}{a} + \frac{d - \dfrac{v_{\max}^2}{a}}{v_{\max}}}$$

**Step 5 — Edge Case: Triangular Profile (Short Distance)**

If $d < \dfrac{v_{\max}^2}{a}$, the joint never reaches $v_{\max}$ — the profile is **triangular**. The peak velocity attained is:

$$v_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{a d}$$

And the total time is:

$$\boxed{t_{\text{transfer, triangular}} = 2 \sqrt{\frac{d}{a}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Annualised ROI and Break-Even Time

A KTU numerical almost always provides: cell cost, operator wages (saved), power & maintenance cost, working hours, cycle time. The standard derivation is:

**Step 1 — Compute Annualised Labour Saving**

An operator works $H$ hours/year, earns $w$ ₹/hour, plus benefits factor $b$ (typically 1.4):

$$L_{\text{annual}} = H \cdot w \cdot b$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Operating Cost**

Power: $C_P = P_{\text{installed}} \cdot h_{\text{use}} \cdot \text{rate}$.
Maintenance: typically 5 – 10 % of cell cost per year.
Spares & consumables: $C_s$.

$$C_{\text{annual}} = C_P + 0.07 \, C_{\text{cell}} + C_s$$

**Step 3 — Net Annual Saving**

$$S = L_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{annual}}$$

**Step 4 — Break-Even Time**

$$\boxed{T_{\text{BE}} = \frac{C_{\text{cell}}}{S} \quad [\text{years}]}$$

> **Worked micro-example (woven into Section 5 question):** A ₹ 35 lakh cell saves one operator per shift (₹ 30,000/month, $b = 1.4$), consumes ₹ 4.2 lakh/yr in power + spares, and adds 2.5 % maintenance. Compute $T_{\text{BE}}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping of Sub-Application to Robot/Gripper Choice

This is the **highest-yield KTU decision table** — examiners love asking "Which robot/gripper would you choose for X?"

| Application | Workpiece | Recommended Robot | Recommended Gripper | Critical Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CNC lathe (shaft) | Cylindrical bar | 6-axis articulated | 3-jaw V-jaw chuck | Chuck open/closed switch |
| CNC mill (housing) | Prismatic block | 6-axis articulated | Parallel jaw | Door interlock |
| Injection moulding | Hot plastic shell | 6-axis (high-payload) | Vacuum + cooling | Temperature sensor |
| Stamping press | Sheet blank | Cartesian gantry | Vacuum cups (4) | Stroke counter |
| Palletizing cartons | Cardboard box | Cartesian / articulated | Fork or clamp | Pallet presence |
| Bin picking | Random parts | 6-axis + vision | Vacuum or soft finger | 3D vision camera |
| Bottle loading | PET bottle | Delta | Vacuum + neck grip | Cap presence |

---

## 3.5 Common Pitfall Analysis

> [!WARNING]
> - **Pitfall 1:** Using $g = 10$ m/s² instead of $9.81$ m/s². The KTU key uses $9.81$ unless stated otherwise.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to add **$t_{\text{dwell}}$** (safety interlock wait) in $t_c$. Many students omit it and lose 2 marks.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Using **grip force per jaw** instead of **total** in the Kutz formula. The factor 2 in the denominator is critical.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Computing break-even in **months** but plugging shift hours in **hours** without unit conversion.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Confusing **payload** (mass robot can carry continuously) with **peak load** (momentary). KTU expects **continuous payload**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Loading/Unloading Robotic Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input[Input Subsystem]
        rawBin[Raw Workpiece Bin or Conveyor] --> partSensor[Part Presence Sensor]
        partSensor --> orienter[Part Orienter Optional]
    end

    subgraph robot[Robotic Subsystem]
        controller[Robot Controller PLC] --> arm[6 Axis Manipulator Arm]
        arm --> gripper[End Effector Gripper]
        controller -. I/O bus .-> vision[Vision System]
        controller -. Modbus .-> plcCNC[Machine Tool PLC]
    end

    subgraph machine[Machine Tool Subsystem]
        plcCNC --> chuckDoor[Chuck or Door Actuator]
        chuckDoor --> process[Manufacturing Process]
        process --> finished[Finished Part]
    end

    subgraph output[Output Subsystem]
        finished --> outConveyor[Output Conveyor or Pallet]
        outConveyor --> outSensor[Out Sensor]
        outSensor --> quality[Quality Check Station]
    end

    orienter --> gripper
    gripper --> process
    arm --> finished
    quality --> controller
    controller --> safetyCurtain[Safety Light Curtain ISO 13849]
    controller --> eStop[Dual Channel E Stop]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** are **physical material flow** (raw → machine → finished).
- The **dotted arrows** are **information flow** (sensor feedback, interlocks, control signals).
- The **controller** is the central node that orchestrates synchronisation between the robot and the machine tool.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Pick-Place-MachineTend Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Operator
    participant Controller
    participant Robot as Robot Arm
    participant Gripper
    participant CNC as CNC Machine
    participant Output as Output Bin

    Operator->>Controller: START cycle
    Controller->>CNC: Request door OPEN
    CNC-->>Controller: Door OPEN confirmed
    Controller->>Robot: Move to PICK position
    Robot->>Gripper: ACTUATE (close jaws)
    Gripper-->>Robot: GRIP OK (force sensor)
    Robot->>CNC: Insert part into chuck
    Controller->>CNC: CLAMP part
    CNC-->>Controller: CLAMPED
    Robot->>CNC: Retract to SAFE
    Controller->>CNC: CLOSE door + START machining
    CNC-->>Controller: MACHINING COMPLETE
    Controller->>CNC: OPEN door
    Robot->>CNC: Approach finished part
    Gripper->>CNC: UNCLAMP request
    CNC-->>Gripper: PART FREE
    Gripper->>Robot: GRIP finished part
    Robot->>Output: Place part in bin
    Gripper-->>Robot: RELEASE
    Robot-->>Controller: Cycle COMPLETE
```

**Reading the sequence diagram:** Each horizontal arrow is a **message** between subsystems. The vertical lines are **lifelines**. The KTU examiner often asks students to draw a simplified version of this — a 3-mark question at minimum.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Gripper Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start: Choose Gripper] --> q1{Workpiece material?}
    q1 -- Ferromagnetic --> magGrip[Magnetic Gripper]
    q1 -- Non magnetic --> q2{Surface flat and smooth?}
    q2 -- Yes --> vacGrip[Vacuum Gripper]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Prismatic with two parallel faces?}
    q3 -- Yes --> pjGrip[Parallel Jaw Gripper]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Cylindrical or spherical?}
    q4 -- Cylindrical --> vjGrip[Three Jaw V Gripper]
    q4 -- Spherical --> fgGrip[Three Finger Centric Gripper]
    q1 -- High temperature --> htGrip[High Temp Compliant Gripper]
```

**Reading the decision tree:** It begins with the **material property** of the workpiece (the most decisive factor) and branches down to the optimal gripper type. KTU loves questions of the form *"For a hot aluminium die-casting of irregular shape, justify your gripper selection."*

---

## 4.4 State Machine — Robot Cell Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Picking: Cycle start
    Picking --> Loading: Grip confirmed
    Loading --> Waiting: Part clamped by machine
    Waiting --> Unloading: Machining done
    Unloading --> Placing: Grip on finished part
    Placing --> Idle: Part released
    Idle --> Fault: Sensor trip or E stop
    Fault --> Idle: Operator reset
```

**Reading the state machine:** Each rounded rectangle is a **state** the robot can occupy. Each arrow is a **transition** triggered by an event. The `[*]` symbol denotes the start/end pseudo-state. KTU students should be able to enumerate the states in a written answer.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`
**Q: Define "Loading/Unloading" as a robotic application. Differentiate it from palletizing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Loading/Unloading (or **machine tending**) is the automated transfer of workpieces into and out of a processing machine (CNC, press, moulder) using a robot. **(1 mark)**

| Aspect | Loading/Unloading (Tending) | Palletizing |
|---|---|---|
| Primary function | Feed raw part, retrieve finished part | Stack finished parts on a pallet |
| Precision required | High (positional accuracy ± 0.05 – 0.5 mm at the chuck) | Low (positioning ± 5 – 20 mm is acceptable) |
| Payload range | 1 – 500 kg typical | 5 – 1500 kg typical |
| Workspace | Confined around a single machine | Large, often 2–3 m cube |
| Cycle time emphasis | Sub-second gripper actuation | High payload, moderate speed |

**(2 marks)** — One mark for each correctly differentiated row.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Q: List the three most common types of end-effectors used in loading/unloading robots and give one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Mechanical Gripper (Parallel Jaw)** — Used for prismatic workpieces such as rectangular billets loaded into a CNC milling machine. **(1 mark)**
2. **Vacuum Gripper (Suction Cup)** — Used for sheet-metal blanks loaded into a stamping press. **(1 mark)**
3. **Magnetic Gripper (Electromagnetic or Permanent)** — Used for ferromagnetic steel rods loaded into a heat-treatment furnace. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions are internally chosen between two options. Both options below are designed for full 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question B – Option A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]`

**Q: A CNC turning centre in a small-scale industry is to be automated with a robotic loading/unloading cell. The raw material is cylindrical bar stock of length 250 mm, diameter 60 mm, and mass 2.5 kg. The chuck has a maximum allowable approach misalignment of $\pm 1.0$ mm. The robot transfers the part over a distance of 0.8 m between the input conveyor and the chuck at a cruise velocity of 1.2 m/s with acceleration/deceleration of 2.0 m/s². The gripper actuation time is 0.3 s, door open time is 1.5 s, retract time is 0.8 s, and safety dwell is 0.5 s.**

**(a)** Compute the **minimum grip force** required, assuming a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.35$ between the rubber jaw pads and the oily steel bar, a safety factor of 2.0, and an upward acceleration of 1.5 m/s² of the arm during lift.
**(b)** Compute the **total cycle time** and the **hourly throughput** in parts/hour.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.** $m_p = 2.5$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $a_d = 1.5$ m/s², $\mu = 0.35$, $k_s = 2.0$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Apply the Kutz–Morinelli equation.** Total grip force on both jaws is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_g &= \frac{k_s \cdot m_p \cdot (g + a_d)}{2 \mu} \\
&= \frac{2.0 \times 2.5 \times (9.81 + 1.5)}{2 \times 0.35} \\
&= \frac{2.0 \times 2.5 \times 11.31}{0.70} \\
&= \frac{56.55}{0.70} \\
&= 80.79 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(4 marks)** — 2 for formula, 2 for arithmetic.

**Step 3 — Per-jaw force (intermediate result).**

$$F_n = \frac{F_g}{2} = \frac{80.79}{2} = 40.4 \text{ N per jaw}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Convert to pneumatic pressure (optional advanced).** If the jaw area is 10 cm² = $10 \times 10^{-4}$ m², then pressure required:

$$P = \frac{F_n}{A} = \frac{40.4}{10 \times 10^{-4}} = 40{,}400 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.4 \text{ bar}$$

**(1 mark)**

> **Final Answer (a):** $F_g \approx 80.8$ N (total grip force, both jaws).

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Check whether the cruise velocity is reached.** Required distance to attain $v_{\max}$ is:

$$d_{\text{accel}} = \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a} = \frac{1.2^2}{2.0} = \frac{1.44}{2.0} = 0.72 \text{ m}$$

Since the actual transfer distance $d = 0.8$ m $> 0.72$ m, **the cruise phase is reached**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Compute the transfer time** using the trapezoidal formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{transfer}} &= \frac{2 v_{\max}}{a} + \frac{d - \dfrac{v_{\max}^2}{a}}{v_{\max}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 1.2}{2.0} + \frac{0.8 - 0.72}{1.2} \\
&= 1.2 + \frac{0.08}{1.2} \\
&= 1.2 + 0.0667 \\
&= 1.267 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Sum all cycle components.** Using $t_c = t_a + t_g + t_t + t_m + t_r + t_d$ where $t_a$ (approach) is approximately equal to the transfer time (we assume identical reverse motion):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_c &= 1.267 + 0.3 + 1.267 + 1.5 + 0.8 + 0.5 \\
&= 5.634 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Compute hourly throughput.** Assuming 95 % machine uptime:

$$N_{\text{hour}} = \frac{3600 \times 0.95}{t_c} = \frac{3420}{5.634} = 607 \text{ parts/hour}$$

**(2 marks)**

> **Final Answer (b):** $t_c \approx 5.63$ s, throughput $\approx 607$ parts/hour.

---

### **Question B – Option B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: A small manufacturing firm is considering installing a robotic loading/unloading cell for its CNC milling machine. The proposed cell has the following financial data:**

- **Total installed cost of the cell:** ₹ 32 lakh
- **Operators displaced per shift:** 1 (wage ₹ 28,000/month, benefits factor 1.4)
- **Working shifts:** 2 per day, 26 days/month
- **Annual power cost:** ₹ 1.8 lakh
- **Annual spares cost:** ₹ 0.6 lakh
- **Annual preventive maintenance cost:** 6 % of cell cost
- **Annual cell uptime:** 96 %

**(a)** Compute the **annual net saving** and the **break-even time** in years.
**(b)** If the cell runs at a cycle time of 7.5 s, compute the **annual production output** and comment on whether the cell improves productivity.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Annual labour saving.**

$$L_{\text{annual}} = 1 \times 28{,}000 \times 1.4 \times 12 = ₹ 4{,}70{,}400 \text{ per year}$$

(2 shifts × 26 days × 12 months does not multiply wage further because wage is already monthly. The 2-shift operator count is 1 per shift, and the question states operators displaced = 1, so the wage is computed once for one operator per month.) **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Annual operating cost.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{annual}} &= \text{Power} + \text{Spares} + \text{Maintenance} \\
&= 1.8 + 0.6 + 0.06 \times 32 \\
&= 1.8 + 0.6 + 1.92 \\
&= ₹ 4.32 \text{ lakh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Net annual saving.**

$$S = L_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{annual}} = 4.704 - 4.32 = ₹ 0.384 \text{ lakh/year}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Break-even time.**

$$T_{\text{BE}} = \frac{C_{\text{cell}}}{S} = \frac{32}{0.384} = 83.3 \text{ years}$$

**(2 marks)**

> **Critical comment:** The break-even is **unrealistically long** because only one operator is displaced. To make the cell viable, the firm should either reduce cell cost (use a Cartesian gantry at ₹ 18 lakh) or run 3 shifts.

**Adjusted realistic scenario:** If 2 operators per shift are displaced and 3 shifts run:

$$L_{\text{annual}} = 6 \times 28{,}000 \times 1.4 \times 12 = ₹ 28.22 \text{ lakh/yr}$$

$$S = 28.22 - 4.32 = 23.9 \text{ lakh/yr}$$

$$T_{\text{BE}} = \frac{32}{23.9} = 1.34 \text{ years}$$

> **Final Answer (a):** Break-even = **1.34 years** in the realistic 3-shift case.

---

#### **Model Solution – Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Hourly throughput (96 % uptime).**

$$N_{\text{hour}} = \frac{3600 \times 0.96}{7.5} = 460.8 \text{ parts/hour}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Hours per year (assuming 2 shifts × 8 hours × 300 working days).**

$$H = 2 \times 8 \times 300 = 4800 \text{ hours/year}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Annual production.**

$$N_{\text{annual}} = 460.8 \times 4800 = 2{,}211{,}840 \text{ parts/year} \approx 22.1 \text{ lakh parts/year}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Productivity comment.**
A manual operator can typically load/unload a CNC mill at 60 – 90 parts/hour. The robotic cell achieves 460 parts/hour — a **5× to 7× productivity improvement**, with the additional benefit of **continuous 24×7 operation** (in 3-shift mode), **zero ergonomics injury**, and **consistent quality** of placement (no chip contamination from human hands). **(2 marks)**

> **Final Answer (b):** Annual output ≈ **22.1 lakh parts**, productivity gain ≈ **5×** over manual.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on Loading/Unloading questions:**
> 1. **Omitting the safety factor $k_s$** in the Kutz formula — costs 2 marks. KTU key explicitly lists it.
> 2. **Forgetting the dwell time** in the cycle time sum — costs 1–2 marks. The dwell is a *real* interlock wait, not optional.
> 3. **Wrong unit in break-even** — mixing hours/year with months. Convert to a common unit **before** dividing.
> 4. **Choosing the wrong robot type** — students often pick a 6-axis robot for a simple palletizing task. The KTU examiner deducts for **economic mismatch** (a Cartesian gantry is 3–5× cheaper for axis-symmetric palletizing).
> 5. **No mention of safety standards (ISO 13849 / Category 3)** — a 1-mark item that is often skipped.
> 6. **Confusing cycle time $t_c$ with throughput** — write both and show the conversion formula $N = 3600 \eta / t_c$.
> 7. **Not drawing/listing the system components** — KTU awards 2 marks for a labelled block diagram even in numerical problems. Always include a small **system schematic** in your answer sheet.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must be at your fingertips in the exam hall):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Loading/Unloading = automated feeding + retrieval of workpieces at a machine tool, press, or pallet.
- [ ] **Grip force formula:** $F_g = \dfrac{k_s \, m_p \, (g + a_d)}{2 \mu}$ — remember the factor 2 in the denominator.
- [ ] **Cycle time formula:** $t_c = t_a + t_g + t_t + t_m + t_r + t_d$ — **all six** terms are additive.
- [ ] **Trapezoidal transfer time:** $t_t = \dfrac{2 v_{\max}}{a} + \dfrac{d - v_{\max}^2 / a}{v_{\max}}$.
- [ ] **Triangular edge case:** when $d < v_{\max}^2 / a$, use $t_t = 2 \sqrt{d / a}$.
- [ ] **Throughput:** $N = 3600 \, \eta \, / \, t_c$ parts/hour.
- [ ] **Break-even:** $T_{\text{BE}} = C_{\text{cell}} / (L - C)$ in **consistent units** (years or months).
- [ ] **Gripper types:** mechanical (parallel jaw, 3-jaw V), vacuum, magnetic, soft/compliance — select by **material, geometry, and temperature**.
- [ ] **Robot selection rule of thumb:** 6-axis universal; SCARA for fast small-part; Cartesian gantry for heavy/large; Delta for ultra-high-speed.
- [ ] **Sensors mandatory:** part-presence, door/chuck interlock, force/torque wrist, safety light curtain (ISO 13849 PL d).
- [ ] **Standard compliance:** ISO 10218 (industrial robot safety), ISO 13849 (safety of machinery), OSHA / Indian Factory Act interlocks.
- [ ] **Typical payback:** 12 – 24 months (one of the shortest in robotics).
- [ ] **Common KTU traps:** safety factor missing, dwell time missing, unit mismatch in ROI, wrong robot morphology.

> **Final KTU Mantra for this topic:** *"Loading/Unloading is the gateway automation — its cycle time and gripper design decide whether the cell pays back."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of a Robot in a Manufacturing Cell. Safety Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET411 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Role of a Robot in a Manufacturing Cell. Safety Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Manufacturing Cell — Formal Definition

A **Manufacturing Cell** is a group of workstations, machines, and material handling devices (including industrial robots) arranged in close physical proximity and organized under a unified control system to process a family of parts that share similar manufacturing requirements. When an **industrial robot** is integrated as the central material-handling and process-coordinating agent, the configuration is termed a **Robotic Manufacturing Cell (RMC)** or simply a **Robot Cell**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The role of a robot is not merely "to do one task." A robot in a manufacturing cell acts as the **flexible, reprogrammable, multi-axis coordinator** of part flow, machine tending, inspection, and assembly — replacing the rigid hard-automation of transfer lines of the past.

The **Robotic Industries Association (RIA)** defines a robotic cell as:
> *"A collection of one or more industrial robots, associated tooling, end-effectors, work fixtures, and supporting equipment (conveyors, part feeders, safety barriers) configured to perform a defined set of manufacturing operations."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Robotic Cell as a Choreographed Dance Studio"

Imagine a **dance studio** where:
- The **robot** is the lead dancer, capable of repeating any pre-programmed choreography perfectly.
- The **CNC machine / press / welding station** is a stationary prop that the dancer interacts with.
- The **conveyor** is the side-stage entrance/exit where new performers (parts) enter and leave.
- The **controller cabinet** is the choreographer holding the music score.
- The **safety fencing** is the audience barrier that keeps untrained viewers away from the spinning dancer.

Just as a choreographer (the engineer) plans routines (cycle time, sequence, synchronization) for a team of dancers (machines + robot) to perform a synchronized act (manufacturing the part family) inside a confined stage (the cell) — a robotic cell is a tightly orchestrated, geographically compact, safety-bounded production unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** A robotic cell is fundamentally about **integration, not just robotics**. The robot is one element; the cell includes fixtures, conveyors, sensors, controllers, safety systems, and human-machine interfaces working in concert.

---

### 1.3 Why Use a Robot in a Manufacturing Cell? — The "Three Fs"

Robots are deployed in cells to satisfy the **Three Fs of Modern Manufacturing**:

| Pillar | Meaning | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Flexibility** | Quick reprogramming between part variants | Same robot cell produces 6 different gearbox housings |
| **Fidelity** | Repeatable accuracy over millions of cycles | ±0.02 mm placement over 10 million cycles |
| **Footprint Efficiency** | High output per square meter of floor space | 6-axis robot replaces 4-station transfer line |

Additional drivers include **hazard substitution** (welding fumes, heat, heavy parts), **labour cost reduction**, and **24×7 lights-out operation**.

---

### 1.4 Safety Considerations — Formal Definition

**Safety in Robotics** refers to the systematic identification, evaluation, and mitigation of physical, electrical, and operational hazards associated with industrial robots, their end-effectors, the parts they manipulate, and the surrounding workspace, in compliance with national and international standards such as **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012**, **ISO 10218-1/2:2011**, and **ISO/TS 15066:2016** (for collaborative robots).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:**
> Safety questions in KTU 2024 scheme often test three things:
> 1. Classification of robot hazards.
> 2. Knowledge of international safety standards.
> 3. Selection of appropriate safeguarding devices for a given cell layout.

---

### 1.5 Types of Hazards in a Robotic Cell (Intuitive Grouping)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "MEPHS": Mechanical, Electrical, Pneumatic/Hydraulic, Human, Systemic**

- **Mechanical Hazards** — Trapping/crushing between robot links, unexpected motion, end-effector tooling.
- **Energy Hazards** — Stored electrical energy in capacitors, residual pneumatic pressure, hydraulic fluid leaks.
- **Human Hazards** — Operator entering the cell during auto-mode, complacency, pinch points.
- **Environmental/Systemic** — EMI, software faults, sensor failure, communication loss.

---

### 1.6 Standard Safety Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Maximum Allowable Speed (in collaborative mode):** **250 mm/s** (per ISO/TS 15066 for power-and-force-limited robots).
- **Minimum Safe Stand-off Distance:** Computed via **ISO 13855** formula (covered in §2).
- **Emergency Stop Response Time:** **≤ 250 ms** (per ISO 10218).
- **Robot Stop Categories (per IEC 60204-1):** **Category 0** (uncontrolled by immediate removal of power), **Category 1** (controlled stop with power maintained until stop achieved, then removed), **Category 2** (controlled stop with power retained).
- **Risk Reduction Target:** **PL = d** (Performance Level d) with **Category 3** architecture (per ISO 13849-1) for safety-related parts of control systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Robotic Manufacturing Cell — Layered Breakdown

A robotic cell has **six functional layers**, each of which must be engineered:

| Layer | Function | Typical Components |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Part Supply** | Feed raw blanks into cell | Bowl feeder, magazine, conveyor, AGV |
| **L2 — Fixturing / Work Holding** | Locate and clamp part | Pneumatic vise, hydraulic clamp, pallet |
| **L3 — Robot + Tooling** | Move part, perform operation | 6-axis arm, gripper, weld gun, paint nozzle |
| **L4 — Process Machines** | Material-removal/forming stations | CNC mill, press, injection moulder, oven |
| **L5 — Inspection** | Verify part quality | Vision camera, CMM, force/torque sensor |
| **L6 — Part Egress & Control** | Discharge finished part, supervise | Out-feed conveyor, PLC, HMI, MES link |

The **robot is the cross-layer coordinator**: it physically moves parts between L1, L2, L3, L4, and L5, while communicating with L6 for sequencing and reporting.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Robotic Cells (By Layout Geometry)

| Cell Type | Layout | Typical Application | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **In-line Cell** | Robot on linear rail between machines | Transfer between press stations | High throughput | Inflexible to layout change |
| **Rotational (Indexing) Cell** | Parts rotate on a turntable past fixed stations | Small part assembly | Compact, short cycle | Limited to small parts |
| **Rectilinear (Matrix) Cell** | Robot at centre, machines at cardinal points | Heavy part machining (engine block) | Excellent reach utilisation | Larger footprint |
| **Mobile / AGV-based Cell** | Robot mounted on AGV moves between stations | Aerospace, large structures | Scalable to large parts | Complex coordination |
| **Collaborative Cell** | No fence, human + robot share workspace | Final assembly, finishing | No safety fence needed | Strict speed/force limits |

---

### 2.3 The Role of the Robot — Functional Decomposition

The robot performs **seven canonical roles** in a cell:

1. **Machine Tending** — Loading/unloading CNC, press, injection moulder.
2. **Material Handling** — Inter-station transport of blanks, WIP, finished parts.
3. **Process Execution** — Welding (spot, MIG, TIG), painting, adhesive dispensing, deburring, drilling.
4. **Assembly** — Pick-and-place of sub-components (snap-fit, screw-driving, insertion).
5. **Inspection / Quality Control** — Pick-and-place to vision station, in-process gauging.
6. **Palletising / De-palletising** — Stacking finished goods, unstacking blanks.
7. **Cell Coordination** — Acting as the "active fixture" — reorienting parts between operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Role" Matters in KTU:**
> Examiners often pose a scenario (e.g., "a manufacturer produces 200 aluminium housings/day with 5 variants") and ask the student to **justify the role of the robot**. The answer must link part family, batch size, cycle time, and cell layout — not just list functions.

---

### 2.4 Cycle Time and Throughput — The Engineering Core

A cell's productivity is governed by the **bottleneck station**. The **robot's cycle time** $T_c$ is the sum of all motion segments and dwell times during one part:

$$
T_c = t_{a \rightarrow b} + t_{\text{grasp}} + t_{b \rightarrow c} + t_{\text{release}} + t_{\text{return}} + t_{\text{dwell}}
$$

Where:
- $t_{a \rightarrow b}$ = traverse time from home to pick position.
- $t_{\text{grasp}}$ = gripper closure time (typical 0.3–0.8 s for pneumatic).
- $t_{b \rightarrow c}$ = traverse time from pick to place.
- $t_{\text{release}}$ = gripper opening time.
- $t_{\text{return}}$ = traverse time back to home / next station.
- $t_{\text{dwell}}$ = wait time for machine cycle (e.g., CNC milling finishes).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** $T_c$ is **not** the maximum of the station times; it is the **sum** of the robot's motions *plus* the maximum of the station dwell times (the bottleneck). Students who incorrectly use $T_c = \max(t_i)$ lose 2 marks.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Formula Reference — Memorize These.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = \sum t_{\text{motion}} + t_{\text{dwell,max}}$ | Robot cycle time | s | Throughput calculation |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{bottleneck}}}{T_c} \times 100\%$ | Cell balance efficiency | % | Identifying imbalance |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Parts per hour per robot | parts/hr | Capacity planning |
| 4 | $S_{\text{reach}} = \sum L_i$ | Kinematic reach (sum of links) | mm | Work envelope sizing |
| 5 | $t_{\text{traverse}} = \dfrac{\theta_i \cdot D_i}{\dot{q}_i}$ | Joint motion time | s | Cycle time breakdown |
| 6 | $D_{\text{safe}} = K \cdot (T_{\text{stop}} + T_{\text{response}})$ | Minimum safe distance (ISO 13855) | mm | Safety fence placement |
| 7 | $F_{\text{max,coll}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot K}{M_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Max contact force limit (collaborative) | N | PFL robot qualification |
| 8 | $v_{\text{coll,max}} = 250$ mm/s | Quasi-static contact speed limit | mm/s | Collaborative mode |
| 9 | $\text{MTTF}_d = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_d}$ | Mean time to dangerous failure | hr | ISO 13849 PL computation |
| 10 | $\text{PFH}_d \le 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-7}$ | Probability of dangerous failure per hour | 1/hr | PL d / PL e requirement |

> **Notation note for Table:** All absolute-value bars and divisibility uses have been replaced with `\cdot` and explicit fractions so markdown table rendering is preserved.

---

### 2.6 Safety Standards — The Regulatory Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Section is KTU-High-Yield:**
> Every year, at least one Module-4 question asks the student to "list and briefly explain the safety standards relevant to industrial robots." Memorize the table below.

| Standard | Full Title | Scope | Region |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** | Industrial Robots and Robot Systems — Safety Requirements | General robot cell safety | USA (adopted globally) |
| **ISO 10218-1:2011** | Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots — Part 1: Robots | Robot manufacturer | International |
| **ISO 10218-2:2011** | Part 2: Robot systems and integration | System integrator | International |
| **ISO/TS 15066:2016** | Collaborative robots — Technical specification | Cobot-specific limits | International |
| **ISO 13849-1:2015** | Safety of machinery — Safety-related parts of control systems | Performance Level (PL) | International |
| **ISO 13855:2010** | Positioning of safeguards w.r.t. approach speed | Safe distance calculation | International |
| **IEC 60204-1** | Electrical equipment of machines | E-stop categories | International |
| **EN ISO 12100** | General principles for design — Risk assessment | Risk methodology | EU / International |

---

### 2.7 Safety System Layers — The "Onion Model"

A robotic cell is protected by **defence-in-depth** (concentric safety layers):

- **Layer 1 — Inherent Safe Design:** Limit robot's reach, use soft padding on links, low-inertia actuators.
- **Layer 2 — Safeguarding Devices (Passive):** Fixed perimeter fences, interlocked gates.
- **Layer 3 — Safeguarding Devices (Active / Electrosensitive):** Light curtains, laser scanners, safety mats, area scanners.
- **Layer 4 — Control System Safeguards:** Safety-rated PLC, dual-channel E-stop wiring, watchdogs.
- **Layer 5 — Administrative / Procedural:** Lockout-Tagout (LOTO), training, signage, SOPs.
- **Layer 6 — PPE:** Safety glasses, steel-toe shoes (last line of defence).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often skip Layer 1 (inherent design) and jump straight to "fence + E-stop." Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing the *hierarchy of controls* principle (elimination → substitution → engineering → administrative → PPE).

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding cells** use 6-axis robots with safety-rated zone control — 8 robots in one cell, fenced but with mutual interlock via safety PLC.
- **Collaborative cells** in electronics assembly (e.g., PCB screw-driving) use **PFL (Power and Force Limiting)** cobots at 250 mm/s with joint torque sensors.
- **Aerospace drilling cells** use **AGV-mounted robots** entering the cell dynamically — safety logic is distributed across the AGV fleet manager and the cell safety controller.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Safe Stand-off Distance (ISO 13855)

The **safeguarding distance** $S$ is the minimum physical separation between a protective device (e.g., light curtain) and the hazard zone (the robot's reach envelope) such that a human approaching the hazard will be detected *before* reaching the pinch point.

> **Assumption (ISO 13855 vertical light curtain):** Approach is perpendicular to the detection plane.

**Step 1 — Define the detection event timing chain:**

When a person walks toward the cell at approach speed $K$ (typically 1.6 m/s for walking, 2.0 m/s for hand approach):

$$
S = K \cdot (T_1 + T_2) + C
$$

Where:
- $K$ = approach speed (m/s).
- $T_1$ = total response time of the safety device (detection + signal transmission, in s).
- $T_2$ = response time of the machine / robot (time from stop signal to cessation of hazardous motion, in s).
- $C$ = intrusion distance (how far past the detection plane a person/limb can travel before the machine actually stops). For vertical light curtains with reach-over, $C = 8 \cdot (d - 14)$ where $d$ is the resolution in mm and $d \ge 14$ mm.

**Step 2 — Substitute the worst-case response times:**

A typical robot cell has:
- $T_1 = 30$ ms (light curtain response).
- $T_2 = 220$ ms (robot Category 1 stop + brake engagement).

**Step 3 — Compute numerically:**

$$
K \cdot (T_1 + T_2) = 1.6 \text{ m/s} \cdot (0.030 + 0.220) \text{ s} = 1.6 \cdot 0.250 = 0.400 \text{ m} = 400 \text{ mm}
$$

For a light curtain of resolution $d = 30$ mm:

$$
C = 8 \cdot (30 - 14) = 8 \cdot 16 = 128 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore:

$$
S = 400 + 128 = 528 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rounded Value (per ISO rounding rules):** $S = 530$ mm. KTU expects students to round **up** to the nearest 10 mm.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Throughput of a Multi-Station Cell

Consider a 3-station robotic cell with a single robot. Each station has process time $T_1, T_2, T_3$, and the robot's transit + grasp + release time between any two adjacent stations is constant $T_r$.

**Step 1 — The robot's cycle time per part:**

$$
T_c = T_r \cdot 2 + T_{\text{grasp}} + T_{\text{release}} + \max(T_1, T_2, T_3)
$$

The robot **must wait** at the bottleneck station, so we take the maximum of the station times.

**Step 2 — Cell throughput (parts/hour):**

$$
P = \frac{3600 \text{ s/hr}}{T_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example:**

Given: $T_1 = 8$ s (CNC mill), $T_2 = 5$ s (deburr), $T_3 = 12$ s (wash + dry), $T_r = 2$ s, grasp = 0.5 s, release = 0.5 s.

$$
T_c = 2 \cdot 2 + 0.5 + 0.5 + \max(8, 5, 12) = 4 + 1 + 12 = 17 \text{ s}
$$

$$
P = \frac{3600}{17} \approx 211.76 \text{ parts/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Cell balance efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{\text{bottleneck}}}{T_c} \cdot 100\% = \frac{12}{17} \cdot 100\% \approx 70.6\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** When $\eta < 80\%$, the cell is "robot-starved" — the robot is idle more than 20% of the cycle. Solution: add a 4th station or parallelize the bottleneck.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Power-and-Force-Limited (PFL) Robot — Contact Force Limit

For a **collaborative robot** in quasi-static contact (slow speed contact, < 250 mm/s), the maximum allowable force on a human body region is governed by **ISO/TS 15066 Annex A**. The biomechanical limit is:

$$
F_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot K_{\text{spring}}}{m_{\text{eff}}}}
$$

**Step 1 — Define effective mass and stiffness:**

For a robot arm striking a stationary hand:
- $m_{\text{eff}}$ = effective mass of robot link + payload (kg).
- $K_{\text{spring}}$ = contact stiffness of human body region (N/m). For the hand: $K \approx 75{,}000$ N/m.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

Suppose $m_{\text{eff}} = 5$ kg:

$$
F_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 75{,}000}{5}} = \sqrt{30{,}000} \approx 173.2 \text{ N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO/TS 15066 limit for hand/finger contact = 140 N (quasi-static).** Our computed 173.2 N exceeds the limit — meaning the design must either reduce $m_{\text{eff}}$ (lighter link) or slow the contact speed below the 250 mm/s threshold.

**Step 3 — Find allowable $m_{\text{eff}}$ for 140 N limit:**

$$
140 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 75{,}000}{m_{\text{eff}}}} \implies m_{\text{eff}} = \frac{150{,}000}{140^2} = \frac{150{,}000}{19{,}600} \approx 7.65 \text{ kg}
$$

So with 5 kg effective mass, we are **safe** for dynamic-clamping interpretation but need to verify in the **quasi-static** column. Always consult Annex A's body-region table.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cell Selection Decision Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-yield KTU examination tool.** Use it to answer "select a cell type" / "justify a cell layout" questions.

| Selection Criterion | In-line | Rotational | Rectilinear | Collaborative | AGV-based |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Part weight (kg) | 1–50 | 0.1–5 | 5–500+ | 0.1–15 | 50–2000 |
| Batch size | High | Med | Med | Low–Med | Low |
| Part family variety | Low | Med | Med | High | High |
| Cycle time target (s) | < 10 | 5–30 | 30–180 | 30–120 | 60–600 |
| Floor space | Medium | Small | Large | Small | Variable |
| Safety fence required? | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | Partial |
| Capital cost (INR, indicative) | ₹30–80 L | ₹10–30 L | ₹50–200 L | ₹15–50 L | ₹80 L–5 Cr |
| Best-fit industry | Automotive body | Electronics | Heavy machinery | FMCG, light assembly | Aerospace, shipbuilding |

---

### 3.5 Risk Assessment Methodology (per ISO 12100)

**Step 1 — Determine the limits of the machine** (use, misuse, environment).
**Step 2 — Identify hazards** (mechanical, electrical, thermal, noise, vibration, radiation, ergonomic).
**Step 3 — Estimate the risk** for each hazard using a **Severity × Probability × Possibility of Avoidance** matrix.

$$
\text{Risk Level} = S \times P \times A
$$

| Severity ($S$) | Probability ($P$) | Avoidance ($A$) | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Negligible) | 1 (Improbable) | 1 (Avoidable) | 1–3 → **Low** |
| 2 (Marginal — bruise) | 2 (Seldom) | 2 (Avoidable with care) | 4–10 → **Medium** |
| 3 (Serious — fracture) | 3 (Frequent) | 3 (Unavoidable) | 11–18 → **High** |
| 4 (Catastrophic — death) | 4 (Continuous) | 4 (Impossible) | 19–27 → **Very High** |

**Step 4 — Apply the hierarchy of controls:**

1. **Inherent safe design** (eliminate the hazard).
2. **Safeguarding / complementary protective measures** (light curtain, fence).
3. **Information for use** (signage, training, manual).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** A common mistake is to apply Layer 3 (signage) before Layer 1 (inherent design). The hierarchy is **non-negotiable** — credit is lost if reversed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Robotic Manufacturing Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Infeed Conveyor L1] --> B[Pickup Station L2 Fixture]
    B --> C[Robot Arm with Gripper L3]
    C --> D[Process Machine A L4]
    C --> E[Process Machine B L4]
    C --> F[Vision Inspection L5]
    D --> C
    E --> C
    F --> G[Outfeed Conveyor L6]
    C --> H[Safety PLC E Stop Light Curtain]
    H --> I[Cell Controller HMI PLC]
    I --> C
    I --> G
```

---

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Robot's Role Inside a Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Part Arrives on Infeed] --> P2[Robot Picks Part]
    P2 --> P3[Move to Station A]
    P3 --> P4[Operation A Performs]
    P4 --> P5[Robot Retrieves Part]
    P5 --> P6[Move to Station B]
    P6 --> P7[Operation B Performs]
    P7 --> P8[Robot Retrieves Part]
    P8 --> P9[Move to Inspection]
    P9 --> P10[Vision Check Accept Reject]
    P10 --> P11[Place on Outfeed or Reject Bin]
```

---

### 4.3 Safety Layer Architecture (Onion Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Layer 6 PPE Operator Goggles Gloves] --> B[Layer 5 Administrative LOTO Training Signage]
    B --> C[Layer 4 Control Safeguards Safety PLC E Stop Watchdog]
    C --> D[Layer 3 Active Safeguards Light Curtain Laser Scanner Safety Mat]
    D --> E[Layer 2 Passive Safeguards Fences Interlocked Gates]
    E --> F[Layer 1 Inherent Safe Design Soft Edges Low Reach Limited Force]
```

---

### 4.4 Cell Layout — Rectangular Robot Cell (Top View, ASCII Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Infeed Conveyor]
        A1[Blanks]
    end
    subgraph B[Robot Cell Enclosure with Fence]
        B1[Robot on Pedestal Centre]
        B2[Fixture A North West]
        B3[Fixture B North East]
        B4[Inspection South]
    end
    subgraph C[Outfeed Conveyor]
        C1[Finished Parts]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
```

---

### 4.5 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Safeguarding Device

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Select Safeguarding Device] --> Q1{Is Human Access Required During Operation?}
    Q1 -- No --> F1[Use Fixed Perimeter Fence with Interlocked Gate]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is Approach Frequent and Slow?}
    Q2 -- Yes and Operator Must Reach In --> S1[Use Safety Mat or Light Curtain]
    Q2 -- No Infrequent and Fast --> Q2b[Use Area Laser Scanner with Mute Function]
    Q1 -- Yes and Human Robot Share Workspace --> Q3{Is Contact Acceptable?}
    Q3 -- No --> S2[Speed and Separation Monitoring with Camera Based Scanner]
    Q3 -- Yes --> PFL[Power and Force Limiting Cobot with Joint Torque Sensors]
```

---

### 4.6 State Machine — Robot Cell Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Off
    Off --> Manual: Teach Pendant Key Switch
    Manual --> Auto: Cycle Start
    Auto --> Paused: E Stop Pressed
    Paused --> Auto: Reset
    Paused --> Manual: Switch to Teach
    Auto --> Hold: Hold Button
    Hold --> Auto: Resume
    Auto --> Off: Power Down
    Paused --> Off: Power Down
```

---

### 4.7 Risk Assessment Flow (ISO 12100)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Identify Machine Limits] --> B[Hazard Identification]
    B --> C[Risk Estimation S x P x A]
    C --> D{Risk Acceptable?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Document and Operate]
    D -- No --> F[Apply Inherent Safe Design]
    F --> G{Risk Reduced?}
    G -- No --> H[Add Safeguarding Devices]
    H --> I{Risk Reduced?}
    I -- No --> J[Add Information and Training]
    J --> K{Reassess}
    K --> C
    I -- Yes --> E
    G -- Yes --> E
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Define a robotic manufacturing cell. List any four primary functions performed by an industrial robot within such a cell.**

**Model Answer:**

A **robotic manufacturing cell** is a spatially grouped, integrated arrangement of one or more industrial robots, work-holding fixtures, process machines, material-handling devices, and safety equipment, controlled as a single production unit to manufacture a family of parts.

**Four primary functions of the robot in a cell:**

1. **Machine tending** — loading raw blanks into a CNC / press and unloading finished parts.
2. **Material handling / inter-station transport** — moving WIP between work fixtures.
3. **Process execution** — direct operations such as spot welding, MIG welding, painting, adhesive dispensing, deburring, drilling.
4. **Quality inspection support** — placing parts in front of a vision station or executing in-process gauging with on-board sensors.

> *Examiner note: Award 1 mark for the definition, 0.5 each for the four functions (max 2 marks for functions; any 4 valid points accepted).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**Mention any three international safety standards applicable to industrial robotic cells. State the specific scope of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Standard | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **ISO 10218-1:2011** | Safety requirements for the **robot itself** (manufacturer's responsibility) — electrical, mechanical, control system. |
| 2 | **ISO 10218-2:2011** | Safety requirements for the **robot system / cell** (integrator's responsibility) — layout, safeguarding, end-effector, installation. |
| 3 | **ISO/TS 15066:2016** | **Collaborative robot** specific technical specification — defines four collaboration methods, biomechanical limits for contact, maximum 250 mm/s quasi-static speed. |
| 4 | **ISO 13855:2010** | **Positioning of safeguards** with respect to human approach speed — provides the $S = K(T_1 + T_2) + C$ formula. |
| 5 | **ANSI/RIA R15.06-2012** | Harmonized US standard combining ISO 10218-1 and -2; widely adopted globally. |

*(Any three, with valid scope, earn full 3 marks.)*

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice (either OR). Both alternatives are equally weighted. Each question typically splits into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Cell Design and Layout*

**(a)** A robotic manufacturing cell consists of a CNC milling machine (cycle time = 35 s), a deburring station (cycle time = 12 s), a washing station (cycle time = 18 s), and a vision inspection station (cycle time = 8 s). The robot's average inter-station transit time is 3.5 s, and the gripper grasp/release takes 1.0 s combined. **Determine the robot's cycle time, the cell throughput in parts per hour, and the cell balance efficiency. Identify the bottleneck station and suggest two engineering remedies.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, **describe the six functional layers of a robotic manufacturing cell**. For each layer, give one example component. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify the bottleneck (slowest station):**
$$
\max(35, 12, 18, 8) = 35 \text{ s (CNC milling)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute robot's cycle time:**

The robot must visit **all four stations sequentially** in a typical cell loop. Assuming one part per cycle (one robot, one fixture family):

$$
T_c = \underbrace{n \cdot t_{\text{transit}}}_{\text{robot motion}} + t_{\text{grasp+release}} + \underbrace{\max(t_i)}_{\text{bottleneck wait}}
$$

$$
T_c = 4 \cdot 3.5 + 1.0 + 35 = 14 + 1 + 35 = 50 \text{ s}
$$

*(If the cell uses a single linear flow: 3 transits between 4 stations ⇒ 3 × 3.5 = 10.5 s. Award either interpretation if the student shows the assumption clearly. KTU examiners prefer $n-1$ transits for a chain: 3 transits.)*

> Re-computing with $n-1 = 3$ transits:
> $$ T_c = 3 \cdot 3.5 + 1.0 + 35 = 10.5 + 1 + 35 = 46.5 \text{ s} $$

**Step 3 — Throughput:**

$$
P = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{46.5} \approx 77.4 \text{ parts/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Cell balance efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{\text{bottleneck}}}{T_c} \cdot 100\% = \frac{35}{46.5} \cdot 100\% \approx 75.3\%
$$

**Step 5 — Bottleneck and remedies:**

The CNC mill at 35 s dominates. **Two engineering remedies:**

1. **Add a parallel CNC machine** with a switch conveyor — robot can route the part to whichever CNC is free. This effectively halves the bottleneck wait to 17.5 s.
2. **Two-robot cell** — a second robot pre-loads the next blank while the first is mid-cycle on deburring/washing, overlapping the bottleneck dwell.
3. **Reduce CNC cycle time** by switching to a higher-feed insert or roughing strategy.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**The six functional layers of a robotic manufacturing cell:**

| Layer | Function | Example Component |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Part Supply** | Introduce raw blanks | Vibratory bowl feeder, magazine rack, infeed conveyor |
| **L2 — Fixturing** | Locate and clamp the part | Pneumatic vise, hydraulic clamp, dedicated pallet |
| **L3 — Robot + Tooling** | Move and manipulate the part | 6-axis articulated robot, gripper, weld torch |
| **L4 — Process Machines** | Material removal / forming | CNC mill, hydraulic press, injection moulder |
| **L5 — Inspection** | Verify part quality | Vision camera, CMM, force/torque sensor |
| **L6 — Egress + Control** | Discharge parts and supervise | Out-feed conveyor, PLC, HMI, MES connection |

**Block diagram (textual representation):**

> L1 (Infeed Conveyor) → L2 (Fixture) → L3 (Robot) ↔ L4 (CNC / Press) → L5 (Vision) → L6 (Outfeed + PLC)

The robot is the central coordinator, moving parts from L1 → L2 → L4 (via L3) → L5 → L6. The L6 controller synchronises the L3 robot with L4 machines and the L5 inspection result.

**Marking guide (7 marks):** 1 mark for naming the layers, 3 marks for describing functions with examples, 2 marks for the diagram, 1 mark for explaining the robot's coordinating role.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Safety Engineering*

**(a)** Define the **minimum safe stand-off distance $S$** as per **ISO 13855**. A vertical light curtain with a resolution of 30 mm is installed at the entrance of a robotic cell. The light curtain's response time is 25 ms, and the robot's stop time (Category 1 stop) is 280 ms. **Calculate the minimum safe distance** assuming a walking approach. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat labelled diagram, **describe the "Onion Model" of safety layers** in a robotic cell. For each layer, state one example safeguard. Justify why inherent safe design (Layer 1) is preferred over PPE (Layer 6). *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The minimum safe stand-off distance $S$ (per ISO 13855) is the **shortest permissible separation** between the **detection plane of a protective device** (e.g., light curtain) and the **nearest hazard point** of the machine, such that a person approaching at the prescribed speed $K$ will be reliably detected and the machine brought to a safe state **before** the person reaches the hazard.

**Formula:**

$$
S = K \cdot (T_1 + T_2) + C
$$

**Given:**
- $K = 1.6$ m/s (walking approach per ISO 13855).
- $T_1 = 25$ ms $= 0.025$ s.
- $T_2 = 280$ ms $= 0.280$ s.
- $d = 30$ mm resolution light curtain.
- $C = 8 \cdot (d - 14) = 8 \cdot (30 - 14) = 8 \cdot 16 = 128$ mm $= 0.128$ m.

**Calculation:**

$$
S = 1.6 \cdot (0.025 + 0.280) + 0.128 = 1.6 \cdot 0.305 + 0.128
$$

$$
S = 0.488 + 0.128 = 0.616 \text{ m} = 616 \text{ mm}
$$

**Rounded up to nearest 10 mm (ISO rule):** $S = 620$ mm.

**Mark split:** 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for correct formula, 2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for correct final value with units.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**The Onion Model (described from inner-most to outer-most defence):**

> **Layer 1 — Inherent Safe Design (innermost, most reliable):**
> Examples: rounded robot links, padding on edges, low-inertia actuators, software-based joint-range limits, low maximum torque/power.

> **Layer 2 — Passive Safeguarding:**
> Examples: fixed perimeter fence at 1.8 m height, interlocked access gate, fixed guards over pinch points.

> **Layer 3 — Active / Electro-sensitive Safeguarding:**
> Examples: vertical light curtain at calculated safe distance, horizontal laser area scanner, pressure-sensitive safety mat, safety-rated camera with zone monitoring.

> **Layer 4 — Control System Safeguards:**
> Examples: safety PLC (e.g., Siemens F-CPU), dual-channel emergency stop (per IEC 60204-1), safety-rated I/O, watchdog timers, redundant encoders.

> **Layer 5 — Administrative / Procedural:**
> Examples: Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) procedure, operator training, warning signs, maintenance permits, SOPs.

> **Layer 6 — PPE (outermost, last resort):**
> Examples: safety glasses, steel-toe shoes, cut-resistant gloves, hearing protection.

**Labelled diagram (textual representation — concentric layers):**

> [Layer 6 PPE — outermost ring]
> [Layer 5 Administrative — LOTO, training, signage]
> [Layer 4 Control Safeguards — Safety PLC, E-stop]
> [Layer 3 Active Safeguards — Light curtain, scanner]
> [Layer 2 Passive Safeguards — Fences, gates]
> [Layer 1 Inherent Safe Design — innermost, closest to robot]

**Justification — Why Layer 1 > Layer 6 (3 marks):**

1. **Inherent safe design eliminates the hazard at source** — no human is exposed to the unsafe condition, so PPE becomes unnecessary. PPE only *mitigates* the consequence of an accident; it does not prevent it.
2. **Reliability hierarchy** — PPE depends on correct human use 100% of the time, with zero failure. Inherent design is engineered into the machine and cannot be "forgotten" by the operator.
3. **Compliance with ISO 12100 hierarchy of controls** — the standard *mandates* applying Layer 1 first. Relying on PPE as the primary defence is a regulatory violation in most jurisdictions.
4. **Cost economics** — PPE is consumed (replaced) indefinitely; inherent design is a one-time engineering cost.

**Mark split:** 2 marks for layer description with examples, 2 marks for the diagram, 3 marks for the justification of Layer 1 > Layer 6.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mix-ups in $S$ formula:** Students frequently compute $K(T_1+T_2)$ in mm and $C$ in m, producing a 1000× error. Convert **all** quantities to **metres** first, or **all** to **mm** — but be consistent.
> 2. **Cycle time vs. bottleneck confusion:** $T_c$ is the *robot's* per-part time, **not** simply the maximum station time. Always add the robot motion and gripper times.
> 3. **Missing ISO numbers in standards question:** A vague "ISO safety standard" earns 0.5 marks. Always write the full standard number (e.g., **ISO 10218-1:2011**).
> 4. **Hierarchy of controls inversion:** Listing PPE before inherent design in risk reduction earns 0 marks for that sub-part. The order is non-negotiable.
> 5. **Risk Assessment matrix omission:** If asked for "risk estimation," students often give only severity and probability, forgetting the third factor (avoidance). The standard is $S \times P \times A$ — all three are required.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section. This is your last-minute revision sheet.**

#### **A. Robotic Manufacturing Cell — Key Facts**

- ✅ A **robotic cell** is one or more robots + work-holding + machines + safety under unified control.
- ✅ Robots serve **seven canonical roles**: machine tending, material handling, process execution, assembly, inspection, palletising, and cell coordination.
- ✅ **Cycle time** $T_c$ = (sum of robot motion times) + gripper time + bottleneck station time.
- ✅ **Throughput** $P = 3600 / T_c$ parts/hour.
- ✅ **Cell balance efficiency** $\eta = (T_{\text{bottleneck}} / T_c) \times 100\%$; aim $\eta > 80\%$.
- ✅ **Cell layouts:** in-line, rotational, rectilinear, collaborative, AGV-based.

#### **B. Safety Standards — Mnemonic "R2-2T"**

- ✅ **R**IA R15.06 (USA, harmonized with ISO).
- ✅ **2** × ISO 10218 (Part 1 = robot, Part 2 = system).
- ✅ **2** × supporting (ISO 13849 = control reliability, ISO 13855 = safe distance).
- ✅ **T**S 15066 (cobot-specific).

#### **C. Safety Engineering — Must-Knows**

- ✅ **Safe distance:** $S = K(T_1 + T_2) + C$, with $K = 1.6$ m/s walking, $K = 2.0$ m/s hand approach.
- ✅ **C (intrusion):** $C = 8(d - 14)$ mm for $d \ge 14$ mm.
- ✅ **E-stop categories (IEC 60204-1):** Cat 0 (immediate uncontrolled), Cat 1 (controlled with delayed power removal), Cat 2 (controlled, power retained).
- ✅ **E-stop max response time:** ≤ **250 ms** for industrial robots.
- ✅ **Cobot quasi-static contact limit:** ≤ **250 mm/s**; static force limits per ISO/TS 15066 Annex A (e.g., hand = 140 N, chest = 140 N quasi-static).
- ✅ **Performance Level target:** PL d with Category 3 architecture (or PL e / Cat 3 for higher-risk zones).

#### **D. Risk Assessment — Must-Knows**

- ✅ Risk $= S \times P \times A$ (Severity × Probability × Avoidance).
- ✅ **Hierarchy of controls** (ISO 12100, in strict order): **Inherent safe design → Safeguarding → Information for use**.
- ✅ **Onion model:** 6 layers from inner (inherent design) to outer (PPE).

#### **E. Common Numerical Traps**

- ⚠️ Forget to convert ms → s in $S$ formula (or vice versa).
- ⚠️ Forget to add gripper grasp + release time to cycle time.
- ⚠️ Use $n$ transits instead of $n-1$ for a chain cell (4 stations ⇒ 3 transits).
- ⚠️ Round $S$ **down** instead of **up** — ISO requires rounding up to next 10 mm.
- ⚠️ Confuse **Performance Level (PL)** with **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** — they come from different standards (ISO 13849 vs. IEC 61508).

#### **F. Two-Liner Recall Questions (Quick Quiz Yourself)**

1. *What is the maximum quasi-static contact speed for a collaborative robot?* → **250 mm/s**.
2. *Name the formula for safe stand-off distance and its three terms.* → **$S = K(T_1 + T_2) + C$**: approach speed × (device + machine response times) + intrusion distance.
3. *Which ISO covers robot-system integration safety?* → **ISO 10218-2:2011**.
4. *Name the four collaborative robot operation methods per ISO/TS 15066.* → **Safety-rated monitored stop, hand-guiding, speed and separation monitoring, power and force limiting.**
5. *What is the bottleneck of a cell with stations 10 s, 15 s, 8 s, 22 s?* → **22 s (the 4th station).**

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Role of a Robot in a Manufacturing Cell & Safety Considerations.**
